The high co-occurrence of self-harm thoughts (78%) in a specialist outpatient sample underscores that ED clinicians should routinely assess for self-harm rather than treating it as a secondary concern. A notable blind spot is the exclusion of participants under 16, which may underestimate self-harm prevalence in younger ED populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians in eating disorder outpatient services who routinely assess and manage self-harm risk

title: A Proof-of-Concept Trial of a Culturally Adapted Virtual Reality-Cognitive Behavioural Therapy with Inuit in Montreal
authors: Quinta Seon, Roxanne Drainville, Liliana Gomez Cardona, Joy Outerbridge, Stéphane Bouchard, Outi Linnaranta, Martin Lepage
summary: Describes the cultural adaptation of virtual reality-enhanced CBT for Inuit communities, addressing cultural and logistical barriers to mental health care in remote populations.
clinical_insight: Demonstrates that VR-enhanced CBT can be successfully adapted for Inuit communities when cultural values, language, and local imagery are integrated into the virtual environments. Clinicians serving remote or Indigenous populations can leverage culturally adapted VR-CBT to overcome geographic barriers while respecting cultural context, potentially improving engagement and reducing dropout in communities with limited access to mental health services.
relevant_for: Telepsychology clinicians and cultural adaptation specialists working with remote Indigenous populations

title: Unveiling the Dynamics of Narcissistic Fluctuations and the Affective Cycles in Narcissism
authors: Radosław Rogoza, Georg Krammer, Lidia Baran, Emanuel Jauk, Maria Flakus, Rossella Di Pierro, MARCO DI SARNO, Emma K. Stapp, Marcin Zajenkowski, Michael Dufner, Ramzi Fatfouta
summary: Using intensive longitudinal data (N = 199; 7,991 responses) and dynamic structural equation modeling, this study maps how three facets of narcissism — agentic, neurotic, and antagonistic — fluctuate in daily life and how these fluctuations relate to momentary affect. A key finding is that increases in antagonistic narcissism predicted subsequent increases in both agentic and neurotic narcissism, revealing cascading dynamics that may help explain the affective instability characteristic of narcissistic personality presentations.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with clients who present with narcissistic traits or narcissistic personality disorder, these findings challenge the common clinical tendency to view narcissism as a static, monolithic construct. The demonstration that antagonistic narcissism may act as a catalyst triggering both grandiose and vulnerable states offers a concrete framework for psychoeducation and for helping clients recognize their own state-dependent patterns. This is particularly relevant for schema therapy and psychodynamic approaches that already conceptualize narcissistic presentations as involving oscillating self-states. A significant blind spot is the use of a non-clinical student-adjacent sample, which may not capture the more severe and treatment-resistant presentations seen in clinical practice.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with narcissistic personality pathology, particularly those using schema therapy, psychodynamic, or transference-focused approaches

title: Client Perceptions and Perceived Utility of Standardised Routine Monitoring of Suicide Risk in Psychological Therapy
authors: David John Hallford, Ryan A. Kaplan, Manon Elise Burgat, Glenn A. Melvin
summary: Investigates client perceptions and perceived utility of standardized routine monitoring of suicide risk during psychological therapy through qualitative interviews, examining how clients experience repeated suicide risk assessment, its impact on the therapeutic relationship, and preferences for implementation in clinical practice.
clinical_insight: Reveals that how clients experience ongoing suicide risk monitoring can significantly affect the therapeutic relationship, with some perceiving it as caring and others as intrusive or stigmatizing. Clinicians should transparently discuss the purpose of risk monitoring with clients, invite collaboration in safety planning, and regularly check in on how monitoring practices are experienced to preserve alliance while maintaining safety.
relevant_for: All therapists and counselors who conduct ongoing suicide risk assessment in any clinical setting

title: Turning Over a New Leaf: A Systematic Review of Nature-Based Social Prescriptions for Enhancing Psychological Wellbeing in Military and Emergency Personnel with Trauma Histories.
authors: Jeffery Leech, Lachlan Molan, Genevieve Dingle
summary: Examines the therapeutic potential of nature-based prescriptions and green care interventions for trauma recovery, reviewing evidence for mechanisms and clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: Nature-based prescriptions offer an accessible, low-stigma adjunct to trauma-focused therapy by leveraging outdoor environments to support affect regulation and grounding. Therapists can collaboratively develop nature-exposure plans with clients—such as structured walks or horticultural activities—to complement traditional trauma processing work and enhance between-session regulation skills.
relevant_for: Trauma-focused therapists and those working in integrated or holistic treatment settings

title: Testing predictors of change in suicide ideation among transgender and gender-diverse adults
authors: Natalia Drobotenko, Caitlin Barry, Caroline Pukall, Jeremy G. Stewart
summary: Identifies predictors of suicidal ideation in transgender adults, examining the role of minority stress, social support, and gender-affirming factors.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with transgender adults should routinely assess minority stressors, access to gender-affirming care, and social support as part of suicide risk formulation. Affirming therapeutic stances combined with active facilitation of gender-affirming resources and community connection serve as protective factors that should be central to treatment planning.
relevant_for: LGBTQ+-affirming therapists and suicide risk assessors in community and crisis settings

title: Anxiety is associated with the persistence of conditioned somatic sensations over time
authors: Hugo Alexander Fleming, Isabel Hei Wing Lau, Rukmini Chariar, Gabriel Mackie, Anna Bevan, Tim Dalgleish, Albert Powers, Camilla Nord
summary: Investigates how anxiety becomes associated with conditioned somatic sensations, with implications for understanding panic disorder and health anxiety.
clinical_insight: Recognizing that somatic symptoms can become conditioned triggers for anxiety helps therapists reframe interoceptive cues as learned associations rather than evidence of medical illness. Clinicians can integrate interoceptive exposure and conditioning-based psychoeducation into treatment for panic disorder and health anxiety, directly targeting the learned fear responses that maintain these conditions.
relevant_for: Therapists specializing in panic disorder, health anxiety, and somatic symptom presentations

title: The Importance (and Limits) of Community Belonging as a  Protective Factor for Wellbeing in LGBTIQ+ Individuals
authors: Elena Manchorova
summary: Investigates the relationship between community belonging and mental health outcomes among LGBTIQ+ individuals, examining protective factors within minority stress frameworks.
clinical_insight: Community belonging functions as a powerful buffer against minority stress, suggesting that therapists should actively assess and facilitate clients' connections to affirming social networks as part of treatment. Clinicians can integrate resource mapping and community referral into therapy, helping LGBTIQ+ clients build supportive relationships that complement individual-level coping strategies for depression and anxiety.
relevant_for: LGBTQ+-affirming therapists and mental health clinicians working with sexual and gender minority clients

title: Configuration, not Content: A Network Perspective on Gendered Prototypes of Intimate Partner Violence
authors: Hanna Feige, Timothy J. Luke, Karl Ask
summary: Applies network analysis to understand the dynamics of intimate partner violence, identifying central maintaining factors and potential intervention targets.
clinical_insight: Network analysis reveals which factors—such as jealousy, substance use, or emotional dysregulation—most centrally maintain intimate partner violence cycles, allowing therapists to prioritize intervention targets rather than addressing all problems equally. Clinicians can use these network-informed formulations to design more efficient treatment plans that disrupt the most influential pathways in the violence-maintaining system.
relevant_for: Domestic violence intervention specialists and couples therapists working with relationship aggression

title: Topic shifts elicit costs in naturalistic discourse: Evidence from healthy adults and individuals with traumatic brain injury
authors: Kelly Marshall, Avery Vess, Jared Novick, L. Robert Slevc
summary: Analyzes how traumatic brain injury affects discourse production, specifically topic shifts in conversation, with implications for social communication rehabilitation.
clinical_insight: Difficulty with topic maintenance after TBI can undermine social relationships and therapy engagement, yet this communication deficit is often overlooked in standard cognitive rehabilitation. Clinicians should assess discourse coherence during intake and incorporate conversational scaffolding strategies—such as explicit topic-tracking cues and structured dialogue exercises—into rehabilitation and psychotherapy sessions.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and rehabilitation therapists working with TBI patients

title: Virtual reality interventions for the treatment of psychiatric disorders: A qualitative review of clinician and patient user’s experience
authors: Maria Elena Hernandez Hernandez, Kirsten Leigh Christensen Anderson, Trisha Chakrabarty
summary: Qualitatively reviews clinician and patient experiences with virtual reality interventions across psychiatric disorders, identifying implementation barriers, therapeutic mechanisms including presence and embodiment, and practical considerations for integrating VR into routine clinical practice based on stakeholder perspectives.
clinical_insight: Clinicians considering VR interventions should prioritize patient acceptability and comfort, using end-user preferences to guide session length, immersion level, and the degree of therapist accompaniment. VR exposure for anxiety and psychosis can be highly effective when patients feel in control of the virtual environment and understand the therapeutic rationale.
relevant_for: Psychologists integrating digital therapeutics and VR-based exposure into clinical practice

title: Emotion Regulation in Functional Neurological Disorder: A Multimethod Comparison of Intra- and Interpersonal Strategies
authors: Inga Niedtfeld, Johanna Hepp, Sabine Schellhaas, Astrid Steffen-Klatt, Dominik Klaasen van Husen, Valentin Held
summary: Investigates emotion regulation difficulties in functional neurological disorder (FND), examining how affect regulation strategies relate to symptom presentation.
clinical_insight: Assessing and targeting emotion regulation difficulties should be central to treating FND, with therapists helping patients develop adaptive strategies such as distress tolerance, affect labeling, and graduated exposure to avoided emotions. Psychoeducation that normalizes the link between emotional processing and neurological symptoms reduces stigma and improves treatment engagement.
relevant_for: Neuropsychiatrists and therapists in functional neurological disorder specialty clinics

title: Character Strengths in People with ADHD: A Systematic Review
authors: Biqing Chi, Rebecca de Leeuw, Marieke Fransen, Martine Hoogman
summary: Systematically reviews character strengths in people with ADHD, documenting both deficits in traditional strengths such as self-regulation and perseverance alongside potential enhanced strengths including creativity and spontaneity, with implications for strengths-based assessment and intervention approaches.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with ADHD patients should identify and leverage character strengths such as creativity, humor, and perseverance in treatment planning, using strengths-based approaches alongside symptom management. Incorporating VIA character strength assessments into sessions can boost self-efficacy and provide a counter-narrative to the deficit-focused lens patients often internalize.
relevant_for: ADHD clinicians and positive psychology practitioners in child and adult ADHD services

title: Agents are coming: A 5-Step Taxonomy of language-based AI-Systems for Psychiatry, Psychotherapy and Counselling
authors: Raphael Schuster, Constantin Yves Plessen, Per Carlbring, Andreas Walther
summary: Proposes an AI-driven taxonomy for classifying psychotherapy processes, using computational methods to identify common factors and therapeutic mechanisms.
clinical_insight: An AI-derived taxonomy of therapy processes can help supervisors and trainers identify which therapist behaviors and interaction patterns are most associated with good outcomes across modalities. Clinicians can use these computational frameworks to refine their case conceptualization skills, recognize effective intervention patterns, and engage in more targeted self-reflection on their in-session techniques.
relevant_for: Psychotherapy supervisors, trainers, and process researchers

title: Safety Mechanisms and Risk Mitigation in Generative AI Mental Health Chatbots: A Systematic Scoping Review
authors: Lotenna Olisaeloka, Chris Richardson, Angel Wang, Richard Munthali, Daniel Vigo
summary: Conducts a systematic scoping review of safety mechanisms and risk mitigation strategies in generative AI mental health chatbots, cataloguing current approaches to crisis detection, harmful content prevention, and therapeutic boundary maintenance across commercial and research AI platforms.
clinical_insight: Clinicians considering AI chatbot adjuncts should adopt proposed safety frameworks, including clear informed consent about AI limitations, crisis escalation protocols, and regular human oversight of chatbot interactions. AI tools should be positioned as supplements to — not replacements for — therapeutic relationships, with explicit boundaries about their scope.
relevant_for: Digital mental health program directors and clinicians integrating AI tools into practice

title: Untangling sex and gender differences in impression management and associated autism features in French autistic adults
authors: Wei Ai, Andrea Anchordoqui, Olena Bogdanova, Virginie Pomies, Romain Coutelle, Paola Atzori, marion Leboyer, Meng-Chuan Lai, Anouck Amestoy
summary: Disentangles sex (biological) and gender (social) differences in impression management strategies and associated autism features among French autistic adults, using differential item functioning analysis to identify whether autism measures capture genuine trait differences or measurement bias related to gendered social expectations.
clinical_insight: Assessors should account for camouflaging differences across sex and gender when screening for autism, as women and gender-diverse individuals may mask traits more effectively, leading to missed or delayed diagnoses. Tailoring clinical interviews to probe for hidden autistic features — such as internal distress from masking, sensory coping strategies, and social exhaustion — improves diagnostic accuracy.
relevant_for: Autism assessment clinicians and neuropsychologists in adult diagnostic services

title: A Scoping Review of Service User Experiences of Potential Harm in Early Intervention in Psychosis Services in the UK
authors: Georgie Hudson, India Francis-Crossley, Craig S. Mackie, Rachel Hiller, James B. Kirkbride, Claire Powell
summary: Critically examines potential harms arising from early psychosis intervention, including stigma, identity disruption, and over-treatment, calling for balanced approaches.
clinical_insight: Early psychosis programs, while beneficial, can inadvertently stigmatize young people and disrupt identity development through labeling and intensive treatment demands. Clinicians should adopt a shared decision-making approach, carefully weighing the benefits of early intervention against risks of pathologizing normative experiences, and preserve the young person's sense of agency throughout treatment.
relevant_for: Clinicians and case managers in early psychosis intervention services

title: Cognitive Behavioral Immersion for Depression in the Metaverse: Virtual Reality Outperforms Flat-Screens in Randomized Controlled Trial
authors: Iony Ezawa, Francisco Ramos, Steven Hollon, Gloria T. Han, Noah Robinson
summary: Randomized controlled trial testing virtual reality-enhanced cognitive behavioral interventions for depression, examining added benefit over standard CBT.
clinical_insight: Tests whether adding virtual reality environments to standard CBT enhances depression treatment outcomes, potentially through immersive behavioral activation or cognitive restructuring scenarios. If effective, clinicians could incorporate VR-based exercises—such as virtual exposure to rewarding activities or perspective-taking environments—to augment traditional CBT, particularly for clients who struggle with imaginal techniques or have limited access to real-world activation opportunities.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners and depression treatment specialists with access to VR technology

title: Psychometric Profiles of Object Relations and Identity in Women with Bulimia Nervosa: A Psychodynamic Pilot Study
authors: Evgeniya S. Profatilova
summary: Conducts a psychodynamic pilot study examining psychometric profiles of object relations and identity diffusion in women with bulimia nervosa, using the TAT and object relations measures to explore how internalized object representations and identity consolidation differ from non-eating-disorder controls.
clinical_insight: Frames bulimia nervosa as rooted in early relational disturbances and internalized object relations, suggesting that binging and purging serve functions related to managing internalized relational conflicts rather than solely body image concerns. Psychodynamic therapists can use this formulation to explore transference patterns, early attachment wounds, and the symbolic meaning of disordered eating behaviors, complementing CBT approaches with deeper relational work for clients who have not responded to symptom-focused treatment alone.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic psychotherapists and eating disorder specialists integrating psychodynamic and CBT approaches

title: Cognitive State Integration Method (CSIM): A Self-Administered Metacognitive Technique for State-Dependent Cognitive Changes in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorders
authors: Horiikomi
summary: Introduces the Cognitive State Integration Method (CSIM), a self-administered metacognitive technique for OCD that enables patients to record automatic thoughts and emotions during symptom episodes and compare them with baseline cognition during remission. Designed as an adjunct to Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) within Reflective Cognitive Behavioral Training (RCBT), CSIM aims to enhance metacognitive awareness and facilitate recognition of intrusive thought inaccuracy.
clinical_insight: CSIM offers clinicians a structured tool to bridge the gap between in-session ERP work and patients' daily self-monitoring, potentially strengthening metacognitive awareness outside the therapy room. By providing a concrete comparison framework between symptomatic and remitted states, it may help patients develop more nuanced insight into the distorted nature of obsessional thinking. Clinicians working with treatment-resistant OCD patients may find CSIM particularly valuable as an adjunctive technique when standard ERP alone yields incomplete response.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in OCD treatment, particularly those integrating metacognitive and third-wave CBT approaches

title: Inner Speech and Borderline Personality Disorder
authors: Romain Bourdoncle, Valérian Chambon, Pierre O. Jacquet, Axel Baptista
summary: Examines the phenomenology and functional characteristics of inner speech in borderline personality disorder using experience sampling and experimental paradigms, investigating how inner dialogue quality, dialogic complexity, and self-critical versus supportive content relate to emotional dysregulation and impulsivity.
clinical_insight: Links disturbed inner speech patterns—such as fragmented self-dialogue and poor internal coherence—to core BPD features including identity diffusion and emotional dysregulation. Therapists can use this understanding to help clients develop a more coherent and compassionate internal voice through techniques like chair work, voice dialogue, or structured journaling, directly targeting the intrapsychic processes that underlie BPD instability.
relevant_for: DBT and schema therapy clinicians treating borderline personality disorder

title: Do Mindful Partners Make a Difference? A Meta-Analytic Test of the Actor-Partner Interdependence Model in Dyadic Couples
authors: Sin U Lam, William T. Hoyt, Caroline M. Swords, Siyan Gao, Alaina Srivastav, Simon Goldberg
summary: Meta-analysis using the actor-partner interdependence model to examine how mindfulness in one partner affects relationship outcomes for both partners.
clinical_insight: Shows that one partner's mindfulness practice can produce cross-over benefits for relationship satisfaction in the other partner, supporting the inclusion of individual mindfulness training within couples therapy. Therapists can recommend that even one partner engage in regular mindfulness practice as a lever for improving dyadic outcomes, or incorporate brief mindfulness exercises into couples sessions to strengthen both partners' relational awareness and reactivity regulation.
relevant_for: Couples therapists and relationship counselors integrating mindfulness-based interventions

title: Ketamine’s Impact on Rumination-Related Brain Dynamics: Insights From a Randomized Controlled fMRI Trial
authors: Marvin Sören Meiering, David Weigner, Matti Gärtner, Luisa Carstens, Christian Keicher, Gerd Luippold, Andreas Wunder, Anne Weigand, Simone Grimm
summary: Uses fMRI to investigate how ketamine affects neural correlates of rumination, providing neurobiological evidence for ketamine's rapid antidepressant mechanism.
clinical_insight: Provides neurobiological evidence that ketamine rapidly disrupts the neural circuits underlying repetitive negative thinking (rumination), suggesting a mechanism beyond simple mood elevation. Clinicians overseeing ketamine-assisted therapy can leverage this by timing psychotherapeutic interventions to coincide with the post-infusion window when rumination pathways are most disrupted, potentially enhancing the integration of cognitive and behavioral strategies for treatment-resistant depression.
relevant_for: Psychiatrists and psychotherapists involved in ketamine-assisted psychotherapy programs

title: The Role of Temporal Proximity in Fear Learning and Intrusive Memories during a Trauma Film Conditioning Paradigm
authors: Mikaela Richardson, Rahul Ashwin Masurkar, Lilyan Tyson, Khalisa Amir Hamzah, Gia Nhi Lam, Ottmar V. Lipp, Madeline Jarvis, Jessica Hargreaves, Ellen Barner, Casey Agassiz, Sarah Olsson, Yi Wang, Luke John Ney
summary: Investigates how temporal proximity between conditioned stimuli and unconditioned stimuli affects fear learning, with implications for exposure therapy timing.
clinical_insight: Demonstrates that the temporal gap between a feared cue and an aversive outcome influences the strength and persistence of fear learning, with implications for how exposure therapy sessions are designed. Clinicians can use these findings to optimize the timing and spacing of exposure exercises—for example, by considering whether the feared outcome is perceived as immediate or delayed—to maximize fear extinction and reduce the likelihood of fear renewal between sessions.
relevant_for: Exposure therapists and anxiety disorder specialists using prolonged exposure or fear extinction protocols

title: Development and Validation of a Short-form (6-item) Version of the Clinician-Administered Dissociative States Scale (CADSS-SF)
authors: Ursule Taujanskaite, Sunjeev Kamboj
summary: Develops and validates a short-form version of the Clinician-Administered Dissociative States Scale (CADSS) for efficient dissociation screening in clinical settings.
clinical_insight: Offers a validated short-form dissociation screening tool that retains the psychometric properties of the full CADSS while being practical for busy clinical settings. Clinicians can incorporate this brief measure into routine intakes and ongoing monitoring for clients with trauma histories, enabling faster identification of dissociative symptoms that may require targeted intervention such as grounding techniques or phase-based trauma treatment.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and intake assessors in community mental health and hospital-based settings

title: “Does it really count?”: Women’s Experiences of Doubt and Impostor Syndrome about an Adult Autism Diagnosis
authors: Imogen Rowley, Sarune Savickaite
summary: Explores the phenomenon of diagnostic doubt among adults who receive an autism diagnosis, examining its psychological impact and implications for post-diagnostic support.
clinical_insight: Reveals that many adults experience significant doubt following an autism diagnosis, which can undermine the very sense of identity and self-understanding the diagnosis was meant to provide. Clinicians conducting or supporting adult autism assessments should proactively address diagnostic doubt in post-diagnostic sessions, normalizing ambivalence while helping clients integrate the diagnosis into their self-narrative through psychoeducation and identity exploration work.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and therapists conducting adult autism assessments and post-diagnostic counseling

title: Beyond Symptom Reduction:  The Causal Effect of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy on Interpersonal Problems
authors: Jonas Hallgans, Stephan Bartholdy, Tim Kaiser, Marit Kristine List, Eva-Lotta Brakemeier
summary: Examines the causal effect of routine outpatient CBT on reducing interpersonal problems using a quasi-experimental design comparing treated patients with a naturalistic waitlist control. The study finds that CBT produces meaningful reductions in interpersonal difficulties beyond general symptom improvement, with heterogeneous treatment effects across interpersonal problem subtypes.
clinical_insight: This study provides empirical support for what many CBT therapists observe clinically: that therapy changes not just symptoms but relational patterns. The finding of heterogeneous treatment effects means therapists should assess specific interpersonal profiles (e.g., intrusive vs. avoidant vs. vindictive) at intake to tailor interventions and set realistic expectations. A limitation is the quasi-experimental rather than randomized design, though the routine outpatient setting enhances ecological validity.
relevant_for: CBT therapists treating adults with comorbid mood and personality disorders in outpatient settings

title: Mental Health and Intolerance of Uncertainty across Domains and Age: A Special Role for Intolerance of Social Uncertainty
authors: Weike Wang, Sarah Daniels, Susanne Schweizer
summary: Investigates whether intolerance of uncertainty (IU) operates similarly across different life domains (health, finance, social, environmental) and age groups in relation to mental health outcomes. Findings reveal that intolerance of social uncertainty shows a uniquely strong association with anxiety and depression, suggesting domain-specific pathways rather than a unitary IU construct.
clinical_insight: When assessing and treating anxiety disorders, clinicians should move beyond global IU measures and specifically probe intolerance of social uncertainty, which appears to be the most clinically potent domain. For patients with social anxiety, GAD, or health anxiety, differentiating the domain of uncertainty the patient struggles with can guide more precise exposure hierarchies and cognitive restructuring targets. The cross-sectional design limits causal inference, and the study does not include clinical samples exclusively, so findings need validation in treatment-seeking populations.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists treating generalized anxiety, social anxiety, and health anxiety disorders

title: Evolutionary Systems Therapy for Personality Pathology
authors: Simone Cheli
summary: Introduces Evolutionary Systems Therapy as a novel integrative approach to personality pathology that combines evolutionary clinical psychology with systems theory, offering a framework for understanding personality disorder symptoms as maladaptive extreme variants of evolved social strategies.
clinical_insight: This novel integrative framework combines evolutionary psychology with systems thinking, offering clinicians a new lens for understanding maladaptive patterns as evolved strategies within relational contexts. It can enhance case conceptualization and guide interventions that work with rather than against clients' instinctive behavioral tendencies.
relevant_for: Integrative psychotherapists and clinicians interested in evolutionary approaches to case formulation

title: Use of Multisensory Information as Facilitator of Group Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
authors: Koffert et al.
summary: This paper proposes a novel clinical method for enhancing group schema therapy (GST) for borderline personality disorder (BPD) by incorporating patient-recorded video home assignments. Patients film themselves at home following therapist instructions, then review the recordings in the group session to observe their own speech, gestures, postures, and facial expressions. These multisensory observations are used to examine and restructure dysfunctional emotion-cognition links, with the group providing corrective feedback. The authors present a clinical vignette illustrating the technique and argue that multisensory information facilitates cognitive restructuring more effectively than verbal report alone.
clinical_insight: High dropout rates in BPD group therapy remain a significant clinical challenge. This approach leverages embodied self-observation to help patients access maladaptive schemas through a concrete, multisensory channel rather than relying solely on verbal introspection — a method that may bypass the alexithymic and dissociative barriers common in BPD. Clinicians running schema therapy groups could integrate video-based home assignments to strengthen the experiential component of limited re-parenting and schema mode work. A notable limitation is the reliance on a single clinical vignette without controlled efficacy data.
relevant_for: Clinicians facilitating group schema therapy for adults with borderline personality disorder

title: USE OF MULTISENSORY INFORMATION AS FACILITATOR OF  GROUP SCHEMA THERAPY FOR BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER
authors: Tarja Koffert, Sinikka Luutonen, Jarmo Hietala
summary: Proposes a novel method for facilitating group schema therapy (GST) for BPD using patient-made video recordings watched collectively during sessions. The approach aims to reduce high drop-out rates and heterogeneous treatment responses by making schema modes more observable and tangible for both patients and therapists through multisensory information.
clinical_insight: Schema therapists running BPD groups should consider incorporating patient-generated video homework where patients record themselves in triggering situations and review them in-session. This technique makes abstract schema modes (e.g., Vulnerable Child, Detached Protector) directly observable, potentially accelerating schema mode recognition and limited reparenting. The main limitation is the theoretical proposal stage — controlled trials are needed before widespread adoption, and therapists must carefully manage potential shame and self-criticism triggered by video self-observation.
relevant_for: Schema therapists facilitating group therapy for borderline personality disorder

title: Working hard to stay safe:  Effects of anxiety sensitivity on repetitive threat-neutralization behavior
authors: Riley Rozniarek, Annette Rostel, Aardron Robinson, Ryan Smith, Martin Paulus, Robin Aupperle, Hannah Berg
summary: Examined repetitive threat-neutralization (RTN) behavior — a pattern common across anxiety and OCD — using a novel Tap-to-Safety paradigm. Adults with anxiety-related disorders (n=40) showed slowed extinction of RTN compared to healthy controls, and anxiety sensitivity predicted more persistent threat-neutralization. The findings suggest RTN is a measurable, modifiable behavioral marker that persists even when the threat is removed.
clinical_insight: Repetitive checking, reassurance-seeking, and neutralizing behaviors that clients present in session likely reflect the same threat-neutralization mechanism captured in this paradigm. The finding that extinction is slowed in anxiety-disordered individuals supports the clinical observation that simply telling clients their fears are unfounded rarely works — clinicians should expect these behaviors to be entrenched and plan exposure-based interventions accordingly, rather than relying on cognitive restructuring alone.
relevant_for: CBT and exposure therapists working with anxiety disorders and OCD, particularly those with prominent checking or neutralizing rituals

title: Exploring the Link Between Hypersomnolence and Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: A Case Series.
authors: Vlad Bucurescu, Laure Peter-Derex, Maria Livia Fantini, Christophe Moderie, Benjamin PUTOIS
summary: Retrospective case series of 17 patients previously diagnosed with idiopathic hypersomnia who were reassessed and found to meet criteria for complex PTSD. The study highlights a possible association between hypersomnolence and trauma-related disorders, suggesting that excessive sleepiness may be an underrecognized symptom of CPTSD rather than a primary sleep disorder.
clinical_insight: Sleep medicine and trauma therapists should collaborate when patients present with unexplained hypersomnolence, as this may signal underlying CPTSD rather than a primary sleep disorder. For trauma therapists, screening for excessive daytime sleepiness could reveal a dissociative shutdown pattern that responds to phase-based trauma treatment (stabilization before processing). The case series design is inherently limited — it cannot establish causation or prevalence, and retrospective reassessment introduces diagnostic bias. However, it opens an important clinical question about whether sleep-focused interventions alone are insufficient for this subgroup.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and sleep medicine specialists assessing patients with unexplained hypersomnia

title: Peritraumatic Reactions During Sexual Assault: Associations with Assault Characteristics and Mental Health
authors: Kreis et al.
summary: In a cross-sectional study of 425 recent sexual assault victims (96% female, M=26.2 years), exploratory factor analysis of a 23-item peritraumatic reactions questionnaire developed with lived-experience experts identified six factors: Dissociation and freeze, Cognitive strategies, Active defense behavior, Negative emotions, Perceived life threat, and Reactive surrender. Dissociation/freeze and reactive surrender were far more common than active resistance, directly challenging rape stereotypes. All factors except cognitive strategies and active defense were positively associated with post-traumatic stress and depression/anxiety symptoms.
clinical_insight: This study provides clinicians with an empirically grounded framework for assessing peritraumatic responses that moves beyond simplistic fight-or-flight models. The identification of reactive surrender as a common, adaptive survival strategy directly challenges victim-blaming narratives that clients often internalize. For therapists conducting trauma processing (PE, EMDR, CPT), understanding which peritraumatic reactions most strongly predict later PTSD and depression can inform case conceptualization and treatment prioritization. A limitation is the cross-sectional design and female-heavy sample, which constrains generalizability to male victims.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with sexual assault survivors, particularly those using trauma-focused therapies such as PE, EMDR, or CPT.

title: Investigating the Effects and Mechanisms of Feedback in Psychotherapy:  A Conceptual Framework
authors: Ondřej Fajstavr, Tomáš Řiháček, Michal Čevelíček, Klára Jonášová
summary: Develops a comprehensive conceptual framework for understanding how therapist feedback influences psychotherapy processes and outcomes, synthesizing evidence on feedback timing, content, delivery method, and patient characteristics that moderate feedback effects across therapeutic orientations.
clinical_insight: Routine client feedback on progress and alliance significantly improves outcomes and helps identify clients who are not benefiting. Therapists should implement structured feedback tools to catch treatment failures early and adjust their approach collaboratively before clients disengage or deteriorate.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists across all modalities and clinical training program directors

title: Unraveling individual patterns of passive and active suicidal ideation: A person-specific network modeling exploration
authors: Lara Kirchhofer, Ai Ye, Isaac Galatzer-Levy, Urte Scholz, Birgit Kleim, Stephanie Homan
summary: Uses person-specific network modeling of ecological momentary assessment data to examine how passive and active suicidal ideation differ at the individual level. While group-level networks capture average patterns, person-specific models reveal unique configurations of cognitive and emotional states that precede shifts between passive wish-to-die and active intent, highlighting the limitations of one-size-fits-all risk assessment.
clinical_insight: This study underscores why standardized suicide risk assessments often fail: the pathways from distress to suicidal ideation are individually configured. Therapists should collaboratively map each patient’s personal network of triggers (e.g., hopelessness leading to passive ideation vs. anger leading to active ideation) and use this formulation to guide safety planning. The person-specific approach aligns well with the CAMS framework. A key limitation is the intensive EMA design, which may not be feasible in routine clinical care, though the conceptual framework can still inform clinical interviews.
relevant_for: Suicide prevention clinicians and crisis therapists using EMA-informed formulation

title: Beyond diagnostic artefact: structural stigma, epistemic injustice, and iatrogenic harm in the CPTSD–BPD debate (Response to D’agostino et al., 2026)
authors: Laurence Cobbaert
summary: A commentary arguing that the CPTSD-BPD diagnostic debate is incomplete when framed solely as a question of nosological overlap. Drawing on lived experience research and analyses of epistemic injustice, the paper demonstrates how the BPD label itself can produce structural stigma and iatrogenic harm, particularly for trauma survivors whose experiences are pathologized as personality dysfunction.
clinical_insight: Therapists should be mindful that the BPD diagnosis, while clinically useful for treatment planning, carries significant iatrogenic risks including self-stigma, invalidation of trauma narratives, and restrictive treatment access. For trauma-focused therapists, this commentary supports using formulation-based approaches (e.g., ICD-11 CPTSD framework) alongside or instead of BPD diagnoses, especially with complex trauma survivors. The blind spot is that this is a conceptual commentary rather than empirical research — therapists should balance these concerns with the practical reality that BPD diagnosis often unlocks insurance-funded treatment.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and clinical psychologists navigating the CPTSD vs. BPD diagnostic landscape

title: Psilocybin for Treatment-Resistant OCD: A Randomized Controlled Trial
authors: Benjamin Kelmendi, Thomas Adams, Terence Ching, Rachael Grazioplene, Stephen Kichuk, Geena Fram, Prerana Patel, Jeff Eilbott, Elizabeth D’Amico, Sarah Shnayder, Brad Martins, Calvin Bohner, Lucia Amoroso, Anastasia Jankovsky, Giuliana DePalmer, Gerard Valentine, Gabrielle Agin-Liebes, Jamila Hokanson, Christopher Pittenger
summary: Examines the relationship between mystical experiences during psilocybin sessions and outcomes in OCD treatment, discussing the role of altered states of consciousness.
clinical_insight: The intensity of mystical experiences during psilocybin sessions may predict OCD symptom improvement, suggesting clinicians facilitating psychedelic-assisted OCD treatment should attend to set-and-setting factors that promote meaningful psychological experiences. Integration sessions should help clients make sense of these experiences to consolidate therapeutic gains.
relevant_for: Psychedelic-assisted therapy researchers and OCD treatment specialists

title: Mapping Psychological (In)Flexibility in U.S. Military Veterans: A Directed Acyclic Graph Approach to the Hexaflex Model
authors: Sanders et al.
summary: Using Directed Acyclic Graphs in a sample of 283 U.S. military veterans, this study mapped potential causal pathways between components of the ACT Hexaflex model. Committed action and acceptance emerged as central drivers of psychological flexibility, while cognitive fusion appeared to initiate cascades of inflexibility—flowing through self-as-content and inaction to experiential avoidance, diminished present-moment awareness, and lost values contact. The study provides the first DAG-based analysis of the Hexaflex in a veteran population at elevated risk for mental health complaints.
clinical_insight: The identification of cognitive fusion as the primary entry point into inflexibility cascades has clear clinical implications: targeting defusion early in ACT treatment for veterans may disrupt downstream dysfunction across multiple Hexaflex processes. The central role of committed action in flexibility pathways supports behavioral activation as a key mechanism of change. Clinicians should note that these are cross-sectional pathways, not confirmed causal mechanisms, though the DAG approach provides stronger causal inference than standard correlational analyses.
relevant_for: ACT practitioners working with veterans or trauma-exposed populations, particularly those interested in process-based therapy and mechanisms of change

title: Harmful Healing: Iatrogenic Fragility in Psychological Safety Interventions
authors: Karina Vunnam
summary: Examines the phenomenon of iatrogenic fragility in psychological safety interventions, arguing that overly protective approaches to psychological safety may inadvertently reduce resilience, tolerance for discomfort, and capacity for growth, with recommendations for balancing safety with appropriate challenge.
clinical_insight: The evidence reviewed suggests trigger warnings may inadvertently reinforce avoidance and increase fragility. Clinicians working in educational or clinical settings should prioritize graded exposure, distress tolerance building, and resilience-focused approaches over accommodation-based strategies that may paradoxically maintain anxiety sensitivity.
relevant_for: University counseling center clinicians and clinical training supervisors

title: The Most Vulnerable Are Prone to Use AI Therapists: The Role of Attachment, Epistemic Trust, and Mental Health Symptoms in Acceptance of Digital Mental Health Interventions
authors: Bekes, Aafjes-van Doorn
summary: Surveying 1,612 potential patients and clinicians, this study used cluster analysis to identify three psychological profiles: Avoidant-not trusting, Secure-trusting-healthy, and Young-anxious-gullible-symptomatic. Paradoxically, the two most psychologically vulnerable clusters showed significantly higher acceptance of AI-based digital mental health interventions (avatars, chatbots), while the healthiest cluster preferred human-delivered teletherapy. Prior therapy experience was associated with lower AI acceptance overall. The authors suggest AI-based tools may serve as a critical entry point to care for individuals who struggle with human-to-human therapeutic relationships, but caution that these same individuals may miss out on the relational learning inherent in human therapy.
clinical_insight: As AI therapy tools proliferate, this study provides essential nuance for private practitioners. The paradoxical finding — that the most vulnerable patients are most drawn to AI therapists — carries significant clinical implications. On one hand, AI tools may lower the threshold for engaging patients who would otherwise avoid therapy due to attachment-related fears or epistemic mistrust. On the other, precisely these patients have the most to gain from the corrective relational experience that only a human therapist can provide. Clinicians should be prepared to discuss AI therapy options with avoidant or anxious patients not as a replacement for therapy, but as a potential stepping stone. A blind spot is the Prolific recruitment method, which may not capture the most treatment-averse individuals who never engage with any mental health platform.
relevant_for: Private practice clinicians considering how to position their services relative to emerging AI therapy tools, and those working with attachment-disrupted or treatment-reluctant patients

title: Testing Self-Help Interventions for Adults with Clinical Perfectionism:  A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Online Skills Modules to a Book
authors: Clarissa W. Ong, Eric Lee, Andrew Kurtz, Jacob Avendano, Kate Sheehan, Estella Fox, Andy Wall, Hannah M Johnson, Alexa Skolnik
summary: Randomized controlled trial comparing two self-help formats for clinical perfectionism—structured online skills modules versus bibliotherapy—evaluating differential effects on perfectionism cognitions, clinical outcomes, treatment adherence, and user satisfaction in adults with clinical-level perfectionistic presentations.
clinical_insight: A validated self-help protocol for perfectionism gives clinicians a structured bibliotherapy option to offer patients on waitlists or as a low-intensity step in a stepped-care model. Therapists can also extract specific CBT techniques from the manual (e.g., behavioral experiments challenging all-or-nothing standards) to integrate into individual sessions for perfectionistic presentations.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and guided self-help practitioners in university counseling and eating disorder services

title: Cost-Utility Analysis of Internet-Delivered Cognitive Control Training Plus Treatment as Usual Versus Treatment as Usual Alone for Relapse Prevention in Major Depressive Disorder
authors: Demeester et al.
summary: This health economic evaluation assessed the cost-utility of adding internet-delivered Cognitive Control Training (aPASAT) to treatment as usual for adults in remission from MDD. Using a Markov model over a five-year time horizon from the Belgian healthcare payer perspective, aPASAT + TAU dominated TAU alone, yielding additional QALYs and lower healthcare costs. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis indicated a 99.9% probability of cost-effectiveness at a €48,000/QALY threshold, with scenario analyses consistently confirming cost savings and improved outcomes.
clinical_insight: The finding that a fully unguided, low-cost digital intervention can both improve outcomes and reduce costs has significant implications for stepped-care models in depression relapse prevention. Clinicians working in primary care or mental health systems should consider recommending CCT as a scalable prevention tool for remitted MDD patients. A notable blind spot: the model parameters rely heavily on published literature rather than primary trial data for the economic evaluation, so real-world implementation may yield different cost profiles. The tension between the promise of scalable digital prevention and the need for long-term adherence data remains unresolved.
relevant_for: Clinicians and healthcare administrators designing relapse prevention pathways for major depressive disorder

title: Day-to-Day Dynamics of Facial Emotion Expressions in Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
authors: Ringwald et al.
summary: This innovative study applied idiographic (person-specific) network modeling to daily facial emotion expressions and PTSD symptoms in 112 World Trade Center responders over 90 days, yielding 8,953 video diary recordings analyzed with facial emotion recognition. Eighty percent of participants showed at least one reliable expression-symptom link, with significant heterogeneity—some individuals’ symptoms related to emotional over-expressivity while others showed under-expressivity. The study moves beyond self-report to capture outward emotional behavior as a mechanism maintaining PTSD.
clinical_insight: The finding that PTSD emotion-symptom dynamics are highly individualized has direct implications for case conceptualization: clinicians cannot assume a uniform emotional presentation in PTSD. Some patients may benefit from interventions targeting emotional expression (e.g., exposure-based approaches), while others may need help amplifying emotional awareness. The study’s strength lies in its ecological validity and idiographic approach, though the WTC responder sample limits generalizability to other trauma populations such as interpersonal trauma survivors.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating PTSD, particularly those interested in personalized case formulation and the role of emotional expression in symptom maintenance

title: Personality Markers for Common Mental Disorders: A Multi-Trait, Multi-Diagnosis, Multi-Rater Approach
authors: Ye Zhang, Michelle Luciano, Uku Vainik, René Mõttus
summary: Using data from the Estonian Biobank (N=3,221), this study identifies facet-level personality markers that predict common mental disorders including anxiety and mood disorders, going beyond broad trait domains to examine specific personality facets. By employing a multi-trait, multi-diagnosis, and multi-rater design, the research reveals that narrow personality facets substantially outperform broad domains in predicting clinical outcomes, with different facets predicting different disorders.
clinical_insight: The finding that narrow personality facets outperform broad traits for clinical prediction has significant implications for intake assessment in private practice. Rather than relying solely on broad measures like the NEO-FFI, clinicians should consider facet-level assessment tools like the NEO-PI-R or IPIP-300 to identify specific risk profiles. For example, a patient scoring high on vulnerability (a neuroticism facet) but low on impulsiveness may have a different prognosis and treatment trajectory than a patient with the same overall neuroticism score but the reverse facet profile. The tension between assessment efficiency (short broad measures) and predictive precision (long facet measures) is a practical dilemma for time-limited intake sessions. A blind spot is that biobank samples, while large, may underrepresent the severity and comorbidity typical of clinical populations, potentially overestimating how cleanly personality facets map onto single diagnoses.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting personality-informed case formulations and intake assessments for anxiety and mood disorders in adult private practice

title: Accessibility Model of Overgeneral Autobiographical Memory
authors: Noboru Matsumoto, David John Hallford, Lynn Ann Watson
summary: Introduces and validates the Accessibility Model of Overgeneral Autobiographical Memory, proposing a novel cognitive mechanism whereby reduced memory accessibility—rather than impaired retrieval or executive control—drives overgeneral autobiographical recall in depression and trauma-related conditions.
clinical_insight: Overgeneral autobiographical memory is a transdiagnostic marker that therapists can assess using the Autobiographical Memory Test. Computational modeling of this phenomenon enables more precise identification of patients at risk for treatment non-response, allowing clinicians to target memory specificity training (e.g., MEST) early in treatment to improve outcomes in both depression and PTSD.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and trauma specialists working with depression and PTSD

title: Autistic Psychotherapists’ Practice and Client-Side Experiencing of Therapy: Therapeutic  Orientation Preferences, Therapist-Client Fit Factors, and In-Session Experiences
authors: Matthew J. Bolton
summary: A mixed-methods study of 27 autistic psychotherapists found that both as practitioners and as therapy clients themselves, they preferred nondirective approaches, encouraged leaning into difficult feelings, valued in-session exploration of the therapeutic relationship, and emphasized present-moment focus. As clients, they wanted their therapists to challenge and confront them; as therapists, they prioritized warmth and unconditional positive regard. These preferences showed both overlaps and notable divergences from neurotypical therapist norms across cultures.
clinical_insight: This study is among the first to center autistic clinicians own perspectives on therapy, revealing that neurodivergent practitioners bring distinct preferences that may better serve neurodivergent clients. The preference for nondirectivity combined with a desire for relational depth and confrontation challenges purely manualized approaches and suggests that autistic clients may thrive in psychodynamic, person-centered, or relationally-oriented ACT settings. A key blind spot in the field is the near-total absence of neurodivergent voices in treatment development — incorporating autistic clinicians perspectives could meaningfully improve therapist-client fit and reduce dropout among neurodivergent populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with neurodivergent adults, particularly those in private practice seeing autistic or ADHD clients

title: Mapping Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: A Proof of Concept for Network-Based Case Formulation
authors: Blomfield et al.
summary: Demonstrates a proof of concept for idiographic network-based case formulation in PTSD, using ecological momentary assessment data to build personalized symptom networks grounded in the Ehlers and Clark cognitive model of PTSD.
clinical_insight: Building individualized symptom network formulations for PTSD allows therapists to identify which specific symptoms (e.g., nightmares, avoidance, hypervigilance) are most central for each patient, enabling precision targeting of treatment. Rather than applying a uniform protocol, clinicians can use idiographic network maps to decide whether to prioritize re-experiencing, avoidance, or negative cognition interventions first based on each patient's unique network structure.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists developing personalized PTSD treatment plans

title: When Happiness Becomes Aversive: A Meta-Analysis of Fear of Happiness in Depression and Anxiety
authors: Collins, Coster
summary: This meta-analysis examined associations between fear of happiness—a construct representing reward devaluation—and psychopathology in depression and anxiety. The analysis synthesized available evidence on how aversive responses to positive affect relate to symptom severity and treatment outcomes. The construct is particularly relevant because many current interventions target reward deficits in depression and anxiety, yet fear of happiness may undermine the efficacy of these reward-based approaches by making positive emotional experiences feel threatening or unsafe.
clinical_insight: Fear of happiness represents an underrecognized mechanism that may explain why some patients with depression or anxiety fail to benefit from interventions targeting positive affect or reward processing (e.g., behavioral activation, positive psychology interventions). Clinicians should be alert to patients who resist, avoid, or feel distressed by positive experiences, as this may indicate fear of happiness rather than simple anhedonia. This construct bridges the literatures on reward processing, emotion regulation, and clinical intervention. A key blind spot: the meta-analysis highlights the current lack of validated, brief clinical measures for fear of happiness, limiting its integration into routine assessment. The tension between treating reward deficits and inadvertently triggering reward devaluation remains a challenge for intervention design.
relevant_for: Clinicians using behavioral activation, positive psychology, or well-being interventions who encounter patients who resist positive experiences

title: Diminished Positive Affect in Social Anhedonia: Linking Anticipation and Experience During Dyadic Interaction
authors: Riehle et al.
summary: This preregistered study examined how social anhedonia relates to affective responses before, during, and after dyadic social interactions in 100 young adults. Controlling for social anxiety and suspiciousness, social anhedonia was specifically associated with diminished positive affect both in anticipation of and during social interaction, but not with increased negative affect. Negative affect during interaction was specifically explained by co-occurring social anxiety. An indirect effect of social anhedonia via diminished positive experience on social motivation and volition was also found.
clinical_insight: The dissociation between social anhedonia (diminished positive affect) and social anxiety (increased negative affect) has direct implications for differential diagnosis and treatment planning. Clinicians often encounter patients presenting with social withdrawal who may have either or both conditions. This study suggests that treatments should be tailored: social anhedonia may respond to behavioral activation targeting reward processing and positive affect forecasting, whereas social anxiety requires exposure-based approaches. The exploratory finding that socially anhedonic participants had a comforting effect on their interaction partners warrants investigation. A limitation: the WEIRD young adult sample limits generalizability to older clinical populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating adults with social withdrawal, social anhedonia, or social anxiety disorder

title: Identifying (Effective) Elements in the Psychological Treatment of Suicidality: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
authors: Nikki van Eijk, Daan Creemers, Alexia Marchand, Remco de Winter, Lizanne Schweren, Renske Gilissen, Wouter van Ballegooijen
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis breaks down psychotherapeutic interventions for suicidality into individual components rather than evaluating whole treatment packages. By analyzing which specific elements (e.g., safety planning, cognitive restructuring, distress tolerance skills) are associated with reductions in suicidal ideation and behavior, the study aims to move beyond brand-name therapy comparisons toward identifying the active ingredients that make suicidality interventions effective.
clinical_insight: Component-level analysis of suicidality treatments is a paradigm shift from the dominant model of comparing whole therapy packages. For clinicians, this means that rather than asking which manual to follow, the field is moving toward asking which specific techniques to deploy for which suicidal presentation. The tension here is between protocol fidelity and component flexibility: while manualized approaches like DBT and CBT for suicide prevention have strong evidence bases, this component approach may reveal that certain elements (e.g., means restriction counseling, crisis response planning) are the true drivers of change. A notable blind spot is that most suicidality trials still exclude the most acutely at-risk patients, limiting the generalizability of these findings to private practice settings where high-risk clients are the norm.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with suicidal patients across modalities, particularly those in crisis intervention and DBT-informed practice

title: Instinctual Refinement Therapy for Complex Trauma: A Proof-of-Concept Case Series and Research Roadmap
authors: Iqbal et al.
summary: This proof-of-concept case series (N=30, 70% female, mean age 32.4) evaluated Instinctual Refinement Therapy (IRT), a novel protocol targeting evolutionarily conserved instinctual systems disrupted by early adverse experiences. IRT integrates predictive processing and memory reconsolidation frameworks. Preliminary results showed a 94% clinical improvement rate (28/30 cases) over a mean of 54.2 sessions, with large aggregate effect sizes (d=2.1) maintained at follow-up in 80% of assessed cases. Presentations were diverse and often treatment-resistant, including anxiety, mood, and psychotic disorders.
clinical_insight: IRT represents a potentially paradigm-shifting approach for complex, treatment-resistant trauma by targeting instinctual disruptions rather than cognitive schemas alone. The large effect sizes in a treatment-resistant population are striking, though the retrospective design and absence of a control group limit causal inference. Clinicians working with complex trauma clients who have not responded to conventional trauma-focused therapies (EMDR, TF-CBT, sensorimotor psychotherapy) may find IRT a promising next step. A critical blind spot is the lack of formal diagnostic characterization and the long mean treatment duration (54 sessions), which raises questions about feasibility in standard outpatient settings.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in complex trauma, treatment-resistant PTSD/CPTSD, and dissociation who are seeking novel therapeutic approaches for clients unresponsive to standard protocols.

title: When “Bothered by” Means “Often”: Evidence for Misinterpretation of the Patient Health Questionnaire
authors: Panayiotou et al.
summary: This study investigated how respondents interpret the PHQ’s instructions across a general population sample (N=503) and a clinical sample with elevated depression (N=349). Only 54.7% of community participants and 15.5% of the clinical sample interpreted the PHQ as instructed (frequency of being bothered). When asked directly, only 21.3% and 11.7% respectively had responded based on the instructed interpretation, with even fewer intending to do so in the future. PHQ-8 validity varied depending on how participants interpreted the instructions, raising questions about whether the measure captures symptom frequency, distress severity, or some combination.
clinical_insight: This study has immediate clinical implications: if only 11-21% of respondents interpret the PHQ as intended, then depression severity scores in both research and clinical practice are systematically contaminated by response interpretation variance. Clinicians should be aware that a patient reporting being “bothered a lot” may be reporting frequency rather than distress, or vice versa. The finding that clinical participants were even less likely to interpret instructions correctly than community participants suggests that severity of symptoms may further distort comprehension. A blind spot: the study cannot determine which interpretation produces more valid depression measurement—it only demonstrates that the current instrument conflates distinct constructs.
relevant_for: All clinicians using the PHQ-9, PHQ-8, or PHQ-2 for depression screening or outcome monitoring

title: Personalizing Chronic Pain Treatment Using Person-Specific Networks: A Single-Case Experimental Design Study
authors: Saskia Scholten, Verena Eunike Hofmann, Felicitas Kininger, Julia Glombiewski
summary: This proof-of-concept study evaluated whether person-specific network analysis can guide individualized treatment target selection for chronic pain patients with comorbidities. Twelve patients (mean age 60.25) and nine therapists participated in an ABCD single-case experimental design with randomized baselines. Qualitative interviews explored feasibility and acceptability. Compliance reached at least 80% in seven participants, with a mean of 298 observations per person. Multilevel analysis showed an average decrease in disability scores from baseline to intervention phase. The authors conclude that the clinical value of data-informed personalization lies not in algorithmic precision but in facilitating patient-centered, reflective clinical reasoning in complex cases.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with chronic pain, this study supports using repeated self-monitoring data to collaboratively identify each patient's unique symptom network before selecting intervention targets. Rather than applying a standard protocol, the approach uses individual network visualization to help patients recognize patterns in their own daily experience, then matches intervention modules to the most central nodes in their personal network. The finding that structured data-informed reflection, rather than algorithmic output per se, drove therapeutic benefit suggests that network-informed case formulation can be integrated into existing clinical workflows without requiring specialized software. Therapists treating complex somatic presentations where comorbidities obscure the primary maintaining mechanism may find this approach particularly useful for identifying which target to address first.
relevant_for: Therapists working with chronic pain and medically unexplained symptoms who want to integrate personalized formulation into treatment planning

title: Minority stressors and protection factors in trans and non-binary individuals
authors: Gabi Dutra Domingos, Cyber Torres, Dom Condeixa de Araújo, Caio Maximino
summary: Systematically reviews minority stressors (distal and proximal) and protective factors in trans and non-binary individuals, examining the relative contributions of discrimination, internalized stigma, gender affirmation, and community connectedness to mental health outcomes and identifying intervention targets.
clinical_insight: Clinicians should assess for minority stress–specific variables such as internalized transphobia, anticipated discrimination, and gender-affirming support when working with transgender and non-binary clients. These factors have distinct pathways to depression and anxiety that require culturally informed, targeted intervention beyond standard CBT protocols.
relevant_for: LGBTQ+-affirming therapists and gender clinic mental health providers

title: The Interplay Between Cognitive-Behavioural and -Interpersonal Factors in Disordered Eating: A Network Analysis
authors: Chantal P Delaquis, Yasmin Brown, Dean Spirou, Jayanthi (Jay) Raman, Sylvie Berthoz
summary: This study integrated the transdiagnostic cognitive-behavioral and cognitive-interpersonal models of eating disorders using network analysis in 493 Australian participants reporting ED symptoms, of whom 271 scored above the clinical cutoff on the EDE-QS and 108 reported a lifetime eating disorder diagnosis. A partial correlation network was estimated, with bootstrapped stability and centrality difference tests. Concern Over Mistakes emerged as the most central node overall (expected influence = 1.48) and the strongest bridge node connecting cognitive-behavioral and interpersonal clusters (bridge expected influence = 0.86). The edge between Overvaluation of Weight and Shape and Weight and Shape Rigidity was among the strongest in the network (0.45). The network demonstrated excellent stability.
clinical_insight: The prominence of Concern Over Mistakes as both the most central symptom and the primary bridge between cognitive-behavioral and interpersonal dimensions suggests that perfectionism, rather than body dissatisfaction alone, may be the engine that connects disordered eating cognition with relational dysfunction. For therapists, this supports directly assessing and targeting perfectionism early in treatment, even when a client presents primarily with shape concerns or interpersonal difficulties. The strong connection between Overvaluation of Weight and Shape and Rigidity indicates that rigid beliefs about weight, rather than the content of those beliefs alone, may be a key maintaining factor that deserves explicit attention in cognitive restructuring. These findings are cross-sectional and cannot confirm causal pathways, but they provide a empirically grounded map for prioritizing treatment targets within the transdiagnostic framework.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders using CBT-E or integrative approaches, particularly when clients present with comorbid perfectionism and interpersonal difficulties

title: Experiences with One-to-One Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga Sessions: Consensual Qualitative Research
authors: Kelsey Madison Dietrich, Nicole Strumbel, Ashley Baumann, Viann N Nguyen-Feng
summary: Uses consensual qualitative research methodology to examine client experiences with one-to-one trauma-sensitive yoga sessions at a trauma center, identifying perceived benefits including embodied safety, interoceptive awareness, and emotional processing alongside considerations for implementation within trauma treatment contexts.
clinical_insight: Indigenous-adapted trauma-sensitive yoga demonstrates that body-based interventions must be culturally grounded to be effective, guiding clinicians to incorporate community-specific healing frameworks rather than applying generic somatic protocols. Therapists working with Indigenous populations should collaborate with cultural advisors to integrate traditional practices, respect spiritual dimensions of the body, and ensure interventions do not replicate colonial dynamics. This model provides a template for culturally responsive somatic therapy that can be adapted to other marginalized communities.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with Indigenous and culturally diverse communities

title: "It's a balancing act": Autistic fathers navigating parenting in everyday life
authors: Chris Edwards, Abigail Love, Ru Ying Cai, Kees-jan Archbald, Dean Dickson, Sean Hynes, Abel Trindade, Sarah Edwards, Bronwyn Leigh, Vicki Gibbs
summary: This co-produced qualitative study explored the parenting experiences of 19 Autistic fathers using semi-structured interviews (spoken or written, per participant preference), analyzed with reflexive thematic analysis. Four Autistic fathers collaborated as co-researchers in study design, interview development, and analysis. Five themes were generated: finding joy and meaning in everyday fatherhood moments; becoming oneself through fatherhood, where parenting strengthened Autistic identity; walking the tightrope of family life, reflecting the continuous effort of balancing sensory, emotional, and practical demands; parenting as relational work, including the tension arising when fathers felt misunderstood by partners and family; and parenting in systems stacked against us, highlighting barriers in services and wider society that fail to recognize Autistic needs.
clinical_insight: Autistic fathers in this study described fatherhood as a source of identity consolidation and emotional connection, challenging the deficit-focused narrative that still predominates in clinical work with neurodivergent adults. The theme of walking a tightrope of sensory, emotional, and practical demands highlights that autistic parents may experience parenthood not as a category of impairment but as an ongoing balancing act that can become overwhelming when environmental demands exceed capacity. The finding that fathers felt most distressed not by parenting itself but by being misunderstood within family relationships and by services suggests that clinicians working with neurodivergent families should attend to the relational context and systemic barriers rather than focusing solely on individual coping strategies. When an autistic father presents in therapy, asking about parenting experiences may open a productive avenue for exploring identity, self-worth, and systemic stressors.
relevant_for: Therapists working with neurodivergent adults, particularly autistic parents or those navigating family life and relationship dynamics

title: Working with Dissociation in the Context of First Episode Psychosis: Guidance for Therapists
authors: Černis et al.
summary: This clinical guidance paper addresses the under-recognized phenomenon of dissociation in first-episode psychosis (FEP), focusing on a specific subtype termed 'felt sense of anomaly' dissociation. The authors present a cognitive-behavioural formulation where affect-related changes are interpreted as internal threats, driving a maintenance cycle of catastrophic appraisals and safety behaviours. The paper outlines assessment considerations, formulation frameworks, and evidence-based intervention techniques that can be integrated into existing CBT-for-psychosis protocols. Importantly, the guidance demonstrates that widely used therapeutic skills — including psychoeducation, behavioural experiments, and cognitive restructuring — can be effectively adapted to target dissociation within psychosis.
clinical_insight: Dissociation in psychosis is both prevalent and dangerous, associated with increased self-harm and suicidality, yet it is routinely missed in clinical assessment. This paper fills a critical training gap by providing a clear, actionable framework that does not require therapists to learn entirely new models — instead, it maps dissociation onto familiar CBT techniques. For private practitioners who may encounter psychotic-spectrum clients or complex trauma presentations with dissociative features, this guidance offers a structured approach to a phenomenon that often leaves clinicians feeling unskilled. The 'felt sense of anomaly' formulation is particularly useful for distinguishing dissociation from psychotic symptoms that require different management.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and clinical psychologists working with first-episode psychosis or trauma-related dissociation in adult populations

title: "Supporting Children on Therapy Waitlists: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Web-Based Parent-Focused Single Session Intervention for Child Anxiety"
authors: Madelaine Abel, Jessica L. Schleider, Jenna Sung, Alayna D'Amico, Dina R. Hirshfeld-Becker
summary: This pilot parallel-group RCT evaluated a self-guided, web-based single-session intervention (SSI) called Project EMPOWER, delivered to parents of children ages 4-13 with anxiety or OCD who were on a waitlist at a hospital-based CBT clinic. The SSI targets parental accommodation, a well-established family factor that can worsen child anxiety and undermine CBT efficacy. Parents (N=47) were randomized to receive the SSI (N=24) or waitlist as usual (N=23). At 2-week follow-up, parents who received the SSI reported significant reductions in accommodation (d=0.59), child anxiety symptoms (d=1.25), and significant increases in parent agency (d=0.68). Effects were maintained until children started CBT (mean wait of 13.54 weeks). Parents rated the SSI as highly acceptable per preregistered benchmarks.
clinical_insight: Single-session interventions for parents offer a practical, scalable solution to one of the most frustrating systemic problems in child mental health: long waitlists that leave families without support for months. For clinicians running waitlisted services, this study provides evidence that a brief, self-guided web module targeting parental accommodation can meaningfully reduce both accommodation behaviors and child anxiety symptoms while families wait for formal therapy. The effect size on child anxiety (d=1.25) is strikingly large for such a minimal intervention, though the small sample size warrants caution. The focus on parental accommodation is well-chosen: it is one of the most modifiable and clinically impactful factors in child anxiety, and reducing it before CBT begins may actually improve subsequent treatment outcomes. Clinicians should consider recommending SSI resources to families on their waitlists, and those in private practice can use the core principles (psychoeducation about accommodation, guided exercises to reduce it) as take-home materials in the very first session.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with child anxiety in community or hospital settings, particularly those managing long waitlists, and therapists interested in single-session intervention approaches

title: Testing a Modular Online Self-Help Intervention in Adults with Clinical Perfectionism: A Randomized Controlled Trial
authors: Ong et al.
summary: A randomized controlled trial (N=114, mean age 33.9) testing a modular online self-help intervention for clinically significant perfectionism, allowing participants to select their own treatment modules. Participants were randomly assigned to an online training app or an active control condition (self-help book). Both interventions led to similar improvements over time and may serve as helpful low-intensity treatments for clinical perfectionism.
clinical_insight: This study provides preliminary evidence that client-driven modular approaches to perfectionism treatment may be feasible and acceptable, aligning with the broader shift toward personalized psychotherapy. For private practitioners, the modular online format could serve as a scalable between-session adjunct, enabling clients to work on perfectionism-related cognitions and behaviors independently. The active control comparison strengthens confidence that any observed effects are specific to the intervention. A limitation is the lack of a waitlist control condition.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with perfectionism, obsessive-compulsive presentations, and high-achieving adult clients in private practice

title: "Targeting coping strategies, emotion regulation, and self-esteem in children with dyslexia to support mental health: A randomised-controlled trial of Clever Kids"
authors: Mark Boyes, Kirsten Melville, Mandy Nayton, Hannah Pizzey, Suze Leitao
summary: This pre-registered RCT evaluated Clever Kids, a nine-week socio-emotional wellbeing programme developed specifically for upper primary school children with dyslexia. Seventy-eight children (mean age 10.19 years, 54% female) with clinically diagnosed dyslexia were randomized to the programme (n=38) or a wait-list control (n=40). Outcomes included coping skills, emotion regulation, self-esteem, perseverance, assertiveness, and internalising and externalising symptoms measured at pre-, post-, and three-month follow-up. Significant intervention-by-time interactions were found for six outcomes: child-reported coping, emotion regulation, academic and social self-esteem, and parent-reported anxiety and depression, all maintained at three-month follow-up. The programme focuses on building coping strategies and emotional skills within a peer group of children who share the experience of dyslexia.
