clinical_insight: Children with learning difficulties such as dyslexia are frequently overlooked in mental health provision, yet they experience elevated rates of anxiety, depression, and low self-esteem that often persist into adolescence and adulthood. This study demonstrates that a targeted, nine-week group programme can produce meaningful improvements across multiple domains of functioning. For therapists who work with children or young people, the findings underscore the importance of screening for learning difficulties when a child presents with anxiety or low mood, as the two are closely intertwined and the learning difficulty may be the primary driver of the emotional difficulty. Rather than treating the anxiety in isolation, referring to or replicating structured programmes like Clever Kids that address coping, emotion regulation, and self-esteem in the context of the child's specific learning profile may be more effective. The group format also leverages the normalizing power of shared experience among children with dyslexia, which can itself be therapeutic.
relevant_for: Therapists working with children and young people who have co-occurring learning difficulties and mental health needs, particularly in educational or school-based settings

title: Internet Cognitive Therapy for Prolonged Grief Disorder (iCT-PG): A Developmental Case Series
authors: Smith, Wild, Stonciute, Ehlers
summary: This study evaluated a novel 12-session, therapist-assisted internet cognitive therapy adapted from iCT-PTSD for Prolonged Grief Disorder (iCT-PG). Eight adults meeting PGD criteria completed the program over 14 weeks, targeting loss-related memory characteristics, negative grief appraisals, social disconnection, and unhelpful coping. All participants completed treatment, with 87.5% showing both reliable improvement and recovery below clinical cut-offs. Large reductions were observed in comorbid PTSD, depression, anxiety, and functional impairment, maintained at 3-month follow-up. Average therapist time was approximately 10 hours per participant.
clinical_insight: The adaptation of evidence-based CT for PTSD to target PGD-specific maintenance mechanisms represents a promising approach for a disorder with limited treatment access. The strong recovery rate (87.5%) and low therapist time (10 hours) support the scalability of this format. Clinicians should note that the intervention explicitly targeted cognitive mechanisms (negative grief appraisals, memory characteristics) rather than solely providing supportive counseling. The primary blind spot is the uncontrolled design and tiny sample—these results cannot distinguish treatment effects from natural remission or therapist attention, though the consistency of improvement across multiple outcome domains is encouraging.
relevant_for: Grief therapists and clinicians working with prolonged grief disorder, particularly those seeking scalable digital treatment options

title: "A randomized controlled trial exploring dose-response effects of mindfulness meditation practice on wellbeing"
authors: Nicholas I. Bowles, Nicholas T Van Dam, Jonathan N Davies, Julieta Galante
summary: This well-powered RCT (N=716 meditation-naive adults) examined dose-response effects of daily mindfulness meditation across four assigned durations (4, 10, 20, or 30 minutes per day) over a 28-day online intervention. Primary and secondary outcomes included wellbeing, mindfulness-related constructs, and mental health symptoms. All groups showed significant improvements across outcomes (SMD = 0.29-0.56), but no dose-response effect was found: longer assigned durations did not produce greater benefits. Adherence declined steeply at higher doses, with only 48.5% completing two-thirds of sessions in the 30-minute group compared to 70.9% in the 10-minute group. In exploratory analyses, actual practice engagement, goal confidence, and time predicted outcomes regardless of assigned dose.
clinical_insight: These findings have direct implications for how therapists recommend mindfulness to clients. The common clinical assumption that more practice time equals more benefit is not supported here: within the first 30 days, 4-10 minutes of daily practice produced benefits comparable to 30 minutes, while higher doses led to poorer adherence. For clients who struggle to maintain a meditation practice, therapists can confidently recommend short sessions (5-10 minutes) without worrying that they are offering an insufficient dose. This is particularly relevant for clients with depression or anxiety who may find extended sitting meditation aversive or overwhelming. The strong role of goal confidence in predicting outcomes also suggests that therapists should invest time in building clients' belief that mindfulness will help them, rather than focusing on the duration of practice. The study population was non-clinical, so extrapolation to clinical groups requires caution, but the adherence finding is likely even more important in clinical populations where motivation is often lower.
relevant_for: Therapists who incorporate mindfulness into their clinical work, whether as a standalone intervention or as part of broader protocols such as MBCT, ACT, or DBT

title: Network Analysis on the Efficacy of the Unified Protocol for Transdiagnostic Cognitive Behavioral Therapy of Emotional Disorders
authors: Kashihara et al.
summary: Using multilevel vector autoregressive (ML-VAR) network models, this study analysed time-series data from 123 adult patients (M=37.9 years) with emotional disorders undergoing the Unified Protocol (UP) for transdiagnostic CBT in Japanese trials. Functional impairment nodes (both depressive and anxiety-related) emerged as the most central in the temporal network structure. Non-responders had significantly denser network structures than responders, suggesting that treatment resistance may be characterised by more tightly interconnected symptom-impairment cycles. Between-treatment and end-of-treatment cross-sectional network comparisons showed no significant structural changes, challenging the assumption that treatment simply reduces overall connectivity.
clinical_insight: This study offers the first process-level network analysis of the Unified Protocol in action, and its findings have direct clinical implications. The centrality of functional impairment nodes suggests that clinicians using the UP should monitor functional recovery alongside symptom reduction — impairment may be the engine that maintains the symptom network rather than a mere consequence. The finding that non-responders show denser networks points to a potential early-warning marker: patients whose symptom-impairment connections remain highly interconnected early in treatment may need a modified approach, such as more intensive exposure work or adjunctive interventions. The tension between the null cross-sectional results and the meaningful temporal findings highlights a debate in network psychopathology about whether treatment changes network structure or simply weakens individual node activation. The adult sample (mean age 38) makes findings directly relevant to private practice.
relevant_for: CBT clinicians using the Unified Protocol or transdiagnostic approaches for adult anxiety and depression

title: "A re-analysis of Chiang et al. (2017): Cognitive-behaviour therapy does not have a statistically significant effect on levels of depression or mania in bipolar disorder"
authors: David Smailes, Milo Johns, Peter Moseley
summary: This re-analysis challenges the widely cited Chiang et al. (2017) meta-analysis that concluded CBT is effective for reducing depression and mania in bipolar disorder. Smailes and colleagues identified that Chiang et al.'s conclusions depended partially on a pair of implausibly large effect sizes extracted from a 2010 RCT, and that a second publication of the same RCT reported much more plausible (and smaller) effect sizes. Replacing the implausible values with the corrected ones eliminates the statistically significant effect of CBT on both depression and mania severity. The original meta-analysis has been cited over 200 times and has influenced treatment guidelines.
clinical_insight: This re-analysis is important for any therapist who works with bipolar disorder and has used the Chiang et al. findings to justify CBT as an adjunctive treatment. While CBT for bipolar disorder is widely recommended in clinical guidelines, the evidence base may be weaker than previously believed. This does not mean CBT is useless for bipolar clients, but it does suggest that therapists should be honest about the limitations of the evidence and avoid overstating the expected impact on core mood symptoms. Many of the benefits of CBT in bipolar disorder may lie in domains not captured by symptom severity measures alone, such as medication adherence, early relapse detection, coping self-efficacy, and functional recovery. When working with bipolar clients, therapists may want to focus on these functional outcomes rather than expecting large reductions in depression or mania ratings, and to communicate realistic expectations at the outset of treatment.
relevant_for: Therapists providing CBT or other psychotherapies for bipolar disorder, and those involved in clinical training or guideline development

title: Enhancing the Efficacy of Exposure Therapy: Translation of Pharmacological Augmentation of Fear Extinction
authors: Smits, Kredlow, Monfils, Otto
summary: This critical review examines translational research on pharmacological enhancement of fear extinction, focusing on D-cycloserine (DCS)—the most extensively studied agent. Despite early promise, results across preclinical, human laboratory, and clinical trials have been mixed. The authors identify factors accounting for inconsistent findings including study design differences, subject selection, sample size, and measurement approaches. They argue for a rigorous mechanistic research agenda that distinguishes between acquisition, consolidation, and retrieval of extinction processes as separate targets, and examines the relationship between changes in these processes and clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: The mixed evidence on DCS augmentation of exposure therapy has left many clinicians uncertain about its utility. This review provides a framework for understanding why results have been inconsistent and offers a roadmap for future research. For practicing clinicians, the key takeaway is that pharmacological augmentation of exposure remains experimental rather than ready for routine clinical use. The debate over whether DCS enhances extinction learning, consolidation, or retrieval has direct implications for when and how it should be administered relative to exposure sessions. A significant blind spot: the review largely focuses on DCS, leaving other potentially promising augmentation agents (e.g., MDMA, psilocybin) outside its scope.
relevant_for: Exposure therapists and researchers interested in pharmacological augmentation strategies for anxiety disorder treatment

title: "Gaming Disorder: A qualitative meta-synthesis of related case studies"
authors: Tiina Auranen, Matus Adamkovic, Marcel Martoncik, Solip Park, Joona Ville-Valtteri Kauraoja, Yaewon Jin, Ville Malinen, Mikhail Fiadotau, Sabina Hnatova, Anna Urbancova, Lenka Vargova, Jana Papcunova, Artem Buts, Sunny Choi, Veli-Matti Karhulahti
summary: This qualitative meta-synthesis analyzed 88 published case studies comprising 133 individual cases of gaming-related health problems. Using deductive coding and thematic synthesis, the authors identified 845 distinct problems and 35 comorbidities. The resulting thematic network describes a pathway: a spectrum of vulnerabilities places the individual on a high-stress life trajectory; self-medication through gaming becomes both the coping mechanism and the problem; and prolonged gaming or critical life events lead to treatment-seeking or hospitalization. Most treatment-seeking was driven by social compliance rather than intrinsic motivation. Notably, most case studies did not formally diagnose gaming disorder but instead described instances where gaming intersected with mental health difficulties in varied ways. The authors highlight that many findings align with coping theories of gaming disorder.
clinical_insight: The coping model that emerges from this synthesis should reshape how therapists assess and treat problematic gaming. Rather than treating gaming as the primary pathology, therapists should explore what underlying vulnerabilities and stressors the gaming is helping the individual manage. The finding that most people seek treatment due to external pressure (social compliance) rather than personal concern has direct implications for motivational engagement: many clients will present ambivalent about change, and a directive or confrontational approach is likely to be counterproductive. The sheer variety of presentations across the 133 cases, including many that did not meet formal gaming disorder criteria, suggests that therapists should avoid applying a one-size-fits-all framework and instead conduct a thorough functional analysis of what gaming provides for the specific client. The identification of a vulnerability-stress-coping pathway also implies that successful treatment often requires addressing the original stressors and building alternative coping resources, not just reducing screen time.
relevant_for: Therapists working with gaming disorder, behavioral addictions, or young adults presenting with problematic gaming patterns and comorbid difficulties

title: Anxiety Identity Centrality Is Associated With Avoidant Coping in Anxious Adults
authors: Ahuvia et al.
summary: In a large sample of 1,234 high-anxiety adults, this study examined whether anxiety identity centrality—seeing one’s anxiety as important to one’s self-concept—predicts avoidant coping. Using Bayesian ordered probit regression, anxiety identity centrality showed small-to-medium correlations with situational (r=.42) and experiential (r=.12) avoidance. Crucially, these associations held after controlling for anxiety symptom severity, suggesting that identity centrality contributes to avoidance beyond symptom level alone. The construct was measurable with a single-item ordinal measure.
clinical_insight: This study introduces a clinically relevant construct with direct therapeutic implications: patients who have incorporated anxiety into their identity may resist treatment approaches that could reduce their anxiety, as improvement would threaten their self-concept. Clinicians should consider assessing anxiety identity centrality early in treatment, particularly for patients who seem stuck in avoidant patterns despite adequate symptom-focused intervention. The debate about whether labeling mental health conditions promotes or hinders recovery is given empirical grounding here. A blind spot: the cross-sectional design cannot establish whether anxiety centrality causes avoidance or emerges from chronic anxiety experiences, limiting causal inferences for treatment planning.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT practitioners working with anxious adults who show chronic avoidant coping patterns

title: "Indigenous Perspectives of Trauma-Sensitive Yoga Using Community-Based and Consensual Qualitative Frameworks"
authors: Kelsey Madison Dietrich, Linda Zheng, Ashley Baumann, Jill Kessler, Lyndsey Ducheneaux, Viann N Nguyen-Feng
summary: This community-based study documented the first Indigenous TCTSY-Facilitator Training Cohort's perspectives on Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga (TCTSY). Twenty interviews were conducted with 14 program participants (13 women) who completed the 20-hour training and 1 program mentor, drawn primarily from Great Plains Tribal Nations. Using consensual qualitative research methods, the study found that participants reported positive impacts on their approach to trauma care. Key takeaways included learning about the neurobiological effects of trauma and the value of invitational language (offering choices without direction). Participants described integrating TCTSY with Indigenous healing practices including smudging, land-based practice, and intentional movement within ceremony spaces. Suggested improvements included greater emphasis on yoga history and philosophy, collaboration with Elders, and acknowledgment of ongoing community trauma.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with trauma survivors from Indigenous or other culturally distinct communities, this study illustrates both the potential and the complexity of adapting body-based interventions across cultural contexts. The participants' natural integration of TCTSY with existing Indigenous healing practices, such as smudging and land-based ceremony, demonstrates that trauma-sensitive yoga does not need to replace traditional healing but can complement it. The emphasis on invitational language, a core TCTSY principle, resonates with Indigenous values of autonomy and self-determination, and therapists working cross-culturally will find this alignment useful. The finding that participants valued learning about the neurobiological effects of trauma suggests that psychoeducation about trauma's somatic impact can be a powerful bridge between Western and Indigenous healing frameworks. Therapists should be aware that delivering trauma interventions in communities experiencing ongoing collective trauma requires additional sensitivity and that acknowledging current trauma, rather than treating it as purely historical, is essential for ethical practice.
relevant_for: Therapists working with trauma in Indigenous, refugee, or other culturally distinct communities, and those interested in integrating body-based and somatic approaches with existing cultural healing practices

title: Investigating the Efficacy of Group Schema Therapy on Perfectionism, Alexithymia, and Obsessive-Compulsive Personality Disorder Symptoms
authors: Sobhanifar, Samari
summary: This quasi-experimental study examined the efficacy of 10 sessions of group schema therapy for individuals diagnosed with obsessive-compulsive personality disorder (OCPD; N=30, SCID-5-PD confirmed). Participants were randomly assigned to a schema therapy group or a no-intervention control. Group schema therapy significantly reduced OCPD symptoms and perfectionism (p < 0.05) as measured by the MCMI-III and Hill Perfectionism Questionnaire. However, alexithymia (TAS-20) did not show significant change, suggesting that while schema therapy can modify rigid cognitive-behavioral patterns in OCPD, the affective awareness deficits in this population may require additional or alternative intervention.
clinical_insight: OCPD is notoriously difficult to treat due to patients' rigid personality structure and limited insight into emotional processes. This study provides preliminary evidence that a brief 10-session group schema therapy protocol can reduce perfectionism and OCPD symptomatology — a promising finding given the scarcity of treatment trials for this disorder. The lack of change in alexithymia is clinically important: it suggests therapists should anticipate that cognitive restructuring alone may not unlock emotional awareness in OCPD and should consider integrating emotion-focused techniques such as experiential exercises or chair work. The small sample size and absence of follow-up data are limitations.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with adults with obsessive-compulsive personality disorder in group or individual therapy settings

title: "Too Little, Too Much: Evidence for Quadratic Patterns in Emotion Regulation Strategy Use in Daily Life"
authors: Dominique Maciejewski, Yasemin Erbas, Tak Tsun Lo, Egon Dejonckheere, Merlijn Olthof, Andrea Bunge, Eeske van Roekel
summary: This 60-day experience sampling study (N=79 adults) examined how emotion intensity and perceived controllability predict the use of six emotion regulation strategies (distraction, rumination, cognitive reappraisal, expression, suppression, and relaxation) in daily life. Replicating prior work, expression, relaxation, and rumination increased on days with more intense and uncontrollable emotions. The key novel finding was evidence for quadratic relationships: suppression and cognitive reappraisal were most likely on days with average levels of emotional intensity and controllability, and less likely on both low-intensity and high-intensity days. Additionally, people were more likely to switch to atypical strategies on days where emotions were less or more controllable than usual.
clinical_insight: These findings challenge the common therapeutic assumption that clients should be encouraged to use adaptive regulation strategies like cognitive reappraisal in proportion to emotional difficulty. Instead, reappraisal and suppression appear to be strategies people deploy in a middle zone of emotional activation, not when emotions are most overwhelming. When emotions are either too mild (no need to regulate) or too intense (capacity to reappraise may be overwhelmed), people are less likely to use these cognitive strategies. This has important implications for therapy: teaching cognitive reappraisal skills in session may not translate to use during acute emotional crises, and therapists should consider helping clients develop strategies for the high-intensity end (such as grounding, distraction, or soothing techniques) alongside cognitive approaches. The finding that people switch to atypical strategies when controllability deviates from normal also suggests that therapy should build a diverse repertoire of regulation strategies rather than relying on a primary approach, so clients have options when their usual strategies no longer fit the situation.
relevant_for: Therapists helping clients develop emotion regulation skills, particularly those working with emotional dysregulation in the context of personality disorders, anxiety, or mood difficulties

title: "I’m Not My True Self": A Systematic Review Exploring the Association Between Camouflaging and Self-Related Factors in Autistic Adults
authors: Celeste Andrade, Nallely Cáceres, Amanda Escobar, Catalina Fuentes, Javier Muñoz, Connor Tom Keating, Juan Carlos Oliveros
summary: This systematic review examines the relationship between camouflaging (masking autistic traits in social contexts) and self-related factors in autistic adults, including self-esteem, self-acceptance, and identity coherence. Findings consistently show that higher camouflaging is associated with lower self-esteem, poorer self-acceptance, and a fragmented sense of identity, with mental health outcomes mediating many of these relationships.
clinical_insight: Camouflaging is increasingly recognized as a key mechanism driving mental health deterioration in autistic adults, yet it rarely appears in standard clinical assessments. For therapists, this review supports routinely assessing camouflaging behaviors in autistic clients, particularly those presenting with depression, anxiety, or identity-related distress. The therapeutic implication is that treatment goals should include reducing camouflaging rather than merely managing its consequences. However, a critical blind spot is that most research conceptualizes camouflaging as an individual deficit rather than a response to an inaccessible neurotypical social world, which risks pathologizing adaptive survival strategies. Clinicians should balance validation of the social pressures driving camouflaging with exploration of its personal costs.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with autistic adults in private practice, particularly those seeing late-diagnosed women and non-binary individuals who present with camouflaging-related burnout

title: "Normalization of anticipatory neural activity in PTSD after EMDR psychotherapy"
authors: Raffaele Costanzo, Biancamaria Di Bello, Maria Gabriela Bevacqua, Francesco Di Russo, Francesca Strappini, Valentina Sulpizio, Sabrina Pitzalis
summary: This study investigated the clinical, behavioral, and neurophysiological effects of EMDR psychotherapy in 18 patients with PTSD and 24 matched healthy controls using a discriminative response task during EEG recording at baseline and follow-up. At baseline, PTSD patients showed greater symptom severity, slower and more variable responses, higher error rates, and reduced Bereitschaftspotential (BP) and prefrontal Negativity (pN) amplitudes, which index anticipatory motor preparation and proactive cognitive control. Following EMDR, patients exhibited significant reductions in anxiety, depressive, and dissociative symptoms, with faster, more stable, and more accurate task performance. Critically, BP and pN amplitudes increased after treatment to levels indistinguishable from healthy controls. No changes were observed in the control group.
clinical_insight: This study provides neurophysiological evidence that EMDR does more than reduce subjective distress — it appears to restore anticipatory cognitive control processes that are impaired in PTSD. For EMDR practitioners, this supports explaining to clients that the therapy is not simply about processing traumatic memories but may also be restoring their brain's capacity for proactive planning and response preparation. Clinically, this may help normalize patients' experiences of difficulty with decision-making and anticipatory anxiety, framing these as treatable consequences of PTSD rather than characterological deficits. The finding that neural normalization accompanied clinical improvement also supports the use of EMDR as a first-line treatment where anticipatory impairment is prominent, such as in clients who describe persistent difficulties with planning, initiating tasks, or feeling prepared for daily challenges. The small sample size (N=18) means these neural findings should be considered preliminary but clinically plausible.
relevant_for: EMDR practitioners and trauma therapists working with PTSD clients who present with cognitive control difficulties, anticipatory anxiety, or impairments in daily functioning beyond intrusive symptoms

title: Affective Suppression Fatigue: A Neurocognitive Framework for Emotional Numbing and Reactive Dysregulation
authors: Kim
summary: This paper introduces Affective Suppression Fatigue (ASF), a neurocognitive model proposing that chronic expressive suppression degrades the brain regulatory systems themselves, particularly prefrontal inhibitory control. The model explains two paradoxical outcomes: emotional numbing (affective blunting under low-intensity conditions) and reactive dysregulation (explosive emotional outbursts under high-intensity conditions). By integrating findings on cognitive depletion, burnout, and chronic stress, ASF offers a unified framework for understanding why long-term emotion suppression often backfires, producing the very dysregulation it was intended to prevent.
clinical_insight: Many patients in private practice present with a pattern of emotional constriction punctuated by sudden dysregulation — a clinical presentation that existing models (gross emotion regulation, dialectical behaviour theory) explain only partially. The ASF model gives clinicians a coherent explanatory framework for this common pattern, linking the patient chronic suppression habit to observable neurocognitive degradation. This is particularly useful for psychoeducation with patients who intellectualise emotions or present with alexithymia — explaining that suppression itself damages the regulatory system may motivate engagement with experiential approaches. The framework also bridges CBT, ACT, and psychodynamic perspectives by identifying a shared mechanism. A limitation is that the model is theoretical; empirical validation is needed.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with patients who present with emotional numbing, alexithymia, or reactive dysregulation patterns across mood, trauma, and personality disorder presentations

title: "Transdiagnostic Physiological Correlates of Unpredictable Threat Anticipation"
authors: Hannes Per Carsten, Kai Harpfer, Franziska Magdalena Kausche, Anja Riesel
summary: This transdiagnostic study examined physiological responses during threat anticipation across 163 participants with specific phobia, social anxiety disorder (SAD), OCD, and unaffected controls using the NPU-threat test. Startle reflex, skin conductance responses (SCR), and event-related potentials (N1, P3) were measured during predictable and unpredictable threat conditions. Patients with OCD showed enhanced autonomic responding (SCR) during unpredictable threat anticipation. Dimensionally, anxious arousal was associated with reduced SCR during unpredictable threat and lower P3 amplitudes. Intolerance of uncertainty modulated startle primarily in controls. A latent profile analysis identified three physiological response profiles; a threat-reactive profile (increased N1, SCR, and startle to unpredictable threat) was particularly clinically enriched, with higher severity and greater representation of SAD and OCD.
clinical_insight: This study advances the understanding of how different anxiety disorders share and diverge in their underlying threat physiology, with direct implications for case formulation and treatment planning. The finding that a specific multisystem threat-reactive profile — rather than any single physiological marker — characterizes the most clinically severe presentations supports a personalized approach to anxiety treatment. For therapists, this suggests that asking clients about their subjective experience of unpredictability and threat anticipation (e.g., "Do you feel on edge when you don't know what's coming next?") can provide clinically meaningful information that goes beyond diagnostic labels. The distinct physiological profile of OCD patients during unpredictable threat aligns with clinical observations that intolerance of uncertainty drives much of the suffering in OCD, and supports prioritizing interventions that target uncertainty tolerance, such as exposure-based approaches that deliberately introduce unpredictability. The finding that intolerance of uncertainty modulated startle primarily in controls, not patients, suggests that once a disorder has developed, the relationship between uncertainty and physiological reactivity may have become dysregulated in ways that standard psychoeducation alone cannot address.
relevant_for: Therapists working with anxiety disorders and OCD, particularly those using transdiagnostic approaches or exposure-based therapies that target intolerance of uncertainty

title: Mental Imagery and Its Role in the Psychopathology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Systematic Review and Narrative Synthesis
authors: Zenoni et al.
summary: This systematic review examined the role of intrusive mental imagery in OCD psychopathology and treatment. Up to 89% of OCD patients reported experiencing intrusive imagery, often vivid and accompanied by bodily sensations, with 70-79% relating intrusions to adverse or traumatic events. Compared to other anxiety disorders, intrusive imagery in OCD occurred more frequently and often led patients to believe they possessed a ‘dangerous self.’ Imagery-based treatments including Imagery Rescripting and EMDR showed promise in reducing symptoms, offering an effective alternative for patients whose primary symptoms are imagery-based.
clinical_insight: This review provides strong justification for routinely assessing intrusive imagery in OCD patients, not just intrusive thoughts. The finding that most OCD imagery is trauma-linked suggests overlapping mechanisms with PTSD and supports trauma-informed approaches to OCD treatment. EMDR and Imagery Rescripting emerge as evidence-supported adjuncts for imagery-dominant OCD presentations. A blind spot: the review notes limited high-quality RCT data for imagery-based interventions in OCD, so while promising, these should be offered as complements rather than replacements for first-line treatments like ERP.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating OCD, especially those whose patients report vivid intrusive images or have comorbid trauma histories

title: "A longitudinal qualitative study of facilitators and barriers to care-experienced children's wellbeing during the transition to secondary school"
authors: Eva A Sprecher, Lisa Holmes, Bethan Carter, Havovi Khareghat, John Macleod, Jeongeun Park, Tara Ramsay-Patel, Charlotte Robinson, Katherine Shelton, julie selwyn, Iram Siraj, Rachel Hiller
summary: This longitudinal qualitative study explored barriers and facilitators to wellbeing in 26 care-experienced children and 25 caregivers during the transition from primary to secondary school in England and Wales. Semi-structured interviews were conducted before and after the transition, one year apart, and analyzed using framework-based thematic analysis. Three overarching themes were identified: children's sense of belonging, relationships and emotional support; the extent to which systemic support met individual needs; and children's personal resources including coping strategies and self-perceptions. While some experiences mirrored those of the general population, distinct challenges were identified requiring targeted responses, particularly around relational stability, the impact of placement changes during the transition period, and the need for support systems that account for maltreatment history.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with looked-after children or those with maltreatment histories, this study highlights that school transitions represent a period of heightened vulnerability that extends well beyond the typical challenges of changing schools. The identification of belonging, relational support, and personal coping resources as the three core domains shaping wellbeing provides a clear framework for clinical assessment during transition periods. Therapists should proactively ask about upcoming school transitions during sessions with care-experienced children and consider whether the child's support network is stable enough to buffer the transition. The finding that systemic support often failed to meet individual needs suggests that therapists who work within or alongside social care systems should advocate for transition planning that is tailored to the child's specific relational and emotional needs rather than following generic protocols. The longitudinal design revealing that some children's wellbeing trajectories diverged over the transition year underscores the importance of ongoing monitoring rather than one-off assessments.
relevant_for: Therapists working with looked-after children, foster carers, and adoptive families, particularly around educational transitions and school-based support

title: MDMA-Assisted Therapy for Complex-Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: Toward a Neurocognitive Account
authors: Gerrans et al.
summary: This paper proposes a novel neurocognitive model explaining how MDMA-assisted therapy may specifically benefit individuals with complex PTSD (C-PTSD). Drawing on active inference and self-modelling theories, the authors elucidate the insula’s role in affective regulation and how its dysfunction underpins C-PTSD symptoms including affective dysregulation, negative self-concept, and interpersonal difficulties. The framework suggests MDMA may recalibrate affective regulation and reinforce a more stable self-model, distinguishing its therapeutic potential for C-PTSD from standard PTSD approaches.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with C-PTSD patients should note the proposed distinction between standard PTSD (fear-conditioning based) and C-PTSD (self-model disruption). The insula-centric framework offers a rationale for why C-PTSD patients may benefit from MDMA’s prosocial and emotion-regulating effects beyond simple fear extinction. A notable blind spot: the model is purely theoretical and has not been empirically validated in C-PTSD populations specifically, so clinical application should await trial data.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in trauma therapy, particularly those working with patients who have prolonged interpersonal trauma histories and exhibit disturbances in self-organization

title: "From Retreat to Reality: A Qualitative Study of How Adolescents Apply Mindfulness One Month After a Weeklong Retreat"
authors: Jiaxiong Li, Brian Galla, Michael J. Tumminia
summary: This qualitative study examined how 15 adolescents (mean age 16.74 years) integrated mindfulness into daily life one month after a six-day immersive mindfulness retreat. Using three-cycle coding, the study identified intra-personal applications (self-awareness and regulation reported by 100%, self-compassion and acceptance by 73%, broader perspective by 60%) and inter-personal applications (navigating social anxiety and building connections by 73%, compassionate communication by 67%, social awareness and attunement by 53%). All adolescents described experiences in both domains, suggesting the retreat fostered transferable skills across personal and social contexts. The immersive format contrasted with typical brief school-based programs.
clinical_insight: This study is valuable for therapists who recommend mindfulness to adolescent clients because it provides concrete examples of how young people actually use these skills, rather than assuming transfer from program completion. The finding that all adolescents applied mindfulness both intra-personally and inter-personally challenges the common therapeutic focus on mindfulness as a purely internal regulation tool. For therapists working with socially anxious teens, the finding that 73% used mindfulness to navigate social situations — including reducing social apprehension and engaging in active listening — suggests that mindfulness can be framed specifically as a social skill, which may increase engagement for adolescents who are ambivalent about introspective practices. The retreat format showing strong transfer at one month also raises the question of whether intensity matters more than duration: a six-day immersive experience may consolidate skills more effectively than weeks of brief sessions, which has implications for how therapists structure mindfulness homework and skills practice between sessions.
relevant_for: Therapists incorporating mindfulness into work with adolescents, particularly those presenting with social anxiety or interpersonal difficulties

title: Early Warning Signs of Bipolar Relapse: Investigating Critical Slowing Down in Smartphone Data
authors: Ludwig et al.
summary: This study examined whether smartphone-based digital phenotyping could detect early warning signals (increased autocorrelation and variance) before mood episodes in 29 bipolar disorder patients tracked over one year (10,587 patient-days). Activity-related measures predicted manic episodes, while sleep parameters predicted both manic and depressive transitions. However, even the best combined predictors did not meet predefined clinical utility thresholds on ROC analysis, indicating that while the dynamical systems approach shows promise, current predictive accuracy remains insufficient for clinical deployment.
clinical_insight: While digital phenotyping for bipolar disorder is not yet clinically actionable, this study demonstrates that passive smartphone data captures meaningful signal about impending episodes. Clinicians should be aware that patients increasingly use such tools and may bring self-tracked data into sessions. The honest reporting of below-threshold accuracy is methodologically commendable. The tension between research promise and clinical readiness remains unresolved—future work may benefit from combining digital phenotyping with clinician judgment rather than replacing it.
relevant_for: Clinicians managing bipolar disorder who are interested in digital mental health tools and relapse prevention strategies

title: Transtheoretical components of evidence-based psychological interventions for stimulant use disorders: a systematically developed taxonomy
authors: Chrysanthi Blithikioti, Camilla Cadorin, Marcella Lucente, Lorena Pizzocri, Giuliano Tomei, Matt Field, Patricia Logullo, Wolfgang Lutz, Toshi A. Furukawa, Ioana Cristea
summary: This study extracted and categorized the active components of 109 psychological intervention arms from RCTs targeting stimulant (cocaine and/or amphetamine) use disorder, drawn from a network meta-analysis of 50 RCTs plus 10 additional RCTs identified in a search update. A total of 1,592 distinct intervention elements were identified from full intervention descriptions (protocols, manuals), then consolidated into 25 transtheoretical components. These components were further organized into 40 unique intervention profiles (distinct combinations of components). The four most frequently employed components were relapse prevention (present in 34 of 40 profiles), functional analysis (32), skill generalization (29), and therapeutic alliance (29). The least frequent were mindfulness and meditation (3 profiles) and subpopulation issues (4). Inter-rater reliability was assessed via Cohen's kappa on a random 35% subset: two components showed fair agreement (κ=0.20-0.40), four showed moderate agreement (κ=0.41-0.60), and eleven showed substantial agreement (κ>0.60). The taxonomy was developed through systematic extraction, iterative consensus coding, and refined in a 2.5-day expert meeting.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with stimulant use disorders, this taxonomy provides a concrete, evidence-based component checklist that goes beyond brand-name protocols. The dominance of relapse prevention (85% of profiles), functional analysis (80%), skill generalization (72.5%), and therapeutic alliance (72.5%) identifies a de facto core package for stimulant use — therapists should verify these four components are present regardless of the manual they are using. The relative rarity of mindfulness (7.5%) and subpopulation-specific components (10%) is a notable counterpoint to trends in other areas of mental health, suggesting that for stimulant users specifically, behavioral and skill-based components drive change rather than contemplative or identity-focused approaches. The therapeutic alliance appearing in nearly three-quarters of profiles reinforces that in addiction treatment, where dropout and disengagement are primary barriers, the relationship is not supplementary but central. Practically, therapists can use this taxonomy as a component-selection tool: rather than asking "Which manual?", ask "Which combination of these 25 components does this specific client need?" — for example, a client with strong social reinforcement for use may need more functional analysis and skill generalization, while one with poor self-efficacy may need more psychoeducation and coping skills training.
relevant_for: Therapists treating stimulant use disorders, addiction counselors designing individualized treatment plans, clinical supervisors ensuring core components are delivered

title: Single-Session Interventions for Mental Health: Umbrella Review
authors: Schleider et al.
summary: First umbrella review of SSIs synthesizing 24 systematic reviews (415 unique trials) across anxiety, depression, externalizing, eating, and substance use problems. SSIs showed positive overall effect (SMD=-0.25), with 83% of reviews reporting significant effects.
clinical_insight: SSIs are a pragmatic tool for private practitioners to offer meaningful change in single contact, especially for waitlisted patients. Implementation research is the critical next step to determine which SSIs work in routine settings. The review does not address which formats work best for specific disorders.
relevant_for: Private practice clinicians and service managers maximizing impact of brief or single-visit interventions

title: Breaking the Cycle: Rewiring Negative Symptoms in Schizophrenia Through Physical Exercise
authors: Benedicte Thonon, Damien Brevers
summary: This narrative review (UCLouvain) proposes an integrated neurocognitive framework explaining why physical exercise can reduce negative symptoms of schizophrenia while the symptoms themselves impede exercise engagement. The review synthesizes evidence across four domains: (1) exercise's effects on negative symptoms — meta-analyses report small effects for aerobic interventions (Sabe et al., SMD=-0.20, 95% CI -0.37 to -0.03), medium effects across broader exercise modalities (Kim et al., g=-0.51, 95% CI -0.72 to -0.31), and medium-to-large effects via multilevel meta-analysis (Gallardo-Gomez et al., g=0.65, 95% CI 0.53-0.78); (2) evidence that negative symptoms reduce physical activity engagement, including digital phenotyping data (Yang et al., 2025) showing avolition and anhedonia strongly associated with reduced step counts and GPS-derived distance traveled in 99 recently discharged patients; (3) subjective exercise experience in schizophrenia, including findings from Duncan et al. (2016) that moderate-intensity walking increased perceived exertion during the task while pleasure improved only after completion, and Décombe et al. (2020) showing individuals with schizophrenia report higher perceived effort overall than healthy controls even at low physical demands; and (4) a novel learning-based framework in which exercise generates prediction errors — discrepancies between anticipated and experienced effort/pleasure — that can update maladaptive internal models. The framework draws on evidence of preserved consummatory pleasure but impaired anticipatory pleasure, effort-based decision-making deficits (transdiagnostic meta-analysis g=-0.40; Pillny et al., 2026), altered reinforcement learning, and blunted prediction-error signaling in frontostriatal circuits in schizophrenia.
clinical_insight: This review offers a specific, mechanism-based approach to prescribing exercise for patients with schizophrenia that goes well beyond the standard advice to "exercise more." The key clinical protocol derived from the framework has several concrete steps: (1) Before each exercise session, ask the patient to explicitly predict how effortful, unpleasant, or tiring the activity will be on a rating scale — this makes the prediction error salient and encodeable. (2) Start with activities that are genuinely expected to be easy (generating positive prediction errors where exercise feels less effortful than expected), drawing on the finding that lower-than-expected exertion increases pleasure (Brevers et al., 2024). (3) After each session, compare predictions with actual experience and explicitly discuss the discrepancy. This is critical because prediction error processing is altered in schizophrenia (attenuated neural responses in ventral striatum during reward anticipation), so positive experiences will not automatically update beliefs. (4) When exercise is harder than expected (negative prediction errors), reframe the experience as evidence of mastery and tolerance ("this was difficult, but I managed it") rather than confirming that exercise is aversive. (5) Focus on immediate rewards (calm, accomplishment, social connection) rather than delayed health benefits, given that individuals with schizophrenia show steeper delay discounting. (6) Consider aerobic over resistance training initially, as Korman et al. (2026) found that fatigue decreased after aerobic exercise but increased after resistance training in patients with psychotic disorders. This approach can be integrated into existing CBT for psychosis or recovery-oriented practice, and mirrors behavioral activation techniques used for anhedonia in depression.
relevant_for: Psychologists and mental health workers supporting people with schizophrenia-spectrum disorders, psychiatric rehabilitation specialists, CBT for psychosis practitioners, exercise physiologists working in mental health settings

title: Qualitative Analysis of a Patient Experience of Ketamine-Assisted Psychotherapy for Substance Use Disorder: Empirical Synergies with Twelve-Step Programs
authors: Petrovitch et al.
summary: This case report details a 28-year-old woman treated for alcohol and prescription opioid use disorder using an inter-clinic collaborative treatment plan combining ketamine-assisted psychotherapy (KAP) with existing 12-step-based psychosocial treatments. Qualitative analysis of the patient lived experience revealed that ketamine-induced experiences meaningfully overlapped with 12-step concepts such as powerlessness, spiritual awakening, and moral inventory. The authors detail specific clinical practices for managing the unique risks of using ketamine — itself a substance with recreational potential — as a treatment for SUD, and highlight the synergies between psychedelic experiences and 12-step recovery frameworks.
clinical_insight: As ketamine clinics expand rapidly, the question of how to integrate KAP with established addiction treatment frameworks like 12-step programs is increasingly urgent. This case report provides a practical clinical roadmap: rather than positioning KAP as an alternative to 12-step work, the authors demonstrate how ketamine experiences can deepen engagement with recovery concepts such as surrender, spiritual experience, and self-examination. For addiction clinicians, the detailed risk-management practices for using a psychoactive substance to treat substance use disorder are particularly valuable. The case highlights a tension in the field: patients may attribute recovery to the ketamine experience itself, while clinicians may see it as a catalyst for engagement with existing therapeutic frameworks. A limitation is the single-case design and the specific cultural context of 12-step programs, which may not generalise to all treatment approaches.
relevant_for: Addiction clinicians integrating ketamine-assisted psychotherapy with 12-step or other structured psychosocial treatment programs

title: Social disconnection, depression, and anxiety during parental leave among Slovak women
authors: Hana Celusakova, Petra Brandoburova, Simona Krakovska, Michal Hajduk
summary: Cross-sectional online survey of 410 Slovak biological mothers on maternity/parental leave (M age=33.4, SD=4.61; 88.3% living with a partner; 83.7% university-educated; mean leave duration=34.9 months, SD=28.0, median=27.5, range=1-176 months), recruited October 2025-January 2026 via social media, pediatricians, and maternity centers. Depression was assessed with PHQ-9 and EPDS, anxiety with GAD-7, loneliness with UCLA-3 (3-item), and social isolation with LSNS-6. Prevalence of moderately severe-to-severe depression was 38.3% (PHQ-9 >=10) and 36.6% (EPDS >=13), with strong agreement between measures (McNemar p=.419). Anxiety prevalence was 28.3% (GAD-7 >=10), with 23.2% meeting criteria for both depression and anxiety. Elevated loneliness affected 78.3% (UCLA-3 >=6), while 26.8% were at risk of social isolation (LSNS-6 <12), and 23.6% met criteria for both. Direct Likert measures showed 39.3% felt lonely "sometimes" and 24.1% "most of the time," while only 8.8% reported never feeling lonely. Two multiple linear regression models (controlling for age, SES, education, number of children, and leave length) found anxiety was the strongest predictor of depressive symptoms (PHQ-9: beta=.701, p<.001; EPDS: beta=.647, p<.001), followed by loneliness (PHQ-9: beta=.135, p<.001; EPDS: beta=.173, p<.001). Social isolation showed a small negative association with EPDS (beta=-.111, p<.001). Models explained 61.8% (PHQ-9) and 60.5% (EPDS) of variance. Length of parental leave was not a significant predictor in either model (PHQ-9: beta=-.036; EPDS: beta=.039), nor were number of children or SES.
clinical_insight: The 38.3% rate of moderate-to-severe depression among Slovak mothers on parental leave is more than triple the typical postpartum prevalence (~12%) and substantially higher than early postpartum rates previously reported in Slovakia (6.1% shortly after childbirth, 11.6% at 6-8 weeks; Skodova et al., 2021), suggesting that risk accumulates over the course of extended leave rather than being confined to the early postpartum period. For clinicians, several specific implications follow. First, the finding that 78.3% reported elevated loneliness — nearly four in five mothers — indicates that social disconnection during parental leave is normative, not exceptional, and should be proactively assessed rather than waiting for clients to report it. Second, anxiety (beta=.701) was by far the strongest predictor of depression, suggesting that treating comorbid anxiety may be the most efficient route to reducing depressive symptoms in this population. Third, the irrelevance of leave duration to depression (beta=-.036, nonsignificant) directly challenges the assumption that extended leave is protective — in Slovakia, where leave can extend to three years, longer leave does not reduce and may increase isolation risk. Therapists working in systems with extended leave policies should screen for depression and loneliness throughout the entire leave period, not just in the first few months. Fourth, the negative association between social isolation (LSNS-6) and EPDS scores (beta=-.111), alongside the positive association of subjective loneliness (UCLA-3), indicates that perceived loneliness is the more clinically relevant target than objective social network size — mothers can have adequate social networks and still feel lonely, and it is the subjective experience that drives depression.
relevant_for: Perinatal mental health therapists, those working with postpartum depression and anxiety, practitioners in healthcare systems with extended parental leave policies (especially Central/Eastern European contexts), maternal-child health professionals conducting routine screening

title: The Reciprocal Relationship Between Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Symptoms and Alcohol Use in a Large Multisite Longitudinal Sample Stratified by Sex
authors: Hinojosa et al.
summary: A large multisite longitudinal study (n=2942, 62% female) examining bidirectional temporal relationships between PTSD symptoms and alcohol use over six months following trauma exposure, using random-intercept cross-lagged panel models stratified by sex. Males and females exhibit complex temporal development patterns aligning with the mutual maintenance hypothesis in men but the susceptibility hypothesis in women.
clinical_insight: The sex-stratified design is particularly valuable for clinicians, as it reveals differential pathways to comorbid PTSD-AUD presentations in men versus women, informing gender-sensitive treatment planning. Within-person fluctuations in PTSD symptoms predicted subsequent alcohol use between 3-6 months post-trauma, supporting targeted timing of interventions. The naturalistic longitudinal design across 29 emergency departments increases ecological validity.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating comorbid PTSD and alcohol use disorder, particularly those working in trauma-informed addiction services

title: "Context Matters, Doesn't It? The Role of Context in Everyday Emotion Regulation Strategy Use"
authors: Anh Tran, Rachel Freeman-Robinson, Peter Koval, Katharine Greenaway, Elise Kalokerinos
summary: This integrative analysis combined 11 experience-sampling datasets (N=1,720 individuals, 134,470 observations) to examine how emotion regulation strategy use varies with three contextual features: emotional intensity, perceived controllability, and social context (presence and closeness of others). Against theoretical expectations, the study found limited evidence that context meaningfully drives daily strategy selection. The well-powered design allowed detection of even small effects, suggesting that the strategy-situation fit framework, while theoretically appealing, may not operate as strongly in daily life as laboratory studies suggest.
clinical_insight: This large-scale finding challenges a core assumption underlying much emotion regulation therapy: that teaching clients to match strategies to situations is the primary path to better outcomes. If context drives strategy use less than assumed, therapists may need to reconsider the emphasis on strategy-situation matching in protocols like DBT and CBT-E. Instead, the findings suggest that individual differences in strategy habits and preferences may be more important than contextual fit. For therapists, this means that helping clients identify and practice a personal repertoire of go-to strategies — rather than teaching complex decision rules about which strategy fits which situation — may be more aligned with how regulation actually works in daily life. This does not invalidate the value of teaching multiple strategies, but it does suggest that the therapeutic rationale should focus on building automaticity and personal fit rather than situational analysis. The study also implies that when a client repeatedly uses the same strategy across situations, this may not be a deficit requiring correction but a normal and potentially adaptive pattern.
relevant_for: Therapists teaching emotion regulation skills across modalities (CBT, DBT, ACT), particularly those working with clients who struggle to apply different strategies in different contexts

title: When Deteriorators Report Improvement in Psychotherapy: A Qualitative Analysis
authors: Ghelfi et al.
summary: A qualitative analysis of psychotherapy clients who deteriorated on the OQ-45 but reported subjective improvement (paradoxical outcomes). Of 106 deteriorators, 58.5% believed they had improved. Interviews revealed increased insight, learned coping strategies, interpersonal improvements, and symptom reduction. Discrepancy explanations included extratherapeutic factors, changes in questionnaire interaction, undetected progress, and hindsight bias.
clinical_insight: This study has significant implications for routine outcome monitoring (ROM) programs where deteriorating scores trigger clinical alarms. The paradoxical outcome phenomenon suggests some patients experience meaningful subjective improvement even as standardized measures indicate worsening, potentially reflecting increased awareness or therapeutic deepening. Clinicians should interpret deteriorating OQ-45 scores with nuance, exploring the patient's subjective experience before adjusting treatment.
relevant_for: Clinicians using routine outcome monitoring and those supervising trainees in counseling center settings

title: Assessing chronic pain processes longitudinally: Psychometric evaluation of the German Assessing Chronic Pain Processes (ProCEss) item set
authors: Saskia Scholten, Verena Eunike Hofmann, Felicitas Kininger, Philipp R. S. Wacker
summary: This study developed and psychometrically evaluated a German Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) item set for assessing psychological chronic pain processes. Across three phases—pilot testing (3 people with chronic pain), main evaluation (n=68, 6 assessments/day for 12 days, 4,236 datapoints), and preliminary re-evaluation (n=10, 6 assessments/day for 21 days)—the researchers examined variability and reliability of items capturing clinically relevant pain processes. Most items and process scales met predefined criteria, though four of thirteen scales showed insufficient reliability. Variance was largely attributable to between-person differences. The study presents the first psychometrically evaluated German EMA item set for chronic pain processes.
clinical_insight: This paper offers therapists working with chronic pain patients a validated tool for tracking pain processes in real-time between sessions. EMA-based assessment can capture within-person fluctuations that retrospective questionnaires miss, enabling more personalized treatment monitoring. Therapists can use insights about day-level patterns (e.g., catastrophizing, pain-related fear, disability) to tailor interventions and track progress more precisely. The finding that variance is mostly between-person underscores the importance of individualized rather than one-size-fits-all approaches to chronic pain treatment.
relevant_for:
- Pain psychologists and therapists working with chronic pain populations
- Clinicians using EMA or experience sampling methods for monitoring treatment progress
- Researchers developing assessment tools for chronic pain
- Clinical psychologists interested in within-person process measurement

title: Intervention-Induced Neuroticism Change Across Multiple Timescales
authors: Amanda Jo Wright, Peter Haehner, Chris Hopwood, R. Chris Fraley, Wiebke Bleidorn
summary: Using data from the Changing How I Live Life Study (N=444), which evaluated a digital intervention targeting neuroticism reduction, this study examined intervention-induced personality change across three timescales: momentary, daily, and weekly. Neuroticism changed nonlinearly across the intervention period, with changes at each timescale reliably predicting one another. Bottom-up effects (momentary to daily to weekly) were often larger in magnitude than top-down effects. Intervention groups showed larger mean-level changes and coupled changes across timescales compared to the active control group. Results support both bottom-up and top-down change processes in personality modification.
clinical_insight: This study has important implications for therapists working with clients high in neuroticism. It provides evidence that neuroticism is modifiable through intervention and demonstrates the mechanistic pathways: momentary state changes accumulate into lasting trait change. This supports therapeutic approaches that target daily emotional experiences (e.g., emotion regulation skills, CBT techniques, mindfulness) as pathways to durable personality-level change. The finding that bottom-up effects dominate suggests therapists should prioritize helping clients change moment-to-moment responses rather than focusing solely on trait-level restructuring.
relevant_for:
- Therapists working with clients presenting high neuroticism or emotional instability
- Clinicians interested in personality change mechanisms
- Practitioners using digital or mobile interventions for mental health
- Researchers studying therapeutic mechanisms of personality change

title: Daily fluctuations in delay discounting and smartphone-based multimodal measures track nighttime drinking in alcohol use disorder: A 28-day monitoring study
authors: Hyeonmin Lee
summary: This 28-day longitudinal study (N=143 adults with DSM-5 AUD) used smartphone-based multimodal monitoring to predict same-day nighttime drinking. Daily assessments covered three domains: app-based self-reports (mood, craving, meal intake, sleep), computational tasks (delay discounting, risk preference, ambiguity tolerance), and passive smartphone sensing (time at home, calls, steps). A multimodal model showed highest explanatory power (marginal R-squared=0.22), with within-person increases in delay discounting, alcohol craving, and positive mood, plus lower meal intake and less time at home, all associated with greater likelihood of nighttime drinking. Crucially, within-person delay discounting fluctuations remained predictive after accounting for other measures.
clinical_insight: For therapists treating alcohol use disorder, this study highlights the value of monitoring cognitive markers alongside self-reported states. Delay discounting (preference for immediate over delayed rewards) is not just a stable trait but fluctuates day-to-day and predicts drinking behavior within the same day. Clinicians could help clients recognize their personal high-risk patterns—days when they are more impulsive, crave alcohol more, or spend less time at home—and develop targeted coping strategies. The multimodal approach suggests that combining cognitive, emotional, and behavioral monitoring provides the most complete risk picture for relapse prevention.
relevant_for:
- Addiction counselors and therapists treating alcohol use disorder
- Clinicians interested in ambulatory assessment and digital monitoring for substance use
- Therapists incorporating behavioral economics concepts (delay discounting) into treatment
- Practitioners working on relapse prevention and harm reduction

title: Sensitive periods for childhood adversity and their impact on later life outcomes: A narrative review
authors: Chris Patrick Pflanz, John Gallacher, Sarah Bauermeister
summary: This narrative review scopes research on sensitive periods during which children are particularly vulnerable to abuse and its long-term consequences. Three themes emerged: (1) earlier adversity tends to produce worse outcomes, (2) two sensitive periods correspond to major developmental transitions—early childhood and early adolescence, and (3) sensitive periods are domain-specific, meaning the timing of abuse affects different aspects of psychosocial functioning differently. The authors caution that findings are largely study-specific, and more prospective longitudinal research is needed. They emphasize that timing of child abuse is clinically relevant for optimizing and personalizing treatment.
clinical_insight: For therapists conducting trauma-informed assessments and case formulations, understanding when adversity occurred can meaningfully inform treatment planning. Abuse during early childhood may disrupt attachment and emotional regulation foundations, while adversity during early adolescence may impact identity formation and social-cognitive development. This review supports the clinical practice of carefully mapping the timing and nature of adverse experiences when formulating cases. Therapists can use this knowledge to tailor interventions—for example, prioritizing attachment-focused work for early childhood trauma versus identity and social-processing interventions for early adolescent trauma.
relevant_for:
- Trauma therapists and counselors working with adult survivors of childhood abuse
- Clinicians conducting trauma-informed assessments and case formulations
- Therapists working with developmental trauma and complex PTSD
- Practitioners interested in the developmental timing of psychological interventions

title: Resilience across pandemic phases: distinguishing psychosocial correlates from stress-buffering effects in youth
authors: Lianne P. de Vries, Imme Rahmon, Elske Marra, Tinca JC Polderman, Meike Bartels
summary: This large-scale study examined youth resilience across three phases of the COVID-19 pandemic using Dutch population-based samples (pre-pandemic N=12,937; during N=8,934; post-pandemic N=37,797; ages 12-25). Higher optimism, lower loneliness, and better self-rated health were consistently associated with resilient functioning (better-than-expected mental health given stress exposure) across all phases. However, moderation analyses showed inconsistent evidence that these characteristics buffered the impact of stress on mental health. The findings support resilience as a complex, context-dependent process rather than the result of single protective factors.
clinical_insight: This study challenges a common therapeutic assumption that building protective factors like optimism or social connection will directly buffer clients against stress. For therapists working with young people, the message is nuanced: fostering psychosocial resources is associated with better resilient functioning, but these resources do not reliably moderate the effect of stress itself. Clinicians should set realistic expectations when building resilience—it helps young people function better overall but may not make them immune to stress impacts. The large sample and multi-wave design make these findings particularly robust for clinical guidance.
relevant_for:
- Child and adolescent therapists
- School counselors and youth mental health workers
- Clinicians working on resilience-building interventions with young people
- Therapists treating pandemic-related mental health impacts in youth

title: Depressive Symptom Trajectories Around the Retirement Transition Across Countries and Historical Periods
authors: Yufei Zhang, Zhihao Ma
summary: Using longitudinal data from four large aging cohorts (HRS, SHARE, CHARLS, KLoSA) across the US, Europe, China, and South Korea, this study examined depressive symptom trajectories around the retirement transition. Results showed that changes in depression around retirement were generally modest at the group level, but patterns varied by country and historical period. The US showed pre-retirement increases and an immediate rise at retirement, followed by post-retirement decline. Europe showed a small immediate decrease, China showed steady increases, and South Korea showed an immediate increase followed by decline. Period-specific analyses revealed further variation.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with older adults, this study provides valuable context for understanding how retirement may affect mental health. Rather than assuming a uniform effect, clinicians should assess individual factors including cultural context, pre-retirement mental health, and the specific circumstances of the transition. The finding that changes are generally modest at the group level suggests that retirement itself is not inherently depressive or protective—it is the personal meaning, preparation, and social context that matter. Therapists can use this evidence to normalize clients' experiences and provide psychoeducation that retirement-related mood changes vary widely and are often manageable.
relevant_for:
- Therapists working with older adults and retirees
- Clinicians addressing late-life depression
- Counselors helping clients navigate life transitions
- Practitioners in culturally diverse settings serving aging populations

title: Can a yoga psychology intervention change primal world beliefs?
authors: Sulakshana Kashyap, Jyotsna Agrawal, Shivarama Varambally
summary: This exploratory study examined whether a 12-session yoga psychology intervention could shift primal world beliefs (fundamental assumptions about the world) and improve well-being in adults with mild-to-moderate depression (N=31). Using a single-group pre-post design, results showed significant improvements in well-being across multiple measures (all p<.001), increases in positive primal beliefs (Safe, Enticing, Alive, Good), changes in Triguna (increased sattva, reduced rajas and tamas), and decreased depressive symptoms. Informant ratings paralleled self-reports, suggesting observable change.
clinical_insight: This study introduces an interesting therapeutic angle: addressing fundamental world beliefs through yoga-based interventions. For therapists, this suggests that helping clients shift deeply held beliefs about the world (e.g., from seeing it as dangerous to safe) may accompany broader improvements in well-being. While the single-group design limits causal inference, the convergence of self-report and informant data adds credibility. Therapists could incorporate elements of yoga psychology or body-based interventions when working with depressed clients who hold deeply negative world assumptions, particularly as a complement to evidence-based approaches like CBT.
relevant_for:
- Therapists working with mild-to-moderate depression
- Clinicians interested in integrative or mind-body approaches
- Practitioners incorporating yoga or somatic interventions
- Therapists addressing maladaptive core beliefs about the world

title: Using features of dynamic networks to guide treatment selection and outcome prediction: The central role of uncertainty
authors: Bjorn S. Siepe, Matthias Kloft, Fridtjof Petersen, Laurenz L. Muller, Maarten Marsman, Denny Borsboom, Lourens J. Waldorp, Eiko I. Fried
summary: This study introduces a Bayesian multilevel vector autoregressive model (BmlVAR) for estimating person-specific dynamic network features with uncertainty quantification, and applies it to treatment selection and outcome prediction. In a preregistered simulation study comparing several network estimation methods, results showed that all methods performed mediocrely in estimating centrality measures in practically relevant settings. BmlVAR outperformed other methods in detecting associations with outcomes, but all methods required substantial data or very large effects. The study cautions that centrality-based treatment selection from dynamic networks is unlikely to work well with typical sample sizes.
clinical_insight: This is an important methodological cautionary paper for therapists and clinical researchers who have embraced network analysis for treatment planning. The finding that network centrality measures require impractically large amounts of data to reliably guide treatment selection challenges a growing trend in personalized medicine. For clinicians, this means that identifying "central" symptoms in an individual's network and targeting them therapeutically may not be as straightforward as some have suggested. Therapists should be cautious about over-interpreting network-based assessment tools and continue relying on comprehensive clinical judgment alongside any network-informed insights.
relevant_for:
- Clinical psychologists using network analysis for case formulation
- Researchers developing personalized treatment selection methods
- Clinicians interested in evidence-based assessment methodology
- Therapists working with symptom networks in complex presentations

title: Lost in aggregation: from score-based to representational mentalization
authors: Serena Maria Stagnitto, Celia Martinez Tomas, Serena Lecce, Jose Antonio Hinojosa, Daniele Gatti
summary: This study investigated whether mentalization (the ability to infer others' mental states) is better characterized through vector-based response representations than classical aggregate scores. 164 university participants completed the IRI, RMET, and Triangles task. Classical score correlations showed limited convergence across tasks. Representational similarity analyses showed vector-based representations reliably converged with classical scores within tasks, but cross-task analyses revealed that score-based and vector-based representations produced partly different prediction patterns. The findings suggest that how mentalization is measured (aggregate scores vs. full response configurations) organizes individual differences differently.
clinical_insight: For therapists who use mentalization-based treatment (MBT), this research highlights that standard mentalization assessments may miss important nuances in how clients process social information. Two clients with the same total score on a mentalization measure may have very different response patterns, reflecting different underlying cognitive strategies. This supports clinical approaches that examine not just whether a client can mentalize, but how they mentalize—the specific patterns of their social-cognitive processing. Clinicians should consider going beyond total scores to understand qualitative differences in clients' mentalization profiles.
relevant_for:
- Therapists practicing mentalization-based treatment (MBT)
- Clinicians assessing social cognition and theory of mind
- Practitioners working with personality disorders where mentalization deficits are prominent
- Clinical psychologists developing or refining social-cognitive assessments

title: Already Outdated and Still Under Review: Mapping the Landscape of Research on Adolescent Development and AI Chatbot Use
authors: Anne J. Maheux
summary: This mixed-methods study gathered developmental expert perspectives (survey n=141, interviews n=15) to map the research landscape on adolescent AI chatbot use and inform a coordinated research and policy agenda. Experts prioritized studying use cases with severe or irreversible immediate harms, AI literacy, AI as a social/relational actor, AI-generated misinformation, and overreliance. Key barriers included the rapid pace of AI change, limited industry transparency, and funding gaps. Experts recommended three immediate guardrails: enforceable youth-centered regulation, developmentally informed product design, and multidimensional AI literacy, emphasizing that responsibility for safe AI should not rest primarily on adolescents and families.
clinical_insight: Therapists working with adolescents increasingly encounter clients whose emotional and social lives are intertwined with AI chatbots. This study provides a framework for understanding the clinical landscape: teens may form parasocial relationships with AI, be influenced by AI-generated content, or develop overreliance on AI for emotional support. Clinicians should assess clients' AI chatbot use as part of a comprehensive social history, particularly for vulnerable youth. The expert recommendation that responsibility should not rest solely on families aligns with a systemic therapeutic perspective—therapists can advocate for environmental changes rather than placing the burden on individual clients.
relevant_for:
- Child and adolescent therapists encountering AI-related issues in practice
- School counselors monitoring AI's impact on student wellbeing
- Clinicians working with youth social media and technology use
- Family therapists addressing technology-related family dynamics

title: Forensic Outpatient Cognitive Understanding Study (FOCUS): A study protocol
authors: Juliette Hutten, Hilde Geurts, Tim Ziermans, joan van horn
summary: The FOCUS study protocol describes a design to map cognitive functioning in forensic outpatients (n=100) compared to a matched normative group (n=100). Participants complete a comprehensive neuropsychological battery covering seven domains: intelligence, attention, language, executive functioning, social cognition, memory, and decision making. Aggression is assessed via self-report (RPQ) and behavioral task (CRTT). The study aims to: (1) compare cognitive profiles between forensic outpatients and matched controls, (2) examine correspondence between self-reported and task-based aggression, (3) examine associations between neuropsychological scores and aggression, and (4) examine associations between neuropsychological scores and treatment outcomes including recidivism risk, treatment non-attendance, dropout, and actual recidivism.
clinical_insight: This protocol is directly relevant for therapists working in forensic settings. It applies the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) framework by examining how cognitive functioning maps onto treatment-relevant outcomes like dropout and recidivism. If cognitive deficits in specific domains predict treatment dropout or non-response, therapists could use cognitive screening to identify clients who need adapted treatment approaches. The comprehensive multi-domain assessment approach also models thorough cognitive case formulation in forensic populations, going beyond single-test evaluations.
relevant_for:
- Forensic and correctional therapists
- Clinicians conducting cognitive assessments in forensic settings
- Therapists working with antisocial behavior and aggression
- Clinical psychologists interested in the RNR framework and treatment planning

title: Compassion; A Double-Edged Sword in Psychotherapy and the Shield of Self-Care
authors: Khushee (Kay) Prashar
summary: Examines the paradoxical role of compassion in psychotherapy, arguing that while compassion is a core therapeutic mechanism, therapists' compassionate engagement without adequate self-care boundaries can lead to empathic distress, burnout, and reduced clinical effectiveness, necessitating structured self-compassion practices.
