clinical_insight: Therapists should proactively monitor their own compassion fatigue symptoms and implement structured self-care, peer consultation, and supervision practices. This review identifies evidence-based prevention strategies that can be woven into routine professional development to sustain clinical effectiveness over time.
relevant_for: Mental health professionals working in trauma-focused, palliative, and high-caseload settings

title: Too anxious to learn: Induced state anxiety prevents the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia
authors: Daryna Rubanets, Przemyslaw Babel, Elzbieta A. Bajcar
summary: This study (N=120 healthy volunteers) investigated how pre-existing anxiety affects the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia through classical conditioning. Participants were randomized to three groups: anxiety induction plus nocebo conditioning (ANC), nocebo conditioning only (NC), and control. Contrary to the theoretical prediction that anxiety would enhance nocebo effects, pretreatment anxiety actually disrupted the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia. The NC group showed expected nocebo effects and elevated pain expectancies, but the ANC group did not develop nocebo hyperalgesia despite the same conditioning procedure. Neither pain expectancies nor post-treatment anxiety mediated the effect.
clinical_insight: This finding has a counterintuitive but clinically important implication for pain therapists: patients who are highly anxious before treatment may not develop the negative pain expectations that typically impair recovery. This challenges the common assumption that anxiety always worsens pain outcomes. However, the anxiety used here was anticipatory shock anxiety rather than pain-related anxiety, so the application requires careful thought. Therapists should consider that the relationship between anxiety and pain response is more complex than a simple linear model, and that a patient's emotional state at the time of initial treatment experiences may shape subsequent pain expectations in non-obvious ways.
relevant_for:
- Pain psychologists and therapists working with chronic pain
- Clinicians using psychoeducation about pain expectations
- Therapists interested in nocebo effects and treatment responses
- Health psychologists working in medical settings

title: LGBTQ+ Ayahuasca Retreat Experience is Associated with Benefits to Mental Health, Quality of Life, and Spiritual Well-being: A Prospective, Naturalistic Study
authors: Matthew X Lowe, Quinn A. Darby, Sasha Kalcheff-Korn, Heather Jackson
summary: Prospective naturalistic study examining mental health, quality of life, and spiritual well-being outcomes in LGBTQ+ individuals attending ayahuasca retreats, measuring changes from pre- to post-retreat and at follow-up while documenting both therapeutic benefits and adverse experiences in this population.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with LGBTQ+ clients exploring psychedelic-assisted therapy should attend to how identity-related trauma and minority stress shape the therapeutic experience. Cultural safety, intersectional attunement, and integration support appear critical for positive outcomes in this population.
relevant_for: Psychedelic-assisted therapy facilitators and integration therapists working with LGBTQ+ populations

title: The impact of cash out availability on online betting behavior
authors: Barnabas Szaszi, Wei Yue Hung, Peter Szecsi, Pal Kolumban, Marton Kolozsvari, Miklos Bognar, Arife Yesiloz, Mark Griffiths, Zsolt Demetrovics
summary: This registered report (N=595 Hungarian online gamblers) experimentally tested whether the availability of a cash-out feature in online sports betting causally influences gambling behavior. Participants were randomized to conditions where cash-out was either available or unavailable. Contrary to correlational literature suggesting cash-outs intensify problematic gambling, the experiment found no effect of cash-out availability on bet placement, wager size, or bet risk. However, the authors note that low variance in outcomes may have limited detection of subtle effects, so the null results should not be taken as conclusive evidence of no behavioral impact.
clinical_insight: For therapists treating gambling disorder, this study provides important experimental evidence that challenges the assumption that cash-out features directly drive problematic betting. While correlational studies have linked cash-outs to harm, this causal test did not replicate that finding. Clinicians should be cautious about attributing clients' gambling problems primarily to specific platform features and instead focus on broader psychological mechanisms. The study also illustrates how correlational and experimental evidence can diverge, a useful psychoeducation point when discussing gambling behavior with clients who may blame the platform rather than their own patterns.
relevant_for:
- Therapists treating gambling disorder and behavioral addictions
- Addiction counselors working with online gambling issues
- Clinicians interested in evidence-based psychoeducation about gambling mechanisms
- Researchers studying gambling behavior and policy

title: The Role of Untreated ADHD in Sleep Disturbances and PTSD Treatment Resistance Among Special Operations Forces
authors: Faulkner
summary: Examines how untreated ADHD contributes to sleep disturbances and treatment resistance in PTSD among Special Operations Forces personnel. The study investigates the interplay between ADHD-related neurocognitive deficits, disrupted sleep architecture, and reduced responsiveness to standard PTSD treatments in this high-risk military population.
clinical_insight: Untreated ADHD may be a hidden driver of PTSD treatment failure in military and veteran populations, operating through sleep disruption and attentional deficits that undermine trauma processing. Clinicians working with treatment-resistant PTSD in veterans should routinely screen for undiagnosed ADHD and consider whether stimulant treatment or ADHD-targeted interventions might remove a barrier to PTSD recovery. A blind spot is the focus on Special Operations Forces, which may limit generalizability to broader veteran and civilian comorbid ADHD-PTSD populations.
relevant_for: Military and veteran mental health clinicians treating comorbid ADHD and PTSD, especially in treatment-resistant cases

title: The Nature of Mind-Wandering to Secrets
authors: Valentina Bianchi, Katharine Greenaway, Sarah T. O'Brien, Janine Griffiths, Kate NW Kim, Ella Moeck, Michael Slepian, Elise Kalokerinos
summary: Two studies examined how secrets affect well-being through mind-wandering. A 14-day daily diary (N=240) found that worries about secrets intruded spontaneously, while deliberate mind-wandering involved fantasizing and daydreaming. A 7-day experience-sampling study (N=207) showed that spontaneous (but not deliberate) mind-wandering to secrets predicted greater negative emotion both in the moment and approximately 2 hours later. The key finding is that it is the involuntary, spontaneous intrusion of secret-related thoughts, rather than deliberate rumination, that drives the well-being costs of secrecy.
clinical_insight: Secrets are common in therapy, whether clients hold personal secrets or, in the case of therapists, professional ones. This research clarifies the mechanism: it is not that clients choose to think about their secrets but that the thoughts intrude unbidden, causing sustained negative affect. For therapists, this suggests that helping clients reduce spontaneous intrusions, perhaps through mindfulness or attentional retraining, may be more effective than encouraging them to deliberately process or disclose the secret. The finding that the negative affect persists for approximately 2 hours after intrusion also helps explain why secrets can feel chronically burdensome even when not actively being concealed.
relevant_for:
- Therapists working with clients who hold burdensome secrets
- Clinicians interested in rumination and intrusive thought processes
- Therapists using mindfulness-based approaches for unwanted thoughts
- Practitioners addressing shame and concealment in therapy

title: The Long-Term Effectiveness of Psychodynamic and Analytical Psychotherapy in Routine Care: Results from a Pragmatic Study over Six Years
authors: Dr. Miriam Henkel, Johannes Zimmermann, Matthias Volz, Dorothea Huber, Hermann Staats, Cord Benecke
summary: Reports six-year pragmatic longitudinal outcomes of psychodynamic and analytical psychotherapy in routine clinical care settings, examining symptom trajectories, treatment completion rates, and long-term maintenance of gains in comparison to randomized trial benchmarks and naturalistic clinical expectations.
clinical_insight: The 6-year durability of LTPP outcomes supports its use for clients with complex or personality-based difficulties where shorter treatments have failed. Therapists should consider psychodynamic referral for chronic, characterologically rooted presentations rather than defaulting exclusively to brief manualized interventions.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic psychotherapists and clinicians treating complex, chronic mental health conditions

title: Promoting well-doing: A Positive Psychology intervention for regulating performance momentum and thriving among high achievers
authors: David Foster, D. D. Allan
summary: This study tested a one-week daily journalling intervention called "well done" journalling among 67 high-achieving university students. Participants wrote daily about small actions they had done well. While subjective wellbeing did not change significantly from an already elevated baseline, perceived work performance improved significantly. A dose-response pattern emerged: participants who journaled more frequently showed stronger performance improvements. Qualitative feedback highlighted perceived benefits in positive affect, self-esteem, and motivation.
clinical_insight: This study offers therapists a simple, accessible intervention tool for clients who struggle with self-criticism or perfectionism. The "well done" journalling approach shifts focus from what went wrong to what went right, which can be particularly helpful for high-functioning clients who tend to dismiss their accomplishments. The dose-response finding supports assigning it as between-session homework with daily practice. The fact that wellbeing did not improve (likely due to ceiling effects in this sample) should not discourage therapists from using it with clinical populations who typically present with lower baseline wellbeing where the effect may be more pronounced.
relevant_for:
- Therapists working with perfectionistic or self-critical clients
- Clinicians incorporating positive psychology interventions into practice
- Therapists assigning between-session homework tasks
- Practitioners working with high-achieving or burnout-prone professionals

title: Clinician Perspectives on Process-Based Therapy
authors: Clarissa W. Ong, Kate Sheehan, Joseph Ciarrochi, Eric Lee
summary: Surveyed clinicians about their perspectives on Process-Based Therapy (PBT), an approach focused on precisely matching treatment components to individual needs rather than applying protocols based on diagnosis. Clinicians reported moderate familiarity with PBT and identified both enthusiasm for its individualized approach and barriers including training gaps, implementation challenges, and limited available resources for adoption.
clinical_insight: Process-Based Therapy represents a shift from diagnosis-driven treatment selection to mechanism-driven intervention matching. For therapists already practicing eclectically, PBT provides a formal framework that legitimizes what many already do intuitively. However, the identified training and implementation barriers suggest that adoption will depend on accessible continuing education and clear case illustrations rather than theoretical papers alone.
relevant_for: Therapists interested in transdiagnostic, mechanism-based approaches to treatment selection and those seeking frameworks beyond manualized protocols

title: Brain age gap, cortisol reactivity, and developmental timing of childhood maltreatment in major depressive disorder
authors: Rotem Dan, Sarah Syed, Lauren M. Hutson, Aliza Brown, Emma Palermo, Emily Belleau, Roselinde Kaiser, Fei Du, Diego A. Pizzagalli
summary: This study investigates the relationship between brain age gap (the difference between predicted and chronological brain age), cortisol reactivity, and the developmental timing of childhood sexual abuse in women with major depressive disorder (MDD). Across two independent samples (N=71 and N=85), the authors examined women with current MDD, remitted MDD, and healthy controls, all with histories of childhood sexual abuse. The study explores how childhood trauma timing interacts with depression status and physiological stress responsivity to produce accelerated biological aging in the brain.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with trauma and depression, this study provides biological evidence supporting the link between early adverse experiences and long-term physiological dysregulation. The finding that developmental timing of abuse matters for brain aging trajectories reinforces the clinical importance of detailed trauma history-taking, particularly regarding when maltreatment occurred. Therapists can use this knowledge to psychoeducate clients about the mind-body connection in trauma recovery and to validate that the physiological effects of childhood abuse are real and measurable, not just psychological. The cortisol reactivity findings may also inform discussions about stress management and nervous system regulation in trauma-focused therapy.
relevant_for: Therapists working with adult survivors of childhood sexual abuse, trauma-focused therapists, clinicians treating treatment-resistant depression, therapists integrating psychoeducation about biological stress responses into their practice.

title: Hyper-Associative Cognition:  Rethinking Attention, Trauma, and the ADHD-PTSD Continuum
authors: Charles J. Wolf
summary: Proposes a hyper-associative cognition model that reconceptualizes attentional differences in ADHD and PTSD as related manifestations of excessively broad associative networks, rather than attentional deficits per se, with implications for understanding the ADHD-PTSD comorbidity continuum.
clinical_insight: The shared hyper-associative thinking pattern in ADHD and PTSD suggests clinicians treating either condition should assess for loosely connected, rapidly shifting cognitions. Teaching clients cognitive anchoring strategies such as structured journaling, thought-record worksheets, and mindfulness can improve cognitive coherence and reduce distress from intrusive associations.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and trauma-focused CBT therapists working with ADHD-PTSD comorbidity

title: Can modifiable dementia risk factors help account for the predictive link between personality and midlife cognitive health?
authors: Emorie D Beck, Evan A. Warfel, Winkie Ma, Angelina Sutin, Richard Robins
summary: This 17-year longitudinal study of 1,106 Mexican-origin adults examines whether modifiable Lancet Commission dementia risk factors (hypertension, depression symptoms, sleep problems, etc.) mediate the relationship between Big Five personality traits and midlife cognitive function. The research uses latent growth curve modeling to analyze personality trajectories and their association with cognitive outcomes, testing whether addressing modifiable risk factors could buffer the cognitive impact of personality-related vulnerabilities.
clinical_insight: This study is directly relevant for therapists working with midlife and older adult clients, particularly those concerned about cognitive decline. It identifies personality traits as early markers of cognitive risk and shows that modifiable factors like depression symptoms and sleep quality serve as mediators. For therapists, this means that treating depression, improving sleep hygiene, and managing cardiovascular risk factors in midlife clients may have downstream benefits for cognitive health. The findings support an integrative approach where mental health treatment is framed as part of a broader cognitive health strategy, giving therapists additional motivation-based language for engaging clients in lifestyle and psychological interventions.
relevant_for: Therapists working with midlife and older adults, neuropsychologists, clinicians integrating lifestyle medicine with psychotherapy, therapists treating late-onset depression.

title: Fear From Afar, Not So Risky After All: Distancing Moderates the Relationship Between Fear and Risk-Taking
authors: Mayiwar, Björklund
summary: Demonstrates that cognitive distancing—a core reappraisal technique—effectively moderates the relationship between fear and risk-taking behavior. When participants adopted a distancing perspective, fear no longer predicted risk-taking, suggesting that reappraisal-based distancing can decouple emotional responses from maladaptive behavioral decisions.
clinical_insight: Provides experimental evidence that distancing techniques taught in CBT and ACT can effectively disrupt the fear-behavior chain that maintains anxiety disorders, particularly in situations where fear drives avoidance or impulsive risk-taking. Therapists can use this finding to strengthen the rationale for teaching distancing skills, showing patients that changing their relationship to fear thoughts rather than eliminating fear itself is sufficient to change behavioral outcomes. A blind spot is that the study used non-clinical samples, so the magnitude of distancing effects in clinical anxiety populations remains to be established.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists treating anxiety disorders where fear-driven avoidance or risk-taking is a maintaining factor

title: Learning while scaling: A dual-engine architecture for governing adaptation as evidence-based interventions scale
authors: Tom L. Osborn
summary: This paper proposes a dual-engine framework for scaling evidence-based mental health interventions, addressing the tension between maintaining fidelity to proven mechanisms and adapting to new contexts. Drawing on implementation science and the Shamiri Institute model, it argues that organizations need two simultaneous processes: one optimizing the core intervention components and another learning from real-world adaptations. The framework aims to prevent voltage drop, the silent erosion of active ingredients when interventions scale across different settings, workforces, and populations.
clinical_insight: For therapists and clinical leaders involved in program development, training, or implementing manualized treatments at scale, this framework offers a practical governance model. Many therapists encounter the challenge of adapting evidence-based protocols for their specific client populations while worrying about fidelity. This paper provides a structured way to think about which adaptations are safe and which risk undermining the interventions mechanisms of change. Clinical supervisors and training directors will find the dual-engine concept useful for balancing adaptation guidance with fidelity requirements in their training programs.
relevant_for: Clinical supervisors, training directors, therapists implementing manualized treatments, program managers in community mental health, clinicians involved in dissemination and implementation.

title: Inhibitory Control Deficits in Borderline Personality Disorder: A Meta-Analysis of Stop-Signal and Go/No-Go Tasks
authors: Authors unavailable (embargoed preprint)
summary: This PRISMA-guided meta-analysis of 37 datasets from 35 articles found that adults with BPD show small-to-moderate motor inhibitory control deficits compared to healthy controls on Stop-Signal and Go/No-Go tasks. Contrary to common clinical assumptions, emotional factors did not moderate inhibitory control performance. Crucially, self-reported impulsivity did not correlate with task-based measures, suggesting subjective and objective impulsivity capture fundamentally different constructs.
clinical_insight: The disconnect between self-reported and task-based impulsivity is a critical finding for BPD assessment — clinicians should not assume that a patients subjective sense of impulsivity maps onto motor inhibition capacity. The lack of emotional moderation challenges the widely held model that BPD impulsivity is primarily affect-driven, which has direct implications for DBTs emotion-regulation-focused approach to impulsive behavior. Clinicians might reconsider whether some impulsive acts in BPD reflect trait-level cognitive control differences rather than solely emotional dysregulation. The methodological heterogeneity flagged in this meta-analysis is itself a concern: current neuropsychological assessment of BPD impulsivity lacks standardization, limiting its clinical utility.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in borderline personality disorder, particularly those using DBT and schema therapy approaches

title: Some Religious and Spiritual Experiences Associated with Depression Resemble Hypomania/Mania
authors: Ari Brouwer, Charles L Raison
summary: This study examines whether religious and spiritual experiences (RSEs) that follow depressive episodes resemble hypomanic or manic states. Using content analysis and phenomenological analysis of 249 first-person RSE reports associated with depression, the authors characterize the duration, emotional valence, and phenomenological features of these experiences. Findings reveal that some post-depression RSEs involve prolonged states (days to weeks) of positive emotion, grandiosity, and increased energy that closely parallel hypomanic/manic presentations.
clinical_insight: Differential diagnosis between spiritual experiences and manic/hypomanic episodes is a clinically important challenge, particularly in culturally diverse therapeutic settings. Therapists working with clients who report spiritual or religious experiences following depression need to carefully distinguish between genuine spiritual growth, normative religious experience, and potentially bipolar spectrum pathology. This study provides a phenomenological framework for making this distinction, which is directly applicable to clinical assessment and formulation. The findings support a more nuanced approach to cultural competence in mood disorder assessment, where spiritual experiences are not automatically pathologized but are also not reflexively accepted as benign.
relevant_for: Therapists working with bipolar spectrum disorders, culturally diverse populations, clients integrating spiritual and psychological frameworks, and clinicians conducting differential diagnoses between spiritual experiences and psychopathology.

title: Resilience in Sexual Assault Victimization: Do Mindfulness and Emotion Regulation Buffer Associations with Post-Traumatic Stress?
authors: Wang et al.
summary: This study compared post-traumatic stress severity across three victimization profiles in 247 cisgender women aged 21-30: childhood sexual abuse only, adulthood sexual assault only, and revictimized (both CSA and ASA). Revictimized participants showed the highest PTSS severity, followed by ASA-only and CSA-only. Mindfulness and emotion regulation were directly associated with reduced PTSS across all profiles, suggesting these psychological skills buffer trauma symptoms regardless of victimization timing or pattern.
clinical_insight: The finding that mindfulness and emotion regulation reduce PTSS across all victimization profiles—including the most severe revictimization group—supports incorporating these skills into trauma-focused treatments. For therapists, this reinforces the value of teaching mindfulness-based and emotion regulation strategies (e.g., DBT skills, ACT mindfulness) as transdiagnostic protective factors rather than as primary treatments. A limitation: the cross-sectional design cannot establish causation, and the sample was restricted to cisgender women aged 21-30, limiting generalizability to male survivors, older adults, or gender-diverse individuals.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with sexual trauma survivors, particularly those treating young adult women with complex victimization histories

title: Personality improves prediction of the onset of common mental disorders
authors: Ye Zhang, Michelle Luciano, Uku Vainik, Rene Mottus
summary: This study tests whether Big Five personality traits predict new-onset common mental disorders (CMDs) using biobank data and machine learning approaches. Personality traits are examined as risk predictors measurable long before clinical presentation, leveraging their temporal stability and conceptual continuity with psychopathology. The study investigates both broad traits and personality nuances (facets and items) to determine whether finer-grained personality assessment improves prediction of depression, anxiety, and related disorders.
clinical_insight: For therapists, this research supports the routine use of personality assessment as a screening and case formulation tool. If personality traits reliably predict mental disorder onset, therapists can use personality profiles to identify clients at elevated risk before clinical symptoms fully emerge, enabling earlier intervention and preventive strategies. The finding that personality nuances improve prediction suggests that brief personality measures in intake assessments could serve a dual purpose: informing case formulation while also flagging future risk. This is particularly relevant for therapists in primary care or screening settings who need efficient assessment tools.
relevant_for: Therapists conducting intake assessments, those working in prevention and early intervention, clinicians using personality assessment in case formulation, therapists in integrated primary care settings.

title: Adverse experiences of women with undiagnosed ADHD and the invaluable role of diagnosis
authors: Eve Holden, Helena Kobayashi-Wood
summary: A mixed-methods survey of 28 women with late-diagnosed ADHD revealed pervasive patterns of criticism, lack of support from medical professionals, and internalized shame across the lifespan. Participants described decades of low self-esteem, guilt, and negative self-perception attributed to undiagnosed ADHD. Receiving a diagnosis was experienced as revelatory, with participants reporting improved self-esteem, a sense that their lives finally made sense, and reduced suicidal ideation. Many grieved the lives they could have led with earlier identification.
clinical_insight: This study powerfully illustrates how ADHD in women is routinely misattributed to character flaws, anxiety, or depression, leading to cascading harm across developmental stages. For private practice clinicians, the key takeaway is the critical importance of screening for ADHD in adult women presenting with treatment-resistant depression, anxiety, or chronic low self-esteem — especially when standard treatments have been partially or fully ineffective. The diagnostic revelation narrative reported by participants underscores that assessment itself can be therapeutic. A significant blind spot is the continuing reliance on male-normed ADHD presentations in screening tools, which systematically miss the inattentive subtype predominantly seen in women.
relevant_for: Clinicians in adult private practice, particularly those seeing women with treatment-resistant mood and anxiety presentations

title: Intertwining experience and evidence: how can co-created causal diagrams guide meaningful research questions about psychosocial processes in pain?
authors: Rebecca M. Pearson, Amanda Ly, Neil Heyton-Dumbleton, Alexandria Andrayas, Megan Chambers, Noureen Shivji, Rachael Gooberman-Hill, Samantha Stone, Matthew Nunes, Laura Oporto Lisboa
summary: This participatory co-design study develops a co-creation process for building shared causal frameworks about psychosocial processes in chronic pain. Using facilitated workshops with multiple stakeholders including people with lived pain experience, clinicians, and researchers, the study produces a shared causal diagram that identifies meaningful research questions and guides data modeling decisions. The approach bridges the gap between clinical experience and research evidence.
clinical_insight: Therapists working with chronic pain clients often observe complex psychosocial interactions that are difficult to capture in standardized assessments. This co-created causal modeling approach offers a structured method for therapists to articulate their clinical observations about how psychological, social, and pain-related factors interact in individual cases. The participatory framework also models how therapists and clients can collaborate to develop shared understanding of pain processes, which has direct application to psychoeducation and collaborative case formulation in pain management.
relevant_for: Pain psychologists, chronic pain rehabilitation therapists, therapists using collaborative formulation approaches, clinicians working in multidisciplinary pain teams.

title: Unmasking the Role of External and Internal Shame in Eating Disorders
authors: Olivia Keane, Emanuele Fino, Cherie Armour
summary: Investigates the distinct roles of external shame (how one believes others perceive them) and internal shame (self-directed contempt) in eating disorders, examining how each shame type relates to eating disorder symptom severity, body image disturbance, and treatment outcomes across diagnostic subtypes.
clinical_insight: Shame appears to be a core maintaining factor in eating disorders, indicating therapists should explicitly assess shame levels and incorporate compassion-focused or shame-attenuation strategies such as self-compassion exercises, cognitive defusion, and externalization of the critical inner voice rather than focusing solely on eating behavior change.
relevant_for: Eating disorder specialists and CBT-E practitioners

title: Smoking Exposure Application (SEA): Development and Validation of An Augmented Reality Smoking Cessation App
authors: Reza Barzegar, Javad Hatami, Peyman Ghobadi-Azbari, Hamed Ekhtiari, Tara Rezapour
summary: This study develops and validates the Smoking Exposure Application (SEA), an augmented reality (AR) tool for smoking cue-exposure therapy. By bridging laboratory cue-reactivity paradigms with real-world environments, SEA allows clients to encounter smoking cues in ecologically valid settings through their smartphone. The study reports on the app development, validation of cue materials, and preliminary testing of its ability to elicit craving responses relevant to smoking cessation treatment.
clinical_insight: Cue-exposure therapy is an established component of addiction treatment, but its effectiveness is limited by the gap between controlled clinic environments and the real-world contexts where cravings occur. AR technology offers therapists a practical tool to extend cue-exposure work beyond the therapy room, enabling clients to practice craving management in settings they actually encounter in daily life. Therapists working in smoking cessation can integrate this as a between-session homework tool, enhancing the ecological validity of exposure-based interventions and potentially improving treatment outcomes.
relevant_for: Addiction therapists, smoking cessation specialists, therapists using cue-exposure approaches, clinicians integrating digital tools into addiction treatment.

title: Targeting Mechanisms of Change as a Path to Precision Psychotherapy in Community Settings
authors: Nili Solomonov
summary: Argues for a mechanism-focused approach to improving psychotherapy outcomes for depression in community settings, where response rates remain stagnant despite scientific progress. Proposes integrating neuroscience tools, brain-based mechanisms, and AI methods to identify change mechanisms and develop simpler, scalable interventions that target these mechanisms directly rather than delivering full manualized protocols.
clinical_insight: Community therapists often lack the resources to deliver full manualized protocols with fidelity. This paper supports a pragmatic alternative: identify the active mechanism of change for a given patient (e.g., cognitive reappraisal, behavioral activation, affect regulation) and deliver targeted interventions around that mechanism. This precision approach could improve outcomes without requiring therapists to adopt entirely new treatment models.
relevant_for: Community mental health clinicians treating depression who seek to improve outcomes without overhauling their therapeutic approach

title: Ecological momentary intervention fidelity: Qualitative findings from a habit degradation trial
authors: Robert Edgren
summary: This qualitative study examines intervention fidelity in digital behavior change interventions (DBCIs), specifically an ecological momentary intervention (EMI) targeting unhealthy snacking through habit degradation strategies. Using semi-structured interviews and template analysis, the study explores participant experiences across three fidelity dimensions: delivery (was the intervention delivered as intended), receipt (did participants understand and engage with it), and enactment (did participants actually use the strategies in daily life).
clinical_insight: As therapists increasingly recommend or integrate digital therapeutic tools into their practice, understanding intervention fidelity becomes essential. This study reveals the specific ways that digital interventions can fail to achieve their intended effects: participants may receive the intervention but not deeply engage with it, or may understand the strategies but struggle to enact them in real-world moments. Therapists can use these fidelity insights to better prepare clients for using digital tools, set realistic expectations, and monitor not just whether a client used an app but whether they engaged with it meaningfully.
relevant_for: Therapists recommending digital mental health tools, clinicians working with habit-based interventions, therapists integrating technology into treatment, supervision of digital tool use.

title: Racial/Ethnic Identity Moderates Changes in Skill Use and Therapeutic Alliance but Not Anxiety or Depression in the Unified Protocol
authors: Matthew W. Southward, Alex Urs, Thomas Adams, Shannon Sauer-Zavala
summary: This study examined how racial/ethnic background impacted treatment outcomes, therapeutic alliance, and skill use in the Unified Protocol (UP), a transdiagnostic CBT approach. Among 70 adults with emotional disorders who completed six core UP sessions, minoritized patients showed weaker alliances and less frequent skill use, though outcomes for anxiety and depression were comparable to White patients.
clinical_insight: Therapists using transdiagnostic approaches like the Unified Protocol should proactively attend to racial/ethnic identity as a moderating factor in the therapeutic alliance and skill acquisition, not just as a demographic variable. Even when symptom outcomes appear equivalent across groups, the pathway to change may differ substantially — minority patients may need more alliance-building and skill-use encouragement early in treatment to achieve comparable gains.
relevant_for: CBT therapists using transdiagnostic protocols with racially and ethnically diverse adult populations

title: Developing a measure for the need for sex
authors: Oliver C. Schultheiss, Jessica Hinzmann
summary: This research develops and validates a picture-story measure of the need for sex using an experimental-manipulation, measure, indicator, and criterion (EMMIC) validation approach. The resulting coding system includes a measure of sexual conflict alongside the need for sex, with reliably coded categories that capture individual differences in sexual motivation. The measure is designed to assess an implicit motivational need rather than self-reported sexual behavior or attitudes.
clinical_insight: Sex therapists frequently encounter discrepancies between clients reported sexual attitudes and their underlying motivational dynamics. A validated measure of the need for sex as a motivational construct (distinct from sexual behavior or satisfaction) gives therapists a more nuanced tool for assessing sexual dynamics in couple therapy. The sexual conflict component is particularly relevant for couples presenting with sexual desire discrepancy, where understanding the underlying motivational needs can guide more effective intervention than focusing solely on behavioral frequency or satisfaction scores.
relevant_for: Sex therapists, couples therapists, therapists working with sexual desire discrepancy, clinicians using motivational approaches in sexology.

title: Testing Reciprocal Within-Person Changes in Aversive Reactions to Emotions and Skill Use in the Unified Protocol
authors: Hannah Croom, Matthew W. Southward, Shannon Sauer-Zavala
summary: Using ecological momentary assessment data from 70 adults with emotional disorders undergoing the Unified Protocol (UP) for transdiagnostic treatment, this study examined how within-person changes in aversive reactions to emotions relate to skill use during treatment. The findings reveal bidirectional relationships: as patients practice UP skills, their aversive reactions to negative emotions decrease, which in turn predicts further skill engagement, suggesting a reinforcing therapeutic cycle.
clinical_insight: This study provides empirical support for the core mechanism of the Unified Protocol — that reducing aversive reactions to emotions is not just an outcome but an active driver of continued skill use. For therapists delivering transdiagnostic treatments, the findings reinforce the importance of explicitly tracking patients’ emotional reactivity as a process measure rather than focusing solely on symptom reduction. The EMA methodology demonstrates how intensive longitudinal data can illuminate within-session and between-session processes that traditional pre-post designs miss. The sample was predominantly White and female, limiting generalizability to more diverse clinical populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering the Unified Protocol or other transdiagnostic CBT approaches for anxiety and mood disorders

title: Associations between social cognition measures and alcohol use across age and sex: a network approach
authors: Christophe Romein, Emese Kroon, Karis Colyer-Patel, Rene Freichel, Helle Larsen, Janna Cousijn
summary: Cross-sectional network analysis of 936 alcohol users (ages 16-81, M=32.1, SD=13.9; 52.6% female) drawn from two merged studies, examining unique associations between social cognition measures and alcohol use severity (AUDIT; M=8.16, SD=6.25, 40.5% at-risk). The 10-node LASSO-regularized Gaussian Graphical Model included the Drinking Motives Questionnaire-Revised (social and conformity subscales), the Social Reward Questionnaire (admiration, negative social potency, passivity, sociability, prosocial interactions), the Social Attunement Questionnaire (cognition and behavior subscales), and AUDIT scores. In the partial correlation network (CS stability=0.75, 20 of 45 edges non-zero), only social drinking motives and SRQ sociability were directly connected to alcohol use severity. SAQ behavior (adjusting behavior to match the social environment) emerged as the most central node across centrality indices. An age-moderated network found that all associations were stable across age except the sociability-AUDIT edge, which weakened in older participants (interaction weight=-0.076, retained in 65% of bootstrap samples). A network comparison test between male (n≈443) and female (n≈488) networks found no differences in global strength (S=0.015, p=0.972), structure (M=0.121, p=0.680), or edge weights, despite males scoring higher on AUDIT, negative social potency, and conformity motives, and females scoring higher on SAQ cognition, sociability, and prosocial interactions. Bivariate Spearman correlations showed positive associations between nearly all social measures and AUDIT, but most did not survive network partialing.
clinical_insight: For clinicians treating alcohol use disorder, this network study clarifies that broad social engagement (sociability) and social drinking motives are the primary social drivers of alcohol use severity, while more specific social-cognitive factors like social attunement and prosocial tendencies operate indirectly through these pathways. This means that interventions targeting social contexts of drinking (e.g., reducing alcohol-centered social activities, building non-drinking social outlets) may be more impactful than interventions targeting specific social-cognitive deficits like prosocial orientation. The age finding has a direct practical implication: the link between sociability and drinking weakens with age, consistent with the "maturing out" phenomenon, suggesting that younger clients may benefit most from interventions addressing the social reward value of drinking contexts. The absence of sex differences in network structure, despite mean-level differences in scores, implies that therapeutic approaches addressing social drivers of alcohol use need not differ substantially by gender — what differs is prevalence, not mechanism. Clinicians should be cautious about over-interpreting bivariate associations between specific social-cognitive measures and drinking, as the network shows many of these are indirect.
relevant_for: Addiction therapists, clinicians treating alcohol use disorder, therapists incorporating social cognition into addiction treatment, those working with younger adults whose drinking is socially driven

title: Understanding the Unwanted: A Mixed-Methods Study on Negative Effects in an Internet-based Intervention for Depression
authors: Friederike Fenski, Manuel Heinrich, Carmen Schaeuffele, Pavle Zagorscak, Christine Knaevelsrud, Johanna Boettcher
summary: In 1610 participants undergoing an internet-based depression intervention, 7% reported at least one negative effect. These were categorized into treatment-related (format, contact, implementation) and patient-related (symptoms, insight) negative effects. Treatment-related negative effects showed significantly less symptom improvement than patient-related ones, despite no adherence differences.
clinical_insight: This study's distinction between treatment-related and patient-related negative effects has direct clinical utility: a patient overwhelmed by program pacing needs a different format, while one experiencing increased emotional awareness may be progressing. The 7% negative effect rate is useful for informed consent when referring to digital interventions.
relevant_for: Clinicians prescribing or referring to internet-based depression treatments, and researchers studying psychotherapy adverse effects

title: Obsessive Thinking About a Loved One in Romantic Love: Eight Perspectives
authors: Adam Bode, Sandra Langeslag
summary: This article provides the first comprehensive review of obsessive thinking about a loved one in romantic love, organizing the literature using an Eight Perspectives approach spanning evolutionary, clinical, cognitive, neurobiological, developmental, cross-cultural, comparative, and applied dimensions. The review examines the boundaries between normative romantic obsession, obsessive-compulsive patterns, and clinical pathology.
clinical_insight: Therapists frequently encounter clients whose relationship preoccupation blurs the line between intense romantic attachment and clinically significant obsessive thinking. This review provides a multi-perspective framework for distinguishing normative romantic obsession from OCD-spectrum pathology, which is directly applicable to clinical assessment. Understanding the evolutionary and neurobiological underpinnings of romantic obsession helps therapists normalize certain experiences while identifying when intrusive thoughts about a partner cross into clinically significant territory requiring intervention.
relevant_for: Therapists working with relationship distress, OCD specialists seeing clients with relationship-focused obsessions, couples therapists, clinicians assessing obsessive-compulsive patterns in romantic relationships.

title: Language Processing Differences in Individuals with Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)
authors: Kian Zehtabian, Leila Elahinezhad
summary: This review synthesizes evidence that OCD is associated with distinct language processing abnormalities including difficulties resolving ambiguity, hyper-literal interpretation, and heightened sensitivity to threatening language. The authors link these deficits to serotonin, dopamine, and glutamate dysregulation and frontostriatal circuit abnormalities. Preliminary findings suggest language deficits may serve as endophenotypes, with potential for informing personalized interventions stratified by symptom dimensions.
clinical_insight: The identification of language processing abnormalities in OCD — particularly hyper-literal interpretation and threat-sensitivity — has direct implications for how clinicians communicate with OCD patients during therapy. Therapists using ERP or CBT should be aware that ambiguous or figurative language in session may be misinterpreted literally, potentially exacerbating doubt and rumination. The frontostriatal circuit link connects language deficits to the same neurobiology underlying compulsive behavior, suggesting that language-focused interventions could complement exposure-based approaches. A notable gap is the absence of symptom-dimension-specific language profiles — contamination-related OCD may involve different language biases than harm-related OCD, which could guide tailored treatment planning.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating OCD, especially those using ERP and CBT, who should consider how communication style may interact with language processing biases

title: The Repeatable Social Stress Test (ReSST): a guide and validation of a novel paradigm to elicit repeatable stress responses in humans
authors: Cecilia Vezzani, Liva Martinussen, Matthew Thompson, Claudia Pazmandi, Vera Rudi, Nasimeh Naseri, Martin Trostheim, Molly Carlyle, Isabell M. Meier, Guro Engvig LØseth, Marie Eikemo, Siri Leknes
summary: The Repeatable Social Stress Test (ReSST) is a novel experimental paradigm designed to overcome limitations of existing stress induction tasks, which typically cannot be repeated within or across sessions. By combining adapted versions of the Trier Social Stress Test and the Iowa Singing Social Stress Test with video-based stress reminders, the ReSST enables researchers to elicit and study recurring stress responses in a controlled setting. Validation with 66 healthy adults demonstrated robust increases in subjective stress, heart rate, and cortisol compared to matched no-stress control conditions. The paradigm successfully reactivated stress responses within sessions using video reminders of the social-evaluative component.
clinical_insight: Repeated stress exposure is a hallmark of many clinical presentations, from workplace burnout to chronic PTSD. The ReSST provides an empirically validated tool for clinical researchers investigating how stress accumulates and whether interventions can buffer against repeated activation. For practicing therapists, the paradigm's finding that video reminders alone can reactivate stress responses suggests that environmental cues play a powerful role in maintaining stress cycles, reinforcing the importance of cue-exposure techniques in CBT and other trauma-focused therapies. The sex differences in subjective responding warrant attention in clinical assessment.
relevant_for: Clinical researchers investigating stress reactivity, therapists working with trauma and stress-related disorders, health psychologists studying psychophysiological stress responses.

title: Associations between Personality Profiles and Suicide-Related Risk Factors from the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide
authors: Martina Fruhbauerova, Megan L. Rogers, Julie Cerel
summary: This study examines how Five Factor Model personality profiles relate to suicide-related risk factors derived from the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide (ITS), specifically thwarted belongingness, perceived burdensomeness, and hopelessness. By mapping personality configurations onto ITS constructs, the research identifies which personality profiles carry the highest suicide risk and how these dynamics interact.
clinical_insight: Integrating personality profiling with the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide offers clinicians a more nuanced risk assessment framework than either approach alone. The finding that specific personality configurations (particularly high neuroticism combined with low agreeableness) map onto ITS risk factors has direct implications for case formulation: patients with these profiles may need targeted interventions addressing both personality-level patterns (e.g., schema work for maladaptive interpersonal schemas) and acute ITS factors (e.g., belongingness interventions). The tension in the literature between trait-based and state-based suicide risk models remains unresolved; this study bridges them but cannot establish causality. A blind spot is the near-exclusive reliance on self-report measures of personality, which may be distorted in acutely suicidal patients.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments and personality-guided case formulation in adult inpatient and outpatient settings

title: Investigating prospective associations between household food insecurity and eating disorder pathology in young people in the UK: Evidence from Understanding Society
authors: Akansha Naraindas, Francesca Solmi, Vivienne Hazzard, Fidan Turk
summary: Using data from the UK Household Longitudinal Study, this study prospectively examined whether household food insecurity assessed in 2021-2023 predicted eating disorder pathology two years later in adolescents aged 14-17. Controlling for socio-demographics, carer mental health, ethnicity, financial strain, and urban/rural residence, food insecurity showed only a weak association with later dieting and weight-loss behaviours (RR 1.07, 95% CI 0.99-1.16) and no association with appearance concerns. These findings challenge assumptions about food insecurity as a direct driver of eating disorder pathology.
clinical_insight: Therapists should be cautious about over-attributing eating disorder presentations to food insecurity in their clinical formulations. While food-insecure clients may present with disordered eating patterns, these behaviours may reflect economic necessity rather than psychopathology per se. The weak prospective association found here supports the need for comprehensive assessment that considers multiple pathways to eating pathology. This is particularly relevant when working with socioeconomically diverse caseloads where conflating food restriction due to poverty with eating disorder dieting could lead to misdirected treatment.
relevant_for: Eating disorder therapists, adolescent mental health clinicians, and therapists working in socioeconomically disadvantaged communities.

title: The Effects of Dialectical Behavior Therapy on Joy: A Diary Card Study
authors: Bitran et al.
summary: A diary card study examining whether and how Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) increases experiences of positive emotion, specifically joy, in patients undergoing treatment. Using ecological momentary assessment via standard DBT diary cards, the study tracks changes in positive affect over the course of treatment, extending DBT outcome research beyond symptom reduction to include positive emotional functioning.
clinical_insight: DBT is often perceived as primarily focused on reducing self-harm, suicidality, and emotional dysregulation, but this study suggests it may also actively cultivate positive emotional experiences like joy. Therapists can use this finding to motivate patients who may view DBT as solely about crisis management, highlighting the potential for genuine improvements in quality of life and positive affect. The diary card methodology also demonstrates how routine DBT practice tools can serve dual purposes as both clinical monitoring and research instruments, supporting data-driven treatment adaptation.
relevant_for: DBT therapists and clinical program directors measuring treatment outcomes beyond symptom reduction in BPD and emotion dysregulation disorders

title: Recommendations for Recruiting Clinically-Relevant Stratified Samples Across Race/Ethnicity, Gender, and Socioeconomic Status Using the Prolific Online Platform
authors: Julia Machina, Kimberly Muellers, Xiqiao Chen, E. Samuel Winer
summary: This methodological paper describes in detail how to implement a fully crossed stratified sampling design using the Prolific platform to ensure adequate representation across three key demographic dimensions: race/ethnicity, gender, and socioeconomic status. The study targets 1,008 U.S. adults examining fear of happiness and depression. The authors discuss practical challenges encountered during recruitment, including uncategorizable cases and the need for SES-based recruitment blocks, and provide recommendations for maintaining analytic rigor while pursuing equity in sampling.
clinical_insight: Evidence-based practice requires that the evidence base itself is representative of the clinical population. This paper's methodology is directly relevant to therapists because it demonstrates how research can move beyond WEIRD (Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic) samples. The focus on fear of happiness, an emotion relevant to many clinical presentations including depression and complex trauma, underscores how demographic variation in emotional experience may affect treatment planning and expected outcomes.
relevant_for: Clinician-researchers designing therapy outcome studies, therapists interested in cultural competence and research literacy, training program directors.

title: All Tied Up: Perceptions of BDSM and Kink for Mental Health Providers
authors: Briana Louise Kunstman
summary: This paper examines mental health providers’ perceptions, knowledge, and biases regarding BDSM and kink practices. Despite the removal of BDSM from the DSM as a pathological diagnosis, many clinicians remain poorly informed about kink-related practices, leading to stigma, misdiagnosis, and therapeutic rupture when kink-identified clients seek services. The paper reviews the empirical literature on BDSM and mental health, finding no association between consensual kink involvement and psychopathology, and discusses the ethical implications of clinician bias.
clinical_insight: Clinicians in private practice are highly likely to encounter kink-identified clients, yet most receive minimal training in this area. This paper provides a concise, evidence-based corrective to persistent myths linking consensual BDSM to pathology, and highlights how pathologizing attitudes can drive clients to conceal important aspects of their lives, directly undermining the therapeutic relationship. The paper underscores the need for therapists to develop kink-affirming competencies as part of broader cultural humility training. A limitation is the focus on provider perceptions rather than client-reported outcomes of kink-affirmative versus non-affirmative therapy.
relevant_for: All clinicians in adult practice, particularly couples and sex therapists who work with clients who practice consensual kink or BDSM

title: Gender differences in Academic Stress and Academic Pressure among students aged 18-23 in Sri Lanka
authors: Shameeshika Kuhandran, Stephanie Gunaratne
summary: This study surveyed 58 university students in Sri Lanka (39 female, 19 male) aged 18-23 to investigate gender differences in academic stress and academic pressure. Using validated scales and SPSS analysis including correlation, t-tests, and ANOVA, the study found that 45% of participants experienced academic stress with a strong positive correlation between stress and pressure. No significant gender differences emerged, contradicting some Western findings. The main academic stressors were high workload, peer pressure, and worrying about grades.
clinical_insight: The strong correlation between academic stress and academic pressure suggests these constructs operate as a unified stress system for young adults. For therapists, this means that addressing one component (e.g., workload management) may not be sufficient without also addressing the perceived pressure to perform. In Sri Lankan and similar collectivist cultures where educational achievement carries significant family and social weight, academic stress may present differently than in individualistic Western contexts, potentially manifesting through somatic complaints, family conflict, or withdrawal rather than through explicit anxiety reports.
relevant_for: Therapists working with university students, young adult mental health services, and culturally informed practice.

title: A Systematic Review and Theoretical Framework of Stress Generation in Daily Life
authors: Peter Haehner, Rosalie Andrae, Wiebke Bleidorn
summary: This systematic review integrates research on person-environment transactions with the clinical stress generation literature to develop a novel process model of daily stress generation. Unlike traditional stress generation research that examines major life events over months or years, this framework focuses on how individuals with elevated neuroticism or psychopathology actively contribute to minor stressors in their daily lives through specific behavioral and interpersonal mechanisms, creating self-perpetuating cycles of distress.
clinical_insight: The daily process model of stress generation has direct implications for CBT and behavioral activation interventions, as it identifies specific, moment-level behavioral patterns that clinicians can target in session. Rather than addressing abstract ‘stress’ as a global construct, therapists can help clients recognize how their own behaviors — such as reassurance-seeking, conflict avoidance, or procrastination — generate the very stressors that maintain depression and anxiety. The review’s strength is its bridge between personality-level vulnerability (neuroticism) and observable daily behaviors, making the concept clinically tractable. A limitation is the reliance on EMA studies that may not capture the full complexity of real-world stress generation processes.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and behavioral activation practitioners working with depressed or anxious clients who exhibit self-reinforcing stress cycles

title: Impact of a Pre-Test Measurement of Alcohol Craving: Lessons From a Self-Replication Study
authors: Michelle Heck, Etienne Quertemont
summary: This self-replication study re-examined whether measuring alcohol craving before an experimental task influences post-test craving levels, extending original COVID-lockdown findings to a post-lockdown context. Using 142 psychology students in a pre-post vs. post-only design with an alcohol-related video, the study confirmed that heavier drinking and greater video engagement predict craving. However, the key finding that repeated craving measurement inflates post-test levels did not replicate. Reanalysis of original data revealed a gender-specific pattern where the measurement effect was present in men but absent in women.
clinical_insight: The partial replication failure reminds therapists that psychological mechanisms observed under unusual circumstances (such as lockdown) may not generalise to normal conditions, a caution relevant to applying research findings in clinical practice. For addiction treatment, the robust finding that engagement with alcohol-related content predicts craving reinforces the clinical importance of identifying and modifying clients' patterns of engagement with alcohol cues in their environment. Gender differences in craving reactivity suggest that treatment approaches may need to be tailored, with men potentially more susceptible to measurement-induced cue reactivity.
relevant_for: Addiction therapists, CBT practitioners treating alcohol misuse, clinical researchers studying craving mechanisms.

title: A Descriptive Report on the Psychotherapy Experiences of Adult Autistic Laypeople: Service Utilization, Therapeutic Process Familiarity and Preferences, Client-Therapist Factors, Perceptions of Harm, and Considerations for Those Without Experience
authors: Matthew J. Bolton
summary: In a mixed-methods study of 229 autistic adults, this paper explores psychotherapy service utilization, preferences for therapeutic style (measured via the Cooper-Norcross Inventory of Preferences), client-therapist relationship factors, and experiences of perceived harm in therapy. Most participants had some therapy experience and expressed preferences for therapist directivity, encouragement of emotional expression, and structured approaches. The study also considers the perspectives of autistic individuals who have never accessed therapy, highlighting barriers rooted in sensory, communication, and systemic factors.
clinical_insight: This is one of the largest studies to date on autistic adults’ therapy preferences, offering directly actionable guidance for neurotypical therapists working with neurodivergent clients. The finding that autistic clients prefer greater directivity and structure challenges some contemporary therapy trends toward non-directive approaches and suggests that therapists should assess and adapt their style rather than assume a one-size-fits-all therapeutic stance. The qualitative data on perceived harm is particularly important — it underscores how well-meaning therapeutic interventions (e.g., prolonged eye contact, open-ended emotional exploration) can be experienced as aversive by autistic clients. A limitation is the reliance on self-selected participants, which may not capture the experiences of autistic individuals with the greatest barriers to engaging with research.
relevant_for: Therapists working with adult autistic clients, particularly those in private practice who may lack specialized autism training

title: Sensory Processing Sensitivity is associated with Heightened Emotional Response, not Rumination when viewing Climate-Change Media Content
authors: Annalisa Setti, Catherine Trearty, Anna Oberhaeusser, Anna OBrien
summary: In a pre-post experimental design, 119 participants were randomly assigned to view either a climate disaster video or a neutral weather forecast. SPS moderated the effect of condition on negative affect, with high-SPS individuals showing significantly greater increases in negative affect when exposed to climate content. Positive affect and rumination were not significantly affected by condition or SPS. Nature connectedness emerged as an independent predictor of negative affect. The results demonstrate that SPS is a relevant individual difference for understanding emotional responses to environmental media content.
clinical_insight: Highly sensitive persons (HSPs) frequently present in therapy reporting overwhelm, emotional reactivity, and difficulty coping with news and media. This study offers an evidence base for normalising these experiences and distinguishing between heightened emotional responding (which is SPS-related) and rumination (which is not). In treatment, this distinction matters: interventions for emotional reactivity may focus on sensory regulation, grounding, and controlled exposure, while rumination-focused interventions target cognitive processes. Therapists should assess for SPS as part of case formulation and consider how media consumption patterns may be maintaining distress in sensitive clients.
relevant_for: Therapists working with HSP clients, practitioners treating anxiety and emotional dysregulation, therapists addressing eco-anxiety or media-related distress.

title: Emotion Regulation in Daily Life among Adults with Suicidal Thoughts
authors: Yael Millgram, Daniel D.L. Coppersmith, Gal Sheppes, Rebecca Fortgang, Amit Goldenberg, Matthew K. Nock
summary: Uses ecological momentary assessment to examine emotion regulation strategies in the daily lives of adults with suicidal thoughts, identifying which strategies are used most frequently, which are associated with reductions in suicidal ideation, and how contextual factors moderate strategy effectiveness in real time.
clinical_insight: Real-time data show that specific emotion regulation strategies either amplify or reduce subsequent suicidal ideation, suggesting therapists should help clients identify their habitual strategies and replace maladaptive ones like suppression and rumination with adaptive alternatives like cognitive reappraisal and acceptance during crisis safety planning.
relevant_for: Suicide risk assessors and crisis intervention therapists

title: Continuous Traumatic Stress in Ukrainian Civilians: A Study of War-Affected Populations in Ukraine and Austria
authors: Lukas Steinkamper
summary: This master's thesis empirically validates the construct of continuous traumatic stress (CTS) in 113 Ukrainian civilians affected by the Russo-Ukrainian War, comparing displaced individuals in Austria (n=67) with those remaining in Ukraine (n=46). Using ICD-11-aligned measures (ITQ, IDQ, IAQ) and the CTSR scale developed for Ukrainian war contexts, the study tested three hypotheses: (1) CTS predicts depression, anxiety, PTSD, and DSO symptoms; (2) emotion dysregulation moderates these associations; (3) displaced civilians report fewer symptoms than those in Ukraine. Hypothesis 1 was strongly supported, with CTS explaining 28-47% of variance across outcomes, substantially exceeding the variance added by geographical exposure (0.1-1%). Hypothesis 2 was partially supported: ED significantly interacted with CTS for anxiety, PTSD, and DSO outcomes, but the interactions were small (1-4% additional variance) and in a negative direction — meaning higher ED actually weakened, rather than strengthened, the CTS-psychopathology association. Johnson-Neyman analyses showed that CTS effects on these outcomes became non-significant above specific ED thresholds. Hypothesis 3 was rejected: participants in Austria reported significantly higher PTSD and DSO symptoms and more traumatic exposures than those in Ukraine. Exploratory analyses revealed that 66.4% of the sample met criteria for at least one ICD-11 disorder, with CPTSD prevalence significantly higher among displaced participants (37.3% vs. 13.0%).
clinical_insight: The finding that CTS explains large unique variance in psychopathological outcomes beyond standard ICD-11 diagnostic measures is the most clinically significant result of this study. It suggests that the ongoing, present-tense quality of traumatic stress — as opposed to the past-tense focus of PTSD — captures a dimension of suffering that standard trauma assessments miss. Practically, therapists working with war-affected clients should consider adding CTS assessment (using the Ukrainian-validated CTSR) to their clinical formulation. The counterintuitive finding that displaced participants fared worse psychologically than those remaining in the conflict zone challenges the common assumption that physical safety automatically reduces traumatic stress. For therapists, this means that treatment should address the unique stressors of displacement: loss of social roles and professional identity, uncertainty about legal status and duration of stay, language barriers, and the guilt of being "safe" while others remain in danger. The ED moderation finding requires nuanced clinical interpretation: rather than ED amplifying CTS effects as expected, high ED appeared to saturate distress levels, creating a ceiling effect where ongoing threat adds relatively little. This may indicate that for highly dysregulated clients, trauma processing approaches that assume a linear relationship between stressor exposure and symptom severity need adjustment.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with refugees and displaced persons, clinicians using ICD-11 diagnostic frameworks, supervisors in cross-cultural trauma therapy, mental health professionals in organizations serving displaced Ukrainians.

title: Temporal Relationships between Mood and Eating across Eating Disorders: A Network Approach
authors: Sercan Kahveci, Julia Reichenberger, Ann-Kathrin Arend, Alessandra Mansueto, René Freichel, Ulrich Voderholzer, Jens Blechert
summary: This network analysis examines prospective temporal relationships between negative and positive emotions and eating-related behaviors (hunger, food craving, calorie intake, binge episodes) across different eating disorder diagnoses and healthy controls. By mapping directional pathways between mood states and eating behaviors, the study tests whether the theorized vicious cycle between emotional distress and disordered eating operates consistently across AN, BN, BED, and healthy populations.
clinical_insight: Network analysis of temporal mood-eating relationships provides clinicians with a mechanistic understanding of when and how emotional interventions should be deployed in eating disorder treatment. If negative mood prospectively predicts binge episodes but not restriction, this supports prioritizing emotion regulation skills early in BN and BED treatment, while focusing on different mechanisms in AN. For CBT-E and DBT-ED practitioners, these network structures could guide individualized treatment planning by identifying each patient's specific mood-eating cycle. The tension between universal treatment protocols (CBT-E for all EDs) and diagnosis-specific mechanisms revealed by network analysis is significant: if the temporal structures differ across diagnoses, a one-size-fits-all approach may miss critical intervention targets. A blind spot is that network analyses capture population-level temporal patterns that may not apply to any individual patient, highlighting the ongoing gap between group-level network findings and idiographic clinical formulation.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders with CBT-E, DBT-ED, or integrative approaches who want to understand the temporal dynamics of mood-eating relationships

title: Development, Content Validation, and Preliminary Accessibility Testing of a Workplace Well-Being Assessment Tool for Workers with Intellectual Disabilities
authors: Elodie Leclerc, Clement Ginoux, Pascale Leconte, Marine Peslier, Sophie Posseme, Anne Palanque, Tiphaine Huyghebaert-Zouaghi, Nolwenn Charles, Julio Cesar Alves Da Silva, Sandrine Isoard-Gautheur
summary: This two-study paper develops and initially validates the first workplace well-being assessment battery specifically designed for workers with intellectual disabilities in sheltered employment. The instrument is grounded in three theoretical frameworks: the Job Demands-Resources (JD-R) model, the DISC-R model, and recovery theories (the DRAMMA model). The 80-item battery covers four well-being outcomes (burnout, need for recovery, vigor, job satisfaction), job characteristics (demands and resources), and off-duty recovery (six activity types plus recovery experiences including psychological detachment, relaxation, mastery, control, and affiliation). In Study 1, a 9-member expert panel including both academic researchers and frontline practitioners (neuropsychologists, integration coordinators, workplace supervisors, special educators) conducted four rounds of iterative review using the Lynn content validation method, achieving I-CVI = 1.00 for all items. In Study 2, 29 PwID in two French sheltered employment centres completed the questionnaire with researcher support available, with a mean completion time of 28 minutes. Approximately 20% of items received dont-know responses and 6 participants missed at least one item, suggesting generally good comprehension. Descriptive scores revealed moderate levels of burnout, low need for recovery, and moderate-to-high vigor and job satisfaction.
clinical_insight: For therapists supporting adults with intellectual disabilities, this instrument provides a structured way to differentiate between workplace stressors that require organizational-level intervention versus those requiring individual clinical support. The JD-R framework means that a PwID showing high burnout alongside low job resources can be referred for job redesign or supervisor training rather than individual therapy. The finding that most participants achieved good comprehension with supported administration (but not independently) has direct implications for service delivery: clinicians and case managers working with PwID should plan for assisted completion of any standardized assessments rather than assuming self-report capability. The tool also opens research possibilities for examining how workplace wellbeing intersects with mental health outcomes in the PwID population, an area that has been severely understudied due to the lack of appropriate measurement instruments.
relevant_for: Therapists and case managers working with adults with intellectual disabilities in supported employment, occupational psychologists, vocational rehabilitation specialists, and researchers studying wellbeing in neurodivergent populations.

title: Understanding Types of Transitions in Clinical Change: An Introduction from the Complex Dynamic Systems Perspective
authors: Jingmeng Cui, Fred Hasselman, Merlijn Olthof, Anna Lichtwarck-Aschoff
summary: This theoretical paper introduces four types of sudden transitions in clinical change drawn from complex dynamic systems theory: bifurcation-induced tipping, noise-induced tipping, rate-induced tipping, and noise-induced diffusion. Each transition type describes a distinct mechanism by which therapeutic change can occur suddenly rather than gradually, providing a unifying framework for understanding nonlinear change processes in psychotherapy.
clinical_insight: Sudden therapeutic change, long observed in clinical practice but poorly explained by linear models of psychotherapy, is a phenomenon most therapists recognize intuitively. This framework provides clinicians with a vocabulary for understanding why some sessions appear to produce breakthrough moments while others do not. B-tipping (a critical threshold crossed through accumulated change) supports the dose-response model of therapy where persistence matters. N-tipping (random fluctuations triggering change) helps explain why the same intervention works in one session but not another. For therapists, this validates the clinical intuition that some change cannot be planned but can be facilitated by creating conditions that make tipping points more likely. The tension between this framework and manualized approaches is that manualization assumes predictable dose-response relationships, while tipping point dynamics are inherently unpredictable. A blind spot is the absence of empirical validation of these transition types in actual therapy data.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists across all modalities interested in understanding and facilitating sudden therapeutic change and nonlinear recovery processes

title: Trait Apathy and Fatigue are Associated with Diminished Academic Functioning Among University Students Aged 30 - 50
authors: Mindaugas Jurgelis, Monica Copko, Christopher Leaver, Lachlan White, Josiah Krieg
summary: This study of 332 mature university students (aged 30-50) examines how trait apathy and fatigue relate to academic functioning. The research addresses a gap in the literature, as most motivation research focuses on traditional-age students (18-25) while mature students face unique challenges including role conflicts between study, work, and family. Using validated measures including the Apathy Motivation Index and the Modified Fatigue Impact Scale, the study found that apathy and fatigue are largely unrelated to external workload factors such as employment hours, childcare, or home responsibilities, suggesting these motivational problems are driven by internal dispositions rather than situational demands. Regression analyses revealed that apathy uniquely predicted lower autonomous motivation, reduced study engagement, lower study satisfaction, and reduced rated effort. Fatigue, in contrast, had a distinct profile: it was the strongest predictor of academic stress and showed weaker but significant associations with lower engagement and higher dropout intentions. Notably, GPA and actual study hours showed very weak associations with both constructs. The study was well-powered (a priori G*Power analysis for r=.30 at alpha=.001) and used careful multiple-comparison corrections.
clinical_insight: For therapists, this study's distinction between apathy and fatigue has direct implications for assessment and treatment planning. When an adult student presents with academic struggles, clinicians should assess whether the primary difficulty is one of diminished drive and interest (apathy) versus one of exhaustion and overwhelm (fatigue). For apathy, evidence-based interventions include behavioral activation — scheduling pleasurable activities first to rebuild approach motivation before tackling academic tasks, values clarification to reconnect with personally meaningful goals, and addressing negative self-beliefs that sustain disengagement. For fatigue, interventions should focus on sleep hygiene, energy conservation strategies including strategic rest periods, activity pacing, and cognitive restructuring around the meaning of effort (since perceived stress was the strongest fatigue correlate, addressing catastrophic interpretations of workload may be key). The finding that apathy was the strongest predictor of study satisfaction has a particularly important implication: universities using student satisfaction surveys as quality indicators may be confounded by the trait composition of their student body, and individual-level satisfaction may be less amenable to institutional change than assumed.
relevant_for: Therapists and academic counsellors working with mature students, CBT practitioners addressing motivation and fatigue, clinicians supporting adult learners in higher education.

title: Excessive switching in OCD and paranoia arises from different deficits in belief-updating
authors: Philip R. Corlett, Charlotte Freeland, Santiago Castiello de Obeso, Praveen Suthaharan, Christopher Pittenger
summary: Using a probabilistic reversal learning task, this study demonstrates that excessive belief switching in OCD and paranoia arises from fundamentally different cognitive deficits. Individuals with OCD show instability in learning from positive feedback, while those with paranoia show instability in learning from negative feedback. These distinct computational signatures suggest that OCD and paranoia, despite sharing surface-level features of cognitive inflexibility, are maintained by different belief-updating mechanisms.
clinical_insight: The identification of distinct belief-updating signatures for OCD versus paranoia has direct implications for differential diagnosis and treatment planning. In clinical practice, OCD and paranoid ideation can present similarly, particularly when OCD involves harm-related obsessions that resemble paranoid fears.
