Emotional abuse specifically attenuated normative developmental increases in punishment learning rate (FDR < .05), meaning emotionally abused adolescents showed blunted learning from negative feedback compared to typically-developing peers. Physical abuse attenuated normative increases in both go bias (general tendency to engage) and a summary score of reward learning, indicating reduced behavioral engagement and reward sensitivity. Crucially, adversity did not predict differences in net task success, suggesting that abused youth reach similar behavioral outcomes through compensatory but divergent learning strategies. Broad composite adversity measures obscured these subtype-specific effects, highlighting the need for fine-grained characterization in both research and clinical assessment.
clinical_insight: The dissociable effects of emotional versus physical abuse on learning processes have important implications for case formulation and treatment planning. Adolescents with emotional abuse histories may have specific difficulty learning from negative consequences — a pattern that could manifest as repeating maladaptive behaviors despite apparent awareness of repercussions. This aligns with clinical observations of patients who seem resistant to behavioral feedback. In contrast, physically abused adolescents may show more generalized motivational withdrawal. Clinicians should assess specific adversity subtypes rather than relying on composite trauma scores, as the intervention targets differ: emotionally abused youth may benefit from cognitive restructuring around feedback interpretation, while physically abused youth may need motivational enhancement and reward-based engagement strategies. The finding that abused youth achieve similar outcomes through different strategies also suggests inherent resilience that can be leveraged therapeutically.
relevant_for: Trauma-focused clinicians, adolescent psychologists, and researchers studying adversity-specific mechanisms in psychopathology.

title: Agency in Post-traumatic Stress Disorder: Implications for Therapy
authors: Adrien et al.
summary: This paper proposes an enactive framework for understanding agency deficits in PTSD, linking peritraumatic dissociation to disrupted sense of control. The authors provide a roadmap for agency-based therapies and discuss how sensorimotor technologies such as gesture sonification could help restore agency during trauma recovery.
clinical_insight: The enactive perspective offers clinicians a principled way to understand why traditional exposure alone may fail when patients lack foundational agency. Sensorimotor approaches could complement phase-based trauma treatments, particularly for dissociative PTSD presentations. However, the framework remains theoretical and awaits empirical validation.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with dissociative PTSD and complex trauma seeking somatic adjuncts to phase-based treatment

title: Bringing together health and education with lived experience to co-produce a new pathway of assessment and support for Developmental Coordination Disorder
authors: Lucy H. Eddy, Cara E. Staniforth, Maryam I. Shuaib, Nell Schofield, Nat K. Merrick, Jade Jukes, Rebecca Murray
summary: Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD) affects approximately 5-6% of school-age children but remains significantly under-diagnosed, with existing assessment pathways described as convoluted and difficult to navigate. This study used a Double Diamond design approach (diverge-converge cycles) in a half-day co-production workshop with 13 stakeholders spanning lived experience, paediatrics, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, school leadership, SEN teaching, school nursing, and inequality management. Thematic analysis of workshop discussions and post-workshop questionnaires identified consensus on a new four-element DCD pathway: (1) identifying and mobilising key leaders across health and education systems; (2) embedding evidence-based DCD screening tools in routine educational assessments; (3) creating clear, simplified referral routes that reduce the current multi-step complexity; and (4) establishing joint health-education oversight to monitor pathway fidelity. Participants rated the co-produced pathway as highly acceptable but identified potential barriers including resource constraints, professional boundary issues between health and education sectors, and variability in school-level capacity. The study demonstrates that structured co-production with diverse stakeholders can generate practical, systems-level solutions for neurodevelopmental assessment pathways.
clinical_insight: The identified pathway offers a practical template for clinical services working to improve DCD identification, particularly in underserved populations where current diagnostic delays average 4-5 years. The emphasis on embedding screening within educational settings rather than relying solely on clinical referral is noteworthy — many children with DCD never present to clinical services but are visible in schools. Clinicians involved in neurodevelopmental assessment should advocate for cross-sector collaboration and consider whether their local pathways replicate the barriers identified here. The co-production methodology itself is also instructive: including lived experience alongside professionals generated solutions that single-sector working groups typically miss, particularly around reducing pathway complexity and addressing inequalities in access.
relevant_for: Neurodevelopmental assessment teams, paediatric occupational therapists, educational psychologists, and service designers working on DCD or similar neurodevelopmental pathways.

title: Change of Threat Expectancy as Mechanism of Exposure Therapy: Evidence from 8484 Exercises of 605 Patients
authors: Pittig et al.
summary: Using 8,484 exposure records from 605 patients across anxiety disorders, this study found that the learning rate (how effectively expectancy violation transfers into belief change) and expectancy change magnitude predicted treatment outcome, not violation alone.
clinical_insight: Clinicians should track whether threat beliefs actually update between sessions, not just whether patients complete exposures. Patients showing compliance without belief change may need cognitive restructuring or inhibitory learning strategies. A limitation is the naturalistic design without a control group.
relevant_for: CBT therapists conducting exposure-based treatment for anxiety disorders optimizing learning processes

title: Regulation Without Regulating? Exploring Misalignment Between Self-Reported Emotion Regulation Attempts and Strategy Use
authors: Aya Uchida, Katharine Greenaway, Valentina Bianchi, Maya Tamir, Elise Kalokerinos
summary: Across three studies (total N > 600), this pre-registered investigation examined whether people who report regulating their emotions actually use specific regulation strategies. Study 1 (N=242) found that 72% of participants who reported attempting to regulate their emotions during a negative mood induction did not subsequently use any of the eight measured cognitive reappraisal or distraction strategies, as captured by both open-ended responses and forced-choice selection. Study 2 (N=218) conceptually replicated this misalignment using a different mood induction and expanded strategy set, showing the effect was not task-specific. Study 3 (N=203) demonstrated that the misalignment was not explained by regulation success, motivation, or difficulty ratings. Computational modeling revealed that self-reported regulation attempts predicted increased positive affect change only when participants actually deployed strategies; mere reported intention without strategy use had no effect on mood. The authors propose that self-report measures of emotion regulation conflate intention with execution, leading to systematic overestimation of regulatory behavior in both research and clinical contexts.
clinical_insight: This finding has significant implications for clinical assessment and therapy outcomes measurement. Many widely-used measures (e.g., ERQ, CERQ) ask patients whether they typically use certain regulation strategies, but this research suggests such self-reports may capture intentions rather than actual behavior. In CBT and other therapies that target emotion regulation, clinicians should be aware that patients may genuinely believe they are using techniques while failing to implement them effectively. Therapeutic homework that includes specific behavioral tracking (e.g., momentary strategy use logs) may provide more accurate assessment than retrospective questionnaires. The dissociation between intention and execution also parallels clinical observations of patients who understand regulation concepts intellectually but struggle with in-the-moment application — suggesting that therapy should focus on building procedural skill and automaticity rather than just conceptual understanding.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners, emotion regulation researchers, and clinicians using self-report measures of coping and regulation in assessment or outcome monitoring.

title: Understanding CBT for Psychosis Through the Predictive Coding Framework
authors: Sheffield et al.
summary: This paper integrates CBTp with predictive coding from computational neuroscience, proposing that delusions persist through failed extinction learning and inappropriate reconsolidation of delusional priors, while CBTp works by strengthening non-delusional priors via behavioral experiments.
clinical_insight: Bridging CBTp with predictive coding gives therapists mechanistic language for why behavioral experiments work. The framework suggests spaced retrieval of non-delusional experiences may be more important than massed exposure. The theory remains empirically untested, which is a significant blind spot.
relevant_for: Psychologists and psychiatrists delivering CBTp who want deeper understanding of belief-update mechanisms

title: Anxiety modulates sensitivity and response bias in the AX CPT task
authors: Garima Joshi, Bhoomika R. Kar
summary: This study investigated how anxiety differentially affects proactive and reactive cognitive control using the AX Continuous Performance Task (AX-CPT) across two experiments. In Experiment 1a, healthy young adults grouped into low, medium, and high trait anxiety categories showed that high trait anxious participants maintained intact overall task performance and contextual sensitivity, but exhibited stronger cue-based expectancy bias (B_dprime = .73 vs .50 for low anxiety) and greater susceptibility to probe-level interference. This suggests high trait anxiety enhances strategic reliance on predictive cues without impairing the proactive control mechanism itself. In Experiment 1b, participants with generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) were compared directly with high trait anxious participants. The GAD group demonstrated fundamentally different patterns: reduced contextual sensitivity (A_prime = .84 vs .90), lower cue and probe discriminability, and poorer overall accuracy (d_prime = 2.14 vs 2.86), despite comparable strategic response bias. The authors argue that high trait anxiety and GAD involve qualitatively distinct cognitive control alterations: trait anxiety modulates the strategic weighting of contextual cues, while GAD is associated with a generalized degradation in the fidelity of context representation, suggesting a threshold effect at the clinical disorder level.
clinical_insight: The qualitative distinction between trait anxiety and GAD in cognitive control has direct implications for case formulation and treatment targeting. Patients with high trait anxiety but without GAD may have intact cognitive control capacity but strategically over-rely on predictive cues — a pattern that could manifest as hypervigilance to threat cues in social situations while maintaining adequate overall functioning. In contrast, GAD patients appear to have a more fundamental deficit in maintaining and using contextual information, which could underlie the pervasive worry characteristic of the disorder. For CBT, this suggests that cognitive restructuring targeting cue-interpretation biases may be sufficient for high trait anxiety, while GAD may require more intensive cognitive control training alongside standard therapeutic approaches. The AX-CPT paradigm could also serve as a potential cognitive marker for differentiating normal-range anxiety from clinical GAD.
relevant_for: Anxiety clinicians, neuropsychologists, and researchers studying cognitive control in anxiety spectrum conditions.

title: The Efficacy of Attentional Bias Modification for Anxiety: A Registered Replication
authors: Pond et al.
summary: This registered replication of Hazen et al. (2009) used a Bayesian approach to revisit ABM for GAD, also quantifying potential demand effects. Results add to growing evidence that ABM shows inconsistent efficacy for alleviating anxiety symptoms.
clinical_insight: This reinforces that clinicians should not divert patients from established treatments toward ABM as a standalone intervention. The debate now centers on whether ABM might work as an adjunct, which remains untested.
relevant_for: Clinicians and researchers interested in cognitive bias modification as a complement to standard anxiety treatments

title: What do people believe about artificial intelligence chatbots for mental health support? Evidence from Greece
authors: Aglaia Katsiroumpa, Parisis Gallos, Paschalina Lialiou, Ioannis Moisoglou, Olga Galani, Panagiota Peleka, Angeliki Triantafillaki, Maria Tsiachri, Petros Galanis
summary: This cross-sectional study (conducted January 2026) examined public attitudes toward AI chatbots for mental health support using a convenience sample in Greece. Two validated instruments were administered: the Artificial Intelligence in Mental Health Scale (AIMHS) measuring attitudes toward AI mental health chatbots specifically, and the Artificial Intelligence Attitude Scale (AIAS-4) measuring general attitudes toward AI. Results showed moderate positive attitudes toward AI chatbots for mental health (AIMHS mean scores in the neutral-to-positive range), with a significant positive correlation between AIMHS and AIAS-4 scores (r = .56, p < .001), indicating that general AI acceptance partially transfers to the mental health domain. Multivariable regression revealed that male gender (beta = .12, p < .05), higher education (beta = .15, p < .001), and older age (beta = .14, p < .01) were independently associated with more positive attitudes toward AI mental health chatbots. Daily social media use and digital competence predicted more positive general AI attitudes but did not significantly predict AI mental health chatbot attitudes when other variables were controlled. The authors conclude that while the Greek public shows moderate acceptance of AI in mental health, demographic factors create meaningful variation that should be considered in implementation strategies.
clinical_insight: For clinicians and services considering integrating AI chatbots into mental health care pathways, this study provides actionable data on patient populations most likely to engage. The moderate positive attitudes suggest readiness for carefully implemented AI tools, but the demographic variability highlights equity concerns: younger, less educated, and female patients — groups with high mental health need — showed less positive attitudes, potentially creating access disparities if AI tools become a primary point of contact. Clinicians should view AI chatbots as supplements to, not replacements for, therapeutic relationships. The finding that digital competence predicted general but not mental-health-specific AI attitudes suggests that trust in AI for sensitive psychological concerns involves factors beyond technical comfort — likely including concerns about privacy, empathy, and clinical validity that clinicians are well-positioned to address in psychoeducation.
relevant_for: Mental health service designers, clinicians considering AI-augmented practice, and implementation researchers.

title: Neuroception of Psychological Safety Scale (NPSS): Validation in UK Adults
authors: Cogan et al.
summary: Validated the NPSS (3-factor measure rooted in polyvagal theory) in 2,035 UK adults. Social engagement, compassion, and body sensations subscales showed acceptable-to-excellent reliability, good test-retest, and predicted correlations with PTSD, burnout, and wellbeing.
clinical_insight: The NPSS helps trauma therapists assess capacity for psychological safety before beginning trauma processing. Measuring safety neuroception could determine whether a patient needs more stabilization. Performance in clinical trauma populations is still needed.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists using polyvagal-informed or phase-based approaches assessing readiness for trauma processing

title: Identification of mental illness in maternity settings and care pathways to mental health services during pregnancy in a diverse urban UK population
authors: Sam Burton, Claire Wilson, Fiona Challacombe, Paul Seed, Jane Sandall, Louise Howard, eLIXIR Born in South London Partnership, Abigail Easter
summary: Using linked maternity and mental health records from 67,308 pregnancies (56,426 women) in South London (2018-2023), this study examined the pathway from antenatal mental health screening to actual service engagement. Three screening indicators were analyzed: positive Whooley depression questions, self-reported mental health conditions, and family history of severe mental illness. Among women already in contact with mental health services, 30.74% were not identified as at-risk by any of the three screening questions, while 38.39% were flagged by one, 24.73% by two, and 6.14% by all three. More screening indicators endorsed predicted higher referral and engagement odds (3 indicators vs 1: OR = 6.73 for referral). Significant ethnic disparities emerged: Asian and Asian British women had substantially lower odds of referral (OR = 0.51, 95% CI [0.42, 0.61]) and engagement (OR = 0.73, 95% CI [0.59, 0.91]) compared to White women. Black, African, Caribbean, and Black British women had higher referral odds (OR = 1.45) but lower engagement (OR = 0.73). Women requiring an interpreter had lower referral odds (OR = 0.69). Less deprived women (IMD quintile 5) had markedly lower referral rates (OR = 0.26 vs most deprived), suggesting socioeconomic detection bias.
clinical_insight: This large-scale data linkage study exposes critical gaps in the perinatal mental health care pathway that clinicians and service leads should address urgently. Nearly a third of women with known mental health contact were not detected by standard antenatal screening, indicating significant sensitivity limitations in current screening tools. The ethnic and language-based disparities in referral and engagement point to systemic rather than individual-level barriers: culturally insensitive screening approaches, insufficient interpreter services, and possible implicit bias in clinical decision-making. For perinatal mental health clinicians, these findings support routine re-screening beyond the booking appointment, advocacy for culturally adapted assessment tools, and systematic follow-up to ensure screening-positive results translate into actual care. The socioeconomic gradient in referral (more deprived women more likely referred) may reflect either higher prevalence or lower threshold for concern among clinicians working with disadvantaged populations — both warrant attention.
relevant_for: Perinatal mental health teams, midwifery leads, and health system administrators responsible for maternity mental health pathways.

title: Avatar-Based CBT/ACT Intervention for Female Orgasmic Disorder: RCT
authors: Vila et al.
summary: RCT (N=31) testing avatar-based CBT+ACT intervention in Second Life for female orgasmic disorder showed significant improvements across all outcomes with 100% intervention recovery vs. 0% control at follow-up.
clinical_insight: Virtual environments may reduce barriers to discussing sexuality. The CBT+ACT integration targeting both cognitive and experiential aspects is a strength. The 100% recovery rate raises demand-characteristic concerns warranting replication with larger samples.
relevant_for: Sex therapists and couples therapists working with female sexual dysfunction open to digital therapeutic tools

title: Self-reports of personality functioning and depression are practically interchangeable
authors: Josh Miller, Colin Vize, Nathaniel L. Phillips, Donald Lynam
summary: This commentary critically evaluates Kerber et al. (2026), which used bifactor-(S-1) modeling in a large EMA study (N > 16,000) to argue that personality functioning (PF) and depression (DEP) have distinct affective signatures with different treatment implications. Miller and colleagues present three counterarguments. First, the raw latent correlation between PF and DEP was r = .75-.87 — as high as convergent validity correlations used to establish measure equivalence — and the original (non-residualized) constructs produced nearly identical empirical profiles across outcomes (profile agreement r = .95). Second, the "perils of partialing" are demonstrated: residualized PF scores yielded an empirical profile unrelated to the original PF (r = -.06), with 53% of regression coefficients changing direction. The specific PF factor from bifactor models showed poor psychometric properties (ECVSS mean = .21; H mean = .39, well below the .70 threshold), indicating it is not a reliable or replicable construct. Third, interpretational confounding was evident across models, with PF factor loadings shifting by over .30 between analyses. The authors conclude that current PF measures are essentially markers of general distress and negative self-regard, and that claims of distinct treatment implications for PF versus depression are unsupported by the data.
clinical_insight: This commentary has direct implications for the ICD-11 and DSM-5 Alternative Model personality disorder frameworks, both of which position personality functioning as the central distinguishing criterion. If PF measures are practically interchangeable with depression measures (r = .87), their diagnostic utility for differentiating personality disorders from other conditions is fundamentally undermined. For clinicians using personality functioning assessments (e.g., OPD-SQS, LPFS), this research suggests that elevated scores may primarily reflect general distress rather than personality pathology per se. Treatment decisions should not be based on distinctions between PF and depression as measured by current instruments. The broader methodological critique of bifactor partialing in clinical measurement also serves as a caution for researchers interpreting residualized constructs in treatment outcome studies.
relevant_for: Personality disorder researchers, clinicians using the ICD-11 or DSM-5 AMPD frameworks, and psychometricians working with bifactor models.

title: Predicting Depression and Anxiety from Temporal Rumination Features
authors: Hoebeke, Heeren
summary: ESM study (4 daily assessments, 2 weeks, N=40) found inertia of thought negativity predicted 67.2% of depression variance, outperforming trait rumination. Combined temporal and trait measures worked best. Moment-to-moment variability did not add predictive value.
clinical_insight: Inertia (stickiness) of negative thinking, not variability, predicts depression, supporting interventions targeting cognitive rigidity such as behavioral activation, cognitive defusion, or rumination postponement. Anxiety was better predicted by content features than temporal dynamics, suggesting different targets per disorder. Small sample warrants caution.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists treating depression who want to refine rumination interventions by targeting cognitive inertia

title: The Expression of Self-Conscious Emotions and Their Motivational and Communicative Function in Early Development
authors: Milica Nikolic, Robert Hepach
summary: This narrative review synthesizes emerging evidence on the developmental emergence of self-conscious emotions (embarrassment, guilt, shame, and pride) in infancy and early childhood. Drawing on advanced methodologies including manual and automated micro-coding of non-verbal behavior, depth sensor imaging, and physiological measures, the review documents that self-conscious emotions may be observed earlier than previously assumed — potentially within the first months and years of life. The evidence suggests these emotions serve dual functions from early on: motivating socially appropriate behaviors (e.g., guilt promoting helping and reparative actions even in toddlers) and communicating adherence to social norms (e.g., embarrassment signaling awareness of norm violations). Longitudinal data indicate that individual differences in early self-conscious emotion expression predict later social competence and prosocial behavior. The review highlights that guilt and shame, while often conflated, show divergent developmental trajectories and social consequences — guilt tends to motivate reparative action while shame is more associated with social withdrawal. The authors call for more experimental and longitudinal research, integration of multi-modal data streams, and naturalistic paradigms to advance the field.
clinical_insight: Understanding the normative development of self-conscious emotions provides essential reference points for clinical work with both children and adults. In young children, atypical patterns of guilt, shame, or empathy may signal early risk for psychopathology: excessive shame is linked to later depression and social anxiety, while blunted guilt responses are associated with conduct problems and callous-unemotional traits. For adult clinical practice, the developmental perspective informs case formulation — patients with personality disorders, depression, or social anxiety often present with distorted self-conscious emotion processing (e.g., pervasive shame, absence of healthy guilt, or pride deficits) that may trace back to early attachment experiences. The review's emphasis on the motivational and communicative functions of these emotions also supports therapeutic approaches that help patients differentiate adaptive guilt (which prompts repair) from maladaptive shame (which prompts withdrawal), a distinction central to compassion-focused therapy and schema therapy.
relevant_for: Developmental psychopathologists, child mental health clinicians, and therapists working with shame and guilt in adult populations.

title: Specific, Not Global, Disruptions in Attentional Ability Distinguish Risk for Suicide Ideation from Suicide Attempts: Results of a Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Benau, Yirdong
summary: A systematic review and meta-analysis (12 studies, 26 effect sizes, N=995) examining neurocognitive attentional markers that differentiate individuals with suicidal ideation from those who have progressed to suicide attempts. People with SA history exhibited greater error rates yet more adaptive reaction time patterns compared to those with SI only, with task-specific differences across Stroop-like and Trail Making tasks.
clinical_insight: For clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments, this meta-analysis provides evidence that specific attentional deficits may be more precise markers of transition from ideation to action than broad cognitive impairment. This supports incorporating targeted attentional assessments into suicide risk formulation, potentially improving identification of high-risk patients. The findings also suggest attentional training interventions could be explored as a novel avenue for suicide prevention.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments and those developing targeted cognitive interventions for suicide prevention

title: Current distress does not moderate the efficacy of cognitive reappraisal
authors: Pending
summary: Investigates whether the efficacy of cognitive reappraisal — a core technique in CBT — is moderated by the individual's current level of distress. Using experimental methods, the authors test whether cognitive reappraisal works equally well regardless of how distressed a person is in the moment, challenging assumptions about when emotion regulation strategies should be deployed in therapy.
clinical_insight: Clinicians often hesitate to use cognitive reappraisal with highly distressed clients, assuming it may be less effective during acute distress. This study suggests that distress level does not significantly moderate reappraisal efficacy, supporting the use of CBT techniques even during sessions where clients present with high emotional arousal. This finding encourages therapists to maintain consistent application of reappraisal-based interventions rather than switching strategies based on momentary distress intensity.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners working with anxiety and mood disorders

title: Adaptive Learning From Outcome Contingencies in Eating-Disorder Risk Groups
authors: Pike et al.
summary: Investigates adaptive learning from outcome contingencies in eating-disorder risk groups (n=82: recovered anorexia, high symptom, healthy controls) using a reinforcement learning task with eye-tracking. Unexpectedly, the recovered anorexia group showed elevated learning rate adjustment compared to controls, with reduced pupil dilation to volatile rewards, suggesting a complex relationship between eating disorders and cognitive inflexibility involving noradrenergic mechanisms.
clinical_insight: The reinforcement learning framework offers clinicians a mechanistic understanding of why patients with eating disorders struggle to update maladaptive beliefs about food, weight, and shape despite contradictory evidence. The unexpected finding of elevated learning rate adjustment in recovered anorexia suggests cognitive inflexibility in eating disorders may be state-dependent rather than trait-like, informing treatment approaches that explicitly target belief updating. The noradrenergic finding opens potential pharmacological adjunct avenues.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders, particularly those working with patients who show limited response to standard CBT-E

title: Memory-Based Learning Disabilities: A Syndrome Without a Pigeonhole. Towards a Mechanism-Based Diagnostic Framework
authors: Pending
summary: Proposes a new mechanistic diagnostic framework for memory-based learning disabilities (MBLD), arguing that current diagnostic categories (such as specific learning disorder in DSM-5) fail to capture the heterogeneity of memory-related learning difficulties. The paper draws on clinical cases involving trauma, dissociation, and chronic conditions to illustrate how memory impairments can manifest across multiple diagnostic boundaries.
clinical_insight: Many clients presenting with learning difficulties do not fit neatly into existing diagnostic categories. This framework helps clinicians recognize that memory-based learning disabilities may co-occur with and be maintained by factors like chronic stress, trauma, and dissociation. Assessment should include thorough memory profiling rather than relying solely on IQ-achievement discrepancy models, enabling more targeted intervention planning.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and clinicians assessing learning disabilities in adults

title: Is Symptom Outcome the Whole Story? – A Multilevel Meta-Analysis of Systemic Therapy for Adults Including Family System Functioning
authors: Niels Braus, Christoph Flückiger, Johanna Wichmann, Christian Frankman, Christina Hunger-Schoppe
summary: Conducts a multilevel meta-analysis of systemic therapy for adults that goes beyond individual symptom outcomes to include family system functioning, relationship satisfaction, and communication patterns, examining whether systemic therapy produces change at the family system level beyond individual symptom improvement.
clinical_insight: Meta-analytic evidence supports systemic therapy as effective across a range of presenting problems, suggesting clinicians should consider involving family or relational systems even when the identified patient presents with individual symptoms. Systemic interventions can enhance and sustain outcomes beyond what individual treatment achieves alone.
relevant_for: Family and couples therapists, and individual therapists considering systemic referral options

title: From backlog to blind spots: why neurodevelopmental pathway reform must start with system mapping
authors: Pending
summary: An opinion piece arguing that neurodevelopmental assessment pathways (for autism and ADHD) are failing due to systemic issues that go beyond simple capacity constraints. Using system mapping methodology, the authors identify how current referral-to-diagnosis pathways create blind spots that particularly disadvantage certain populations, and propose a systems-level reform approach.
clinical_insight: Understanding the systemic failures in neurodevelopmental diagnostic pathways helps clinicians contextualize the long wait times their clients experience and identify where advocacy can be most effective. The system mapping approach reveals that simply adding more assessors will not solve the crisis — the pathway itself needs redesign. Clinicians can use these insights to set realistic expectations with clients and to contribute to service-level improvement efforts.
relevant_for: Clinicians involved in autism and ADHD assessment pathways

title: A Pilot Study of Twice-Weekly Group-Based Written Exposure Therapy for Veterans in Residential Substance Use Treatment: Effects on PTSD and Depressive Symptoms
authors: Natalia Van Doren, Fang Hsi Chang, Amanda Nguyễn, Kevin R. McKenna, Derek D. Satre, Shannon Wiltsey Stirman
summary: This pilot integrated 5-session group-based Written Exposure Therapy into a 28-day VA residential SUD program for 48 veterans with comorbid PTSD-SUD. With 92% completing at least 3 sessions and significant pre-post reductions in PTSD (PCL-5) and depressive (PHQ-9) symptoms, the study demonstrates that an abbreviated trauma intervention can be feasibly delivered alongside SUD treatment.
clinical_insight: The comorbidity of PTSD and SUD creates a treatment dilemma: standard trauma therapies require 12-16 sessions, but residential programs typically last 28 days. WET's brevity (5 sessions, no homework, minimal training) makes it one of the few trauma interventions that realistically fits within existing program structures. The 92% retention rate is notably high. The lack of a control group limits causal inference.
relevant_for: Clinicians in residential SUD treatment, dual-diagnosis programs, and trauma-informed veteran care

title: The Role of Interoceptive Awareness and Relationship Satisfaction in Female Orgasm Experience
authors: Pending
summary: Examines how interoceptive awareness — the ability to perceive internal bodily signals — interacts with relationship satisfaction to predict female orgasm experience. Using standardized measures of interoception and relationship quality, the study investigates whether body awareness plays a mechanistic role in sexual functioning beyond relational factors alone.
clinical_insight: Sexual difficulties are common presenting concerns in therapy yet often treated primarily through relational interventions. This research suggests that interoceptive awareness is a distinct predictor of sexual satisfaction, pointing to body-focused interventions (such as mindfulness-based or sensate focus approaches) as complementary to couples work. Clinicians treating sexual dysfunction should assess interoceptive abilities and consider incorporating body awareness exercises into treatment plans.
relevant_for: Sex therapists and couples counselors addressing sexual dysfunction

title: Non-suicidal self-injury as a low-effort strategy for avoiding negative affect
authors: Chloe Roske, Regina Musicaro, Peter Franz
summary: In 149 adults (52% with NSSI history), this study examined whether effort discounting underlies NSSI. NSSI frequency was associated with perceiving non-NSSI regulation strategies as more effortful, and effort discounting was linked to greater avoidant coping. However, avoidant strategy use itself was not independently associated with NSSI frequency.
clinical_insight: This study reframes NSSI through behavioral economics: self-injury may be selected because it requires less cognitive effort than alternatives like cognitive restructuring or seeking social support. For DBT practitioners, this maps onto the distress tolerance module — if patients perceive alternative skills as too effortful, skills training alone may be insufficient. The cross-sectional design limits causal inference.
relevant_for: DBT and ACT practitioners working with adults who engage in NSSI, particularly those with comorbid BPD features

title: The Fearbase: a dynamically growing database and collaborative consortium for experimental work on human fear conditioning research
authors: Pending
summary: Introduces The Fearbase, an open collaborative database for human fear conditioning research. The paper describes the database architecture, the consortium of contributing labs, and demonstrates how pooled data from fear conditioning paradigms can advance understanding of anxiety mechanisms, treatment response prediction, and individual differences in fear learning and extinction.
clinical_insight: Fear conditioning and extinction mechanisms underpin exposure-based therapies for anxiety disorders. This database provides a resource for clinicians and researchers to understand individual differences in fear learning that predict treatment outcomes. Clinicians using exposure therapy can reference Fearbase findings to inform case conceptualization — for instance, understanding that some patients show enhanced fear acquisition or impaired extinction, which may indicate need for augmented exposure protocols or adjunctive pharmacotherapy.
relevant_for: Researchers and clinicians specializing in anxiety treatment and exposure therapy

title: Interoceptive Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Attention are Associated with Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors
authors: Azure Reid-Russell, Matthew K. Nock
summary: This study of 450 adults examines three dimensions of interoception (accuracy, sensitivity, and attention) in relation to suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Individuals with recent suicidal thoughts and behaviors showed lower interoceptive accuracy, difficulty perceiving lower-intensity body sensations, and heightened attention to internal signals. These interoceptive differences persisted after controlling for depression and anxiety, suggesting they represent a distinct risk pathway for suicidality.
clinical_insight: The identification of interoceptive dysfunction as an independent risk factor for suicidality, separate from depression and anxiety, opens a new assessment and intervention pathway. For therapists conducting suicide risk assessments, incorporating brief interoceptive measures could improve risk stratification. For body-based therapists using somatic therapies, sensorimotor psychotherapy, or yoga-informed approaches, this provides empirical support for interventions that rebuild interoceptive awareness as a suicide prevention strategy. The tension between interoceptive accuracy (objective performance) and interoceptive sensitivity (subjective awareness) is clinically important: some patients may be highly attuned to body sensations but misinterpret them catastrophically, a pattern that is particularly relevant to panic disorder and health anxiety comorbid with suicidality. A blind spot is the cross-sectional design, which cannot establish whether interoceptive deficits precede suicidality or result from it, limiting causal inferences for treatment planning.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments and those using body-based or somatic approaches who want to understand the interoceptive dimensions of suicidal risk

title: Predicting Effects of a Computational Empathetic Bot
authors: Pending
summary: Investigates whether an AI-powered empathetic chatbot can produce measurable effects on user psychological states. The study designs a computational empathetic agent and tests its impact on anxiety, stress, and perceived support, exploring the potential for digital tools to extend therapeutic reach in mental health care delivery.
clinical_insight: AI chatbots are increasingly being deployed in mental health settings, yet evidence for their clinical efficacy remains limited. This study contributes to the evidence base by examining what specific components of computational empathy produce measurable psychological effects. Clinicians considering recommending digital mental health tools to clients can use these findings to evaluate which chatbot features are likely to be beneficial versus potentially harmful.
relevant_for: Clinicians interested in digital mental health interventions and AI-assisted therapy tools

title: Cognitive performance in functional neurological disorder: A systematic review and meta-analysis
authors: L.S. Merritt Millman, Isobel A Williams, Johannes Jungilligens, Susannah Pick
summary: A systematic review of 56 studies with 2260 FND patients found mixed and inconsistent evidence for objective cognitive deficits across executive functioning, attention, memory, and processing speed domains. Only 24% of studies assessed performance validity. Meta-analyses revealed that functional seizure patients actually outperformed epilepsy patients on naming and long-term memory tasks, with no significant differences in working memory or cognitive flexibility. Common confounds included demographic differences, medication effects, and intellectual functioning disparities between groups.
clinical_insight: The finding that FND patients do not consistently show objective cognitive deficits — and may even outperform epilepsy patients on some measures — is crucial for case conceptualization. Clinicians should be cautious about attributing functional symptoms to cognitive impairment without formal neuropsychological testing and performance validity assessment. The remarkably low rate of performance validity testing (24%) is a methodological blind spot that limits the interpretability of the entire literature. For therapists working with FND patients, this supports a model where symptoms are better understood through a biopsychosocial framework rather than a cognitive deficit model, and where treatment should focus on functional recovery and symptom management rather than cognitive rehabilitation.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and therapists working with functional neurological disorder, conversion disorder, and psychosomatic presentations

title: Subjective Effects and Characteristics of Salvia Divinorum Use from a Retrospective Largescale Survey
authors: Pending
summary: A large-scale retrospective survey examining the subjective effects, patterns of use, and user characteristics of Salvia divinorum — a potent naturally occurring hallucinogen. The study documents the range of experiential effects including dissociative episodes, explores dose-response relationships, and examines contextual factors surrounding use.
clinical_insight: Salvia divinorum use is under-researched compared to other hallucinogens, yet clinicians may encounter patients who use it. Understanding its characteristic effects — particularly the dissociative and hallucinogenic properties — helps in differential diagnosis and psychoeducation. The dose-response and contextual data can inform harm reduction counseling and help clinicians distinguish between Salvia-related experiences and other conditions such as psychosis or dissociative disorders.
relevant_for: Addiction specialists and clinicians working with substance use and hallucinogen experiences

title: Temporal Relations Among Changes in Activation, Avoidance, and Anxiety  in the Unified Protocol
authors: Doug Terrill, Matthew W. Southward, Madeline Kushner, Lindsey Crowley, Riley Feddock, Shannon Sauer-Zavala
summary: Using random intercept cross-lagged panel models, this study examines the temporal dynamics between behavioral activation, experiential avoidance, and anxiety symptoms during outpatient administration of the Unified Protocol (UP), a transdiagnostic CBT-based treatment. The study tests whether decreases in avoidance drive increases in activation, whether increases in activation drive decreases in anxiety, or whether these changes occur simultaneously, informing the proposed mechanisms of change in transdiagnostic treatments.
clinical_insight: Understanding the temporal sequence of change mechanisms in transdiagnostic treatments like the Unified Protocol has direct implications for session pacing and treatment planning. If avoidance reduction precedes behavioral activation, clinicians should prioritize exposure and avoidance reduction early in treatment before pushing for behavioral activation. Conversely, if activation drives avoidance reduction, then scheduling pleasurable activities first may create momentum that makes exposure work more tolerable. The Unified Protocol is increasingly used in private practice for its flexibility across anxiety, depression, and OCD presentations, yet its proposed mechanisms of change have not been thoroughly tested. This study begins to close that gap. A blind spot is that session-by-session mechanisms may differ from week-by-week mechanisms, and the aggregated data used here may mask important within-session processes that clinicians observe directly, such as the emotional processing that occurs during exposure exercises.
relevant_for: Clinicians using the Unified Protocol, transdiagnostic CBT, or any emotion-focused therapy where activation and avoidance are central treatment targets

title: Neural discrimination of objects, faces, and print in dyslexic and typical readers: A steady-state visual evoked potential study
authors: Pending
summary: Uses steady-state visual evoked potentials (ssVEP) to investigate neural discrimination of objects, faces, and printed words in dyslexic versus typical readers. The study identifies specific neurodevelopmental processing differences that may underlie reading difficulties, contributing to a neural-level understanding of the dyslexia phenotype.
clinical_insight: This study provides neurobiological evidence that dyslexia involves atypical neural processing of print that can be distinguished from general visual or face processing deficits. For clinicians conducting dyslexia assessments, these findings support the use of neurophysiological measures as complementary to behavioral testing, and may help differentiate dyslexia from other neurodevelopmental conditions that affect reading.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and clinicians assessing dyslexia and neurodevelopmental disorders

title: High-threshold-low-tolerance: a model for the emotionality paradox in PTSD
authors: Nachshon Korem, Or Duek, Tobias Raphael Spiller, Ziv Ben-Zion, Ifat Levy, Ilan Harpaz-Rotem
summary: Using hierarchical Bayesian modeling of 1045 trauma-exposed individuals rating valenced images, this study found that individuals with probable PTSD exhibited a steeper emotional transition slope — shifting rapidly between neutral and negative emotional states. Emotional numbing severity significantly predicted this steeper slope, suggesting that numbing and emotional volatility are not opposing symptoms but mechanistically linked. The authors propose a high-threshold-low-tolerance model where emotional numbing narrows the window of emotional regulation, creating abrupt shifts to intense negative affect.
clinical_insight: This computational model directly challenges the common clinical separation of PTSDs emotional numbing and hyperarousal clusters by showing they are mechanistically intertwined. For trauma-focused therapists using EMDR, PE, or CPT, this reframing is significant: addressing numbing in treatment may simultaneously reduce emotional volatility, and vice versa. The model suggests that mindfulness-based emotion regulation strategies, which widen the window between neutral and negative states, could be particularly effective for patients with prominent numbing symptoms. A blind spot in current PTSD treatment protocols is the tendency to address numbing and reactivity as separate targets — this model argues for an integrated approach that targets the transition dynamic itself rather than individual symptom clusters.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists using EMDR, CPT, PE, or body-based approaches working with PTSD and CPTSD presentations involving emotional numbing

title: Exploring diagnosis of Developmental Coordination Disorder and Specific Developmental Disorder of Motor Function: a secondary data analysis using Connected Bradford
authors: Pending
summary: A secondary data analysis using the Connected Bradford dataset to explore diagnostic patterns of Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD) and Specific Developmental Disorder of Motor Function. The study examines comorbidity rates, demographic factors, and diagnostic pathways in a real-world clinical population.
clinical_insight: DCD is frequently underdiagnosed or misdiagnosed, particularly when it co-occurs with other neurodevelopmental conditions. This real-world data analysis helps clinicians recognize the typical comorbidity profiles and diagnostic patterns they are likely to encounter in practice, supporting earlier and more accurate identification of motor coordination difficulties in children and young people.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing neurodevelopmental conditions in children and adolescents

title: Potential Harms from Emphasizing Individual Factors Over Structural Factors in Cognitive Behavioral Therapy with Stigmatized Groups
authors: Isaac L. Ahuvia, Jessica L. Schleider
summary: This paper critically examines how standard CBT formulations, which emphasize individual cognitive and behavioral factors, may inadvertently pathologize or minimize the role of structural oppression (racism, homophobia, sexism) in the mental health of stigmatized groups. The authors argue that when CBT frames distress primarily as the product of maladaptive thoughts rather than rational responses to genuinely oppressive conditions, it risks reinforcing internalized stigma and undermining therapeutic trust with marginalized clients.
clinical_insight: For CBT practitioners working with marginalized populations, this paper raises essential questions about case formulation fidelity and cultural responsiveness. Rather than abandoning CBT, the authors advocate for integrating structural awareness into standard cognitive restructuring — for example, helping clients distinguish between genuinely distorted cognitions and accurate perceptions of discrimination. This tension between individual-level and structural-level explanations is one of the most significant debates in contemporary CBT, and clinicians who fail to address it risk iatrogenic effects. A blind spot is the lack of specific, manualized protocols for structurally-informed CBT, leaving practitioners with conceptual guidance but limited practical tools.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners working with racially, sexually, or otherwise marginalized clients in adult private practice

title: Subjective Experience of Stream of Consciousness Impairment in Three Patients with Severe Amnesia
authors: Pending
summary: A qualitative case study exploring the phenomenology of stream of consciousness impairment in three patients with severe amnesia. Through detailed interviews, the authors characterize how these patients experience disruptions in the continuous flow of conscious experience, contributing to understanding of consciousness disorders in clinical neuropsychology.
clinical_insight: Severe amnesia affects not just memory but the fundamental experience of conscious continuity. Understanding how patients subjectively experience stream of consciousness impairments helps clinicians provide more empathetic and informed care, and improves differential diagnosis between amnesia and other conditions that disrupt conscious experience such as dissociation, depersonalization, or psychotic disorganization.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and therapists working with memory disorders and brain injury

title: A DOCTORAL THESIS EXAMINING THE FIELD OF THERAPIST DRIFT IN COGNITIVE BEHAVIOURAL THERAPY, AND THE EXPLORATION OF META-COMPETENCE IN COGNITIVE BEHAVIOURAL THERAPY
authors: Denika Campbell-Lee, Dr. Stephen Barton, Mr Peter Armstrong
summary: This doctoral thesis combines a scoping review of therapist drift in CBT with an empirical study comparing meta-competence (the capacity to know why and when specific skills are needed) across trainee, recently-qualified, and experienced CBT therapists. Therapist drift, the gradual departure from evidence-based practice, is examined as a systemic problem rather than an individual failing. The empirical component identifies higher-order CBT skills that distinguish experienced therapists from trainees when managing complex atypical presentations.
clinical_insight: Therapist drift is one of the most under-acknowledged threats to treatment efficacy in routine practice. This thesis provides empirical evidence that meta-competence, the ability to flexibly adapt protocols to atypical presentations while maintaining fidelity to core principles, develops with experience but is not guaranteed by it. For CBT supervisors and training programs, the implication is that supervision should explicitly target meta-competence rather than just protocol adherence. The scoping review findings on drift are particularly relevant for private practitioners who work without regular supervision, where drift risk is highest. A blind spot is the lack of research on drift in third-wave therapies (ACT, DBT, schema therapy) where protocol flexibility is inherent rather than exceptional.
relevant_for: CBT therapists, clinical supervisors, and training program directors concerned with maintaining treatment fidelity and developing therapist meta-competence

title: A resting-state EEG functional connectivity study in informal caregivers of patients with various conditions
authors: Pending
summary: Uses resting-state EEG functional connectivity to investigate the neural correlates of caregiving burden in informal caregivers of patients with various conditions. The study examines how the chronic stress of caregiving is reflected in brain network connectivity patterns, providing neurobiological evidence for caregiver burnout.
clinical_insight: Informal caregivers represent a large but often overlooked clinical population at elevated risk for depression, anxiety, and burnout. This study provides objective neurobiological markers of caregiving stress that complement self-report measures. Clinicians working with caregiver populations can use these findings to validate caregivers' experiences and to advocate for early intervention, as neural changes may precede overt clinical symptoms.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with caregivers and chronic illness populations

title: Identifying higher-order CBT skills: Is there a difference between experienced and trainee therapists regarding meta-competence?
authors: Denika Campbell-Lee, Dr. Stephen Barton, Mr Peter Armstrong
summary: This empirical study identifies and compares higher-order CBT meta-competencies across three therapist groups: trainees, recently qualified, and experienced practitioners. Using therapist judgment tasks involving complex client scenarios where standard protocols do not map neatly onto presentations, the study isolates the skills that distinguish experienced therapists, particularly the capacity for flexible conceptualization and adaptive protocol modification.
clinical_insight: This is a rare empirical investigation into what actually distinguishes effective experienced therapists from competent novices beyond simple years of practice. The finding that meta-competence, specifically the ability to recognize when standard protocols need adaptation and how to accomplish that adaptation without losing fidelity, is a distinguishable skill set has implications for both training and supervision. For private practice clinicians working with complex comorbid presentations that rarely fit textbook protocols, this validates the intuitive clinical judgment that experienced therapists develop. The tension with manualized treatment approaches is clear: the field demands both fidelity and flexibility, yet training typically emphasizes the former while the latter is left to implicit learning. A blind spot is that this study examines CBT specifically, and whether similar meta-competencies exist and differ across other modalities (psychodynamic, schema therapy, IFS) remains unknown.
relevant_for: CBT clinicians and supervisors interested in understanding and developing advanced clinical reasoning beyond protocol adherence

title: Factors Shaping Autistic Adults' Experiences and Perceived Benefits of a Co-Designed VR Sensory Application
authors: Pending
summary: Investigates how autistic adults experience and benefit from a virtual reality sensory application that was co-designed with the autistic community. The study examines which features of the VR environment are most valued, how individual sensory profiles moderate the experience, and the perceived therapeutic potential of the tool for managing sensory overload.
clinical_insight: VR-based tools co-designed with neurodivergent individuals represent a promising adjunct to traditional therapy approaches for autism. Understanding which sensory features are most beneficial helps clinicians recommend specific digital tools and provides a model for involving service users in intervention design. The co-design methodology itself offers a template for creating other neurodivergent-affirming clinical resources.
relevant_for: Therapists working with autistic adults and those interested in technology-assisted interventions

title: Transdiagnostic cognitive biases in psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and network meta-analysis
authors: Katie M. Lavigne, Jiaxuan Deng, Delphine Raucher-Chéné, Adèle Hotte-Meunier, Chloé Voyer, Lisa Sarraf, Martin Lepage, Genevieve Sauve
summary: This systematic review and network meta-analysis examines cognitive biases — systematic tendencies in attention, memory, and interpretation — across multiple psychiatric disorders, testing whether these biases share common underlying mechanisms. The network approach maps how different biases relate to each other and to symptom severity across diagnostic categories, offering a data-driven taxonomy of cognitive dysfunction.
clinical_insight: The transdiagnostic perspective on cognitive biases has direct implications for case formulation and treatment planning across diagnostic boundaries. If biases like attentional threat bias, interpretation bias, and memory bias form interconnected networks, then targeting a central bias node could produce cascading improvements across multiple symptom domains. This supports the rationale for transdiagnostic treatments like the Unified Protocol that address cognitive processing broadly rather than disorder-specific content. The meta-analytic approach is methodologically strong, but the review is limited by the heterogeneity of bias measures across studies and the predominance of anxiety and depression samples.
relevant_for: Clinicians using transdiagnostic approaches (UP, CBT-E, DBT) who want to understand which cognitive biases to prioritize in treatment

title: Construct validity evidence for a Workplace Well-Being Questionnaire Battery adapted to Workers with Intellectual Disabilities (WWQBID)
authors: Pending
summary: Presents construct validity evidence for an adapted workplace well-being questionnaire specifically designed for workers with intellectual disabilities. The study addresses the critical gap in assessment tools for this population, demonstrating that the WWQBID reliably measures well-being dimensions in a group often excluded from standard psychometric research.
clinical_insight: Workers with intellectual disabilities are frequently assessed using tools not validated for their cognitive profile, leading to inaccurate measurement of well-being and mental health. The WWQBID provides clinicians and occupational psychologists with a validated alternative for assessing well-being in this population, improving the accuracy of needs assessments and intervention evaluation in supported employment settings.
relevant_for: Clinicians and psychologists assessing well-being in intellectual disability populations

title: Mapping Interpersonal Emotion Regulation in Everyday Life
authors: Anh Tran, Katharine Greenaway, Joanne Kostopoulos, Sarah T. O'Brien, Elise Kalokerinos
summary: Examines how contextual factors (social setting, task demands, time of day) influence the selection and effectiveness of emotion regulation strategies.
clinical_insight: Shows that the effectiveness of emotion regulation strategies varies substantially depending on context (social setting, task demands, time of day), challenging the assumption that certain strategies are universally adaptive. Therapists should teach clients flexible ER repertoires rather than promoting a fixed set of strategies, and use functional analysis to help clients match regulation approaches to the specific situational demands they face in daily life.
relevant_for: Emotion-focused therapists and DBT skills group facilitators

title: Psychometric Comparability of LLM-Based Digital Twins
authors: Pending
summary: Examines the psychometric comparability of LLM-based digital twins — AI models trained to simulate individual human response patterns on psychological measures. The study investigates whether these digital replicas can faithfully reproduce the psychometric properties of the individuals they model, with implications for clinical assessment simulation and research methodology.
clinical_insight: LLM-based digital twins have the potential to transform clinical assessment research by enabling large-scale simulation studies without recruiting participants. However, this study's findings on psychometric comparability are essential reading before clinicians or researchers consider using such tools. Understanding the limitations and validity of digital twins helps prevent over-reliance on AI simulations in clinical decision-making while identifying legitimate research applications.
relevant_for: Clinical researchers interested in AI applications for psychometric assessment

title: Putting the Vicious Cycle to the Test: Evidence for the Cognitive Behavioral Model of Persistent Somatic Symptoms from an Online Study
authors: Alexander Hans Josef Sahm, Michael Witthöft, Josef Bailer, Daniela Mier
summary: This study tests the complete cognitive-behavioral model of persistent somatic symptoms using an online survey of 2,114 participants. Rather than examining isolated components, the research evaluates the full vicious cycle model including predisposing, perpetuating, and precipitating factors and their interactions in explaining somatic symptom onset and endurance. Results provide evidence for the model's proposed pathways linking illness-related cognition, health anxiety, and attentional focus to symptom persistence.
clinical_insight: The CBT model of persistent somatic symptoms is widely used in clinical practice but has rarely been tested in its entirety. This large-scale validation provides empirical support for the complete model, giving CBT practitioners stronger footing when explaining treatment rationale to skeptical patients who attribute symptoms purely to physical causes. For therapists working with FND, somatic symptom disorder, or illness anxiety, this supports a formulation-based approach mapping specific perpetuating factors. The tension between this model and the emerging neurobiological understanding of FND is significant: patients and some neurologists may view psychological models as dismissive, making psychoeducation a critical early intervention. A blind spot is the COVID-19 pandemic data collection, which may have inflated health anxiety and somatic symptom reporting.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners working with somatic symptom disorder, FND, illness anxiety, and chronic pain where the cognitive-behavioral vicious cycle model informs treatment

title: Social Disconnection is Associated with Impaired Social Skills: Evidence from Behavioral Performance and Automated Facial Expression Analysis
authors: Pending
summary: Investigates the relationship between subjective social disconnection and objective measures of social skill, using both behavioral tasks and automated facial expression analysis. The study demonstrates that individuals who feel socially disconnected also show measurable impairments in social-cognitive performance and expressive behavior.
clinical_insight: Social disconnection is a transdiagnostic factor relevant to depression, social anxiety, psychosis, and personality disorders. This study provides objective evidence that subjective disconnection corresponds to measurable social skill deficits, supporting the clinical utility of social disconnection as a screening indicator. Clinicians can use these findings to justify social skills interventions and to track objective improvements alongside subjective reports of connection.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with social isolation across diagnostic categories

title: Pain before, during, and after non-suicidal self-injury: Findings from a large web study
authors: Ryan Carpenter, Johanna Hepp, Timothy J Trull
summary: In a large online survey of 1,630 individuals with NSSI histories, this study tests competing models of the role of pain in self-injury by examining pain before, during, and after NSSI acts. The research assesses pain experience descriptively and tests pre-registered hypotheses about factors explaining individual differences in NSSI pain, including dissociation, emotion regulation difficulties, and self-criticism, providing a comprehensive test of pain-onset, pain-offset, and analgesia models of NSSI.
clinical_insight: The competing models of pain in NSSI (pain as antecedent, consequence, or absent through analgesia) have direct implications for treatment. If pain onset drives NSSI, interventions should target the pre-existing emotional or physical pain preceding self-injury. If pain offset is the mechanism, treatment should focus on building alternative ways to achieve emotional relief. If analgesia is present, the task is to address dissociative processes that disconnect the person from their body. For DBT therapists teaching distress tolerance, understanding which pain model applies to each client determines whether to emphasize TIPP, alternative activities, or mindfulness to reduce dissociation. The tension between these models has persisted because most studies test only one. A blind spot is the reliance on retrospective self-report, which may be distorted by emotional state and time elapsed since episodes.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with NSSI in the context of BPD, complex trauma, or eating disorders, particularly those using DBT and schema therapy

title: Sex Bias in a Gold-Standard Autism Assessment: An Analysis of 19,569 Children and Adults
authors: Austin Wyman, Yuen Yvonne Yu, Chenhao Li, Elle Fox, Johnny Zhang, Caitlin C. Clements
summary:
This cross-sectional study examined sex-based differential item functioning (DIF) in the Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule (ADOS) across its full developmental and language range using a pooled sample of 19,569 individuals from four national databases (SSC, SPARK, AIC, and NDA). Participants ranged from 12 months to 75 years old (14,809 males, 4,760 females), and the analysis covered all eight module-language combinations (Toddler Module Low/High, Module 1 Low/High, Module 2 Low/High, Module 3, and Module 4). Using an IRT Graded Response Model with likelihood ratio tests and Benjamini-Hochberg correction, the study evaluated whether ADOS algorithm items function differently for males and females at equivalent levels of autistic trait severity.

Across all 112 algorithm items analyzed, 11 showed statistically significant sex DIF after multiple comparison correction. The critical finding was a clear developmental pattern: the Toddler Module, Module 1 Low Language, and Module 2 Low/High Language contained zero DIF items after correction, demonstrating that the ADOS is largely unbiased for younger children and those with limited language. Module 3 showed only one DIF item (TOPIC). In striking contrast, Module 4 — administered to verbally fluent individuals over age 16 — contained 7 DIF items (4 social communication, 3 RRBI) among 1,752 males and 731 females. On social communication items (EGEST, FACEE, CAFF, INSIG), females were consistently underscored, meaning they received lower scores than males with equivalent underlying autistic trait levels. On RRBI items (USENS, OMAN, TOPIC), females tended to be overscored. All effect sizes were small except STEREO in Module 1 High Language (medium), which requires replication. The authors propose that camouflaging — the compensatory masking of autistic traits that increases during adolescence and persists into adulthood — likely drives the underscoring pattern, and recommend that future ADOS revisions consider female-specific norms, supplementary camouflaging assessments for verbally fluent adults, and that clinicians can reassure families of younger females that ADOS results are likely valid.
clinical_insight: Clinicians conducting adult autism assessments should be aware that the ADOS-2 Module 4 systematically underestimates social communication difficulties in autistic females relative to males with equivalent trait levels. This is not a small or theoretical concern: it directly contributes to the well-documented under-identification of autistic women, who often receive incorrect diagnoses of personality disorders, eating disorders, or mood disorders before eventually being recognized as autistic. The practical implication is that a "below cutoff" ADOS-2 Module 4 result in a verbally fluent female patient should be interpreted with particular caution, especially when clinical history and self-report suggest autistic traits. Clinicians should consider supplementing the ADOS with validated camouflaging measures (such as the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire, CAT-Q) and gathering collateral developmental history. For younger females and those with less verbal language, the ADOS appears to function equitably, which is an important reassurance to communicate to families and multidisciplinary teams during feedback sessions.
relevant_for: Autism assessment clinicians, clinical psychologists conducting neurodevelopmental evaluations, diagnosticians working with adult female populations, and multidisciplinary teams involved in differential diagnosis between autism and personality disorders.

title: The Relationship Between Cognitive Phenotypes of Compulsivity and Impulsivity and Clinical Variables in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Aaron Thomas Clarke , Naomi Fineberg, Luca Pellegrini, Keith R. Laws
summary: Meta-analysis distinguishing compulsive and impulsive dimensions in OCD, examining their differential relationships with symptom clusters and treatment response.
clinical_insight: Distinguishing compulsive from impulsive dimensions in OCD helps clinicians tailor treatment: compulsive-dominant presentations may respond best to traditional ERP, while impulsive-dominant presentations may require additional impulse control strategies, distress tolerance training, and emotion regulation work alongside exposure to achieve full symptom remission.
relevant_for: OCD specialists and clinical psychologists conducting case formulation for treatment-resistant OCD

title: The Dual Lens Model of Behavior: A Conceptual Framework for Integrating Functional Behavior Assessment and Nervous System State
authors: Olga Sirbu
summary:
This conceptual paper introduces the Dual Lens Model of Behavior (DLM), a theoretical framework designed to unify traditional operant Functional Behavior Assessment (FBA) with internal nervous system state analysis. The author — a Board Certified Behavior Analyst — argues that standard FBA, which focuses exclusively on external environmental contingencies maintaining behavior (what the model terms "Lens 1"), fails to account for internal physiological, neurodevelopmental, and interoceptive variables that lower behavioral activation thresholds and drive initial behavioral responses (termed "Lens 2"). The DLM proposes that challenging behaviors emerge from the reciprocal interaction between external reinforcement variables and internal organismic state-regulation profiles, drawing on radical behaviorism, interoceptive neuroscience (Craig, 2002), allostatic predictive processing (Sterling, 2012), trauma-informed care frameworks (SAMHSA, 2014), and neuroception theory (Porges, 2004).

The model operationalizes internal vulnerabilities into five Nervous System Driver Domains: (1) Internal Body State (interoception, pain, metabolic depletion), (2) Sensory Processing (modulation, habituation, integration of stimuli), (3) Control and Predictability (cognitive certainty, transition stress), (4) Emotional State (autonomic flooding, affective dysregulation), and (5) Neurodevelopmental Load (executive function demands relative to cognitive capacity). A Behavior and State Alignment Matrix maps how each domain can masquerade as a standard operant function — for example, task refusal appearing as escape-maintained behavior may actually be driven by sensory overload or chronic gastrointestinal discomfort. The paper proposes four falsifiable hypotheses distinguishing reinforcement-driven, state-driven, interaction-driven, and iatrogenic escalation phenotypes, and outlines a four-step clinical assessment workflow: (1) standard FBA, (2) Nervous System State Screening using a proposed Dual Lens Behavior Assessment Checklist (DL-BAC), (3) State Manipulation Probes where targeted state-regulation supports are introduced while reinforcement contingencies are held constant, and (4) Interaction Analysis and Classification into behavioral archetypes. The author explicitly acknowledges limitations including domain overlap, difficulty of controlling confounds in naturalistic settings, and the need for empirical validation of the DL-BAC and the model's core predictions.
clinical_insight: This framework has immediate practical implications for any clinician — behavior analyst, occupational therapist, clinical psychologist, or psychiatrist — who encounters patients where standard operant interventions (extinction, differential reinforcement) produce limited results or paradoxical escalation. The key clinical takeaway is that before implementing consequence-based interventions, clinicians should systematically screen for internal nervous system drivers: chronic pain, sensory processing differences, autonomic dysregulation, and neurodevelopmental cognitive load. The paper's most consequential claim is Hypothesis 4 — that applying extinction protocols to state-driven or survival-based behavioral phenotypes can provoke dangerous physiological escalation with prolonged cortisol recovery cycles and iatrogenic behavioral bursts. For therapists working with neurodivergent adults, trauma survivors, or individuals with chronic health conditions, this model provides a structured justification for prioritizing sensory accommodations, co-regulation strategies, and medical evaluation before behavioral modification. The State Manipulation Probe methodology (introducing sensory or physiological supports while holding demands constant) is a practical, testable approach that clinicians can begin applying immediately in assessment and formulation.
relevant_for: Behavior analysts, occupational therapists, clinical psychologists, and psychiatrists working with neurodivergent populations, trauma survivors, or individuals with treatment-resistant challenging behaviors. Particularly relevant for clinicians who have observed extinction bursts or treatment non-response that may indicate underlying nervous system dysregulation.

title: Dynamics of narcissistic grandiosity and vulnerability in naturalistic and experimental settings
authors: Elizabeth A. Edershile, Anna Szücs, Alexandre Dombrovski, Aidan G.C. Wright
summary: This study integrates ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and experimental methods to examine how narcissistic grandiosity and vulnerability fluctuate in response to social feedback. Using naturalistic daily-life assessments alongside controlled lab paradigms involving status threats, the research maps the dynamic interplay between these two narcissistic states and tests theoretical accounts of rapid state-shifting in narcissism.
clinical_insight: The dynamic model of narcissism has significant implications for clinical work with personality-disordered clients. Rather than viewing grandiosity and vulnerability as stable traits, this EMA-based approach reveals them as rapidly fluctuating states triggered by interpersonal context. For therapists using schema therapy, psychodynamic approaches, or IFS with narcissistic patients, this supports pacing interventions around the clients current self-state rather than confronting grandiosity when vulnerability is accessible. The experimental finding that status threats catalyze state shifts has direct implications for therapeutic rupture and repair: challenges to the patients self-perception in session may trigger rapid defensive shifts that derail treatment if not anticipated. A blind spot is that most narcissism research still relies on non-clinical samples, and how these dynamics manifest in clinically diagnosed NPD patients, particularly those in private practice seeking help voluntarily, may differ substantially.
relevant_for: Therapists working with narcissistic personality pathology, particularly those using schema therapy, psychodynamic, or IFS approaches

title: Multi-System Brain-Body Mechanisms of Mental Health in Long-Term Meditation: Toward a Science of Advanced Meditation
authors: Idil Sezer, Terje Sparby, Kamlesh Chalise, Vanessa Naughton, Matthew Sacchet
summary:
This systematic review synthesizes evidence from 52 studies across four databases (PubMed, MEDLINE, PsycINFO, Embase; 1,215 records screened) examining the brain-body mechanisms through which long-term and advanced meditation practice influences mental health. Unlike most meditation reviews that focus on short-term interventions in novice populations, this review specifically targets sustained practice and advanced meditative states, analyzing findings across neuroimaging, electrophysiology, physiology, and molecular levels of analysis. Six interrelated mental health domains emerged: (1) general well-being, (2) interpersonal functioning, (3) emotion regulation, (4) sensory-affective uncoupling of pain, (5) stress resilience, and (6) psychiatric symptomatology and illness prevention.

For well-being, long-term meditators show elevated frontal alpha/theta EEG power, reduced self-referential negativity bias on ERP measures, and deactivation of default mode network regions during advanced insight meditation (Yang et al., 2025; Katyal et al., 2020).
