Self-reported well-being correlates with lifetime practice hours (Malipeddi et al., 2024; r > 5,500 hours average). For interpersonal functioning, loving-kindness meditators show reduced self-other P300 distinctions on EEG (Trautwein et al., 2016) and decreased precuneus grey matter density predictive of practice duration (Berkovich-Ohana et al., 2020), alongside heightened fronto-insular activation during compassion meditation.

For emotion regulation, key structural findings include preserved age-related grey matter in the orbitofrontal cortex and subgenual cingulate cortex (Kurth et al., 2021, 2023), increased right hippocampal volume (Luders et al., 2013), and reduced amygdala volume decline with age (Luders et al., 2021). Functionally, long-term meditators (mean 9,081 practice hours) show reduced amygdala reactivity to positive stimuli (Kral et al., 2018 RCT, n=97), and use acceptance-based deactivation of DMN regions rather than effortful amygdala down-regulation (Taylor et al., 2011). Pain uncoupling findings demonstrate that meditators report reduced pain unpleasantness with intact nociceptive reflexes, mediated by attenuated anticipatory insula activity (Lutz et al., 2013) and operating through non-opioid mechanisms (May et al., 2018; naloxone did not abolish analgesia).

Stress resilience findings include faster cortisol recovery post-Trier Social Stress Test (Gamaiunova et al., 2019), with acceptance as a mediator, higher melatonin and serotonin levels (Thambyrajah et al., 2023), and enhanced hippocampal/subicular volume protecting against stress-related atrophy (Kurth et al., 2015). Molecular markers show downregulation of inflammatory genes and upregulation of antiviral/antibody genes (Wenuganen et al., 2021; Kaliman et al., 2014), increased endocannabinoids and BDNF post-retreat (Sadhasivam et al., 2020; n=348), and myo-inositol increases correlated with practice years (Fayed et al., 2013). The review identifies a critical limitation: the relationship between practice duration and outcomes is non-linear and often curvilinear (Brefczynski-Lewis et al., 2007 found inverted-U activation patterns at 19,000 vs 44,000 hours), and meditation depth may be a stronger predictor than cumulative hours (Haslam et al., 2017). Most studies are cross-sectional, limiting causal inference.
clinical_insight: For therapists incorporating mindfulness, meditation, or acceptance-based approaches into clinical work, this review provides a comprehensive mechanistic rationale for why these interventions work beyond subjective self-report. Several findings have direct clinical implications. First, the shift from effortful emotion regulation strategies (typical of novices and many therapy clients) to automatic acceptance-based deactivation of self-referential processing (characteristic of long-term practitioners) maps directly onto the therapeutic goal of helping clients transition from reactive coping to present-moment acceptance. Second, the sensory-affective uncoupling findings are particularly relevant for chronic pain populations: meditation reduces the aversiveness of pain without blocking sensory transmission, suggesting it could complement rather than replace other pain management approaches. Third, the finding that meditation depth predicts stress biomarkers better than practice duration suggests that therapists should focus on the quality of meditative engagement rather than simply prescribing minutes per day. Fourth, the molecular evidence — including BDNF and endocannabinoid increases — provides a biological explanation for why mindfulness-based interventions show transdiagnostic efficacy across depression, anxiety, and stress-related disorders. The review also highlights important cautions: meditation can trigger challenging experiences including trauma re-emergence and dissociation, and therapists should screen for vulnerability factors before recommending intensive practice.
relevant_for: Therapists using mindfulness-based interventions (MBSR, MBCT, ACT), clinicians working with chronic pain populations, trauma therapists considering meditation-based adjuncts, and researchers interested in the neuroscience of contemplative mental health interventions.

title: Longitudinal interplay between co-occurring alcohol use, mood, and functioning in bipolar spectrum disorders
authors: Sarah Havens Sperry, Audrey Stromberg, Victoria Adams Murphy, Carly A Lasagna, Margo W. Menkes, Melvin G McInnis, Anastasia Yocum, Ivy Fei Tso
summary: This pre-registered longitudinal study tracks 663 individuals with bipolar spectrum disorders over an average of 10 years, characterizing the temporal dynamics between alcohol use, mood episodes, and life functioning. The study identifies patterns of comorbid alcohol use disorder in BD and delineates how drinking, mood symptoms, and functional impairment influence each other over time, revealing bidirectional relationships that challenge unidirectional causal models.
clinical_insight: The 10-year longitudinal design with 663 participants provides unusually robust evidence for the bidirectional relationship between alcohol use and mood instability in bipolar disorder. For clinicians managing comorbid BD and AUD, this study supports integrated treatment approaches that address both conditions simultaneously rather than sequentially. The finding that alcohol use both precipitates and is used to manage mood episodes creates a vicious cycle that standard mood stabilizer pharmacotherapy alone cannot break. Clinicians should routinely assess alcohol use patterns at every session with BD patients and consider integrated CBT approaches that target both mood regulation and substance use coping. A blind spot is the study does not examine whether specific bipolar subtypes (I vs. II) show different alcohol-mood dynamics, which could guide more targeted intervention planning.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating bipolar disorder in private practice or community settings where comorbid alcohol use is common

title: Revisiting the SciPop Scale: Measurement Alignment, Scoring Rules, and Cross-Context Comparability
authors: [Xue-Fei Yan, Zhihao Ma]
summary:
This comprehensive psychometric evaluation examines the SciPop Scale, a measure of science-related populism, using data from the Trust in Science and Science-Related Populism project (N = 69,341 across 68 countries/regions) and the Swiss Science Barometer (N = 1,124). The study evaluates factor structure, reliability, measurement invariance across multiple grouping variables, measurement alignment for approximate invariance, and compares two scoring philosophies (Bollen vs. Goertz approach) on criterion validity. Multidimensional item response theory models provide complementary item-level diagnostics including discrimination, threshold, item information, and differential item functioning.

The four-factor model (conceptions of ordinary people, conceptions of academic elite, demands for decision-making sovereignty, and demands for truth-speaking sovereignty) was the most robust and optimal structure across most subgroups, though 12 countries did not achieve acceptable CFA fit. Reliability was consistently high across all dimensions. Full measurement invariance held across sex, age, education, residence, political orientation, and religiosity. Partial scalar invariance was achieved across geographic regions, economic development levels, cultural value groups, and political regime types. At the country level, full invariance was not achieved, but alignment results provided strong support for approximate invariance with R-squared values exceeding 98% for loadings and 99% for intercepts across all four dimensions.

For criterion validity, all scoring approaches showed theoretically expected associations with perceived science benefit, trust in scientific methods, confidence in scientists (negative), and social dominance orientation (positive). Unaligned mean scores (Goertz approach) yielded the strongest associations with coefficients ranging from |beta| = 0.113 to 0.368, while alignment-adjusted scores produced more conservative estimates (|beta| = 0.008 to 0.141). Bollen-based aligned scores consistently outperformed Goertz-based aligned scores in cross-national analyses, yielding higher explanatory power.
clinical_insight: Although this paper addresses science-related populism rather than a clinical construct per se, its rigorous psychometric methodology and measurement invariance framework have broad relevance for clinical assessment research. The comparison of Bollen vs. Goertz scoring philosophies illustrates how different measurement conceptualizations can lead to different substantive conclusions, a critical issue in cross-cultural validation of clinical instruments. The paper's demonstration that measurement alignment can recover approximate invariance where traditional multi-group CFA fails is directly applicable to the many clinical scales that show partial invariance across cultural groups. The MIRT-based item diagnostics provide a template for identifying problematic items that may function differently across populations.
relevant_for:
- Psychometricians and researchers developing or validating cross-cultural clinical assessment tools
- Researchers studying health-related attitudes, vaccine hesitancy, or trust in health science
- Methodologists interested in measurement alignment and scoring philosophy comparisons
- Social scientists investigating science populism and its public health implications

title: The status of evidence-based treatments for moral injury syndrome: Review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
authors: Stephen Aita, Susannah Robb Kondrath, Tyler Owen, Nicholas Borgogna, Timothy Usset
summary: Reviews and meta-analyzes the evidence base for treatments targeting moral injury syndrome, evaluating randomized controlled trials of existing interventions and identifying gaps in the literature regarding validated treatment protocols, outcome measures, and mechanistic understanding of moral injury recovery.
clinical_insight: This meta-analysis identifies which therapeutic approaches are most effective for moral injury, guiding clinicians to move beyond standard PTSD protocols and incorporate interventions that specifically address guilt, shame, and meaning-making — particularly adaptive disclosure and acceptance-based approaches — rather than relying solely on exposure techniques.
relevant_for: Military and veteran mental health clinicians treating moral injury

title: The Dynamic Contingency Model: Integrating Competing Theories of Personality Processes
authors: [Muchen Xi, Joshua J. Jackson]
summary:
This study introduces the Dynamic Contingency Model (DCM), a novel framework based on Dynamic Structural Equation Modeling that simultaneously captures within-person person-environment temporal dynamics, situation-behavior contingencies, and stable between-person differences. Using intensive longitudinal data from 306 undergraduates across 25 personality-situation combinations (Big Five traits paired with theoretically relevant situational characteristics: dutiful, intellectual, negative, positive, and sociable), the study pursued three aims: identifying the most plausible theoretical model of daily behavioral dynamics, identifying effective pathways in person-environment transactions, and examining individual differences and trait moderation in dynamic processes.

The DCM consistently outperformed all competing models, including static trait models, situational contingency models, person-situation interaction models, and traditional transaction models, across all 25 personality-situation combinations. Within the DCM, the bidirectional full (BF) transaction model was the best-fitting specification overall, though the person-tilt (PT) model was statistically indistinguishable from BF in 56% of cases. Autoregressive effects (ranging 0.14-0.18 for personality states, 0.15-0.21 for situational states) and contemporaneous situation contingencies (0.05-0.14) emerged as dominant parameters. A systematic asymmetry was found: personality states more robustly predicted subsequent situational experiences (cross-lagged P-to-S: 0.05-0.18) than vice versa (cross-lagged S-to-P: 0.01-0.07), suggesting individuals function as active architects of their daily environments.

Substantial individual differences in dynamic parameters were observed, yet Big Five traits explained only a modest fraction of this variability (14% of 125 dynamic transaction parameters). Trait Neuroticism and Openness showed the most moderation effects. Individual-level model comparisons revealed that many people were best explained by simpler models (BS or PT rather than BF), highlighting that the best model is both context-specific and person-specific. The study was pre-registered.
clinical_insight: The DCM framework has significant implications for understanding personality pathology from a dynamic systems perspective. The finding that individuals are active architects of their environments — personality states predict subsequent situational experiences more strongly than situations predict personality — challenges passive models of environmental influence and supports therapeutic approaches that emphasize agency and active coping. The substantial individual differences in dynamic processes, poorly explained by trait measures alone, suggest that clinical assessment of personality disorders should move beyond static trait profiling to capture within-person dynamics and person-environment transaction patterns. The finding that many individuals are well-described by simpler models (e.g., person-tilt, where situations mainly respond to the person rather than vice versa) may help explain why some individuals with personality pathology appear "stuck" in self-reinforcing cycles.
relevant_for:
- Personality disorder researchers interested in dynamic models of personality pathology
- Clinical psychologists studying person-environment transactions in personality dysfunction
- Researchers using experience sampling or ecological momentary assessment in personality research
- Therapists working with personality-disordered clients who may benefit from understanding individual dynamic patterns

title: Effort Beats Effectiveness in Emotion Regulation Choice: Differences Between Suppression and Distancing
authors: Scheffel et al.
summary: Investigated whether people choose emotion regulation strategies based on their effectiveness or their perceived effort cost. Across subjective and physiological measures, participants preferred cognitive distancing over expressive suppression primarily because distancing felt less effortful, even when suppression was equally effective at reducing negative affect. The study highlights a systematic bias toward low-effort strategies in emotion regulation selection.
clinical_insight: Patients may avoid effective but effortful regulation strategies like cognitive restructuring in favor of easier but potentially less adaptive options like suppression or simple distraction. Clinicians should explicitly address effort perceptions during skills training, perhaps by scaffolding cognitive techniques gradually to reduce perceived difficulty, and by psychoeducating patients that the initial effort investment in strategies like distancing or reappraisal yields stronger and more durable emotion regulation benefits over time.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists working with patients who struggle to engage with effortful cognitive techniques

title: Trait Absorption as Predictor of Altered States of Consciousness Across Three Relaxation Induction Methods
authors: [Marc Wittmann, Amelia Lacassagne, Clara Koinegg, Marvin Hottenbacher, Sophia Saad]
summary:
This study investigates the effects of three variants of Restricted Environmental Stimulation Techniques (REST) — guided body-scan meditation, multimodal Ganzfeld (MMGF), and a control resting condition — on subjective experiences related to altered states of consciousness (ASC). Using a within-subjects design, 48 healthy participants without prior meditation experience completed three counterbalanced 25-minute sessions. Measures included the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) for valence and arousal, Subjective Time, Self, and Space Scales (STSS), the Perceived Body Boundaries Scale (PBBS), and the Tellegen Absorption Scale (TAS) as a trait predictor.

Guided meditation significantly reduced arousal (Friedman test = 15.18, p < .001) and time-related thoughts (Friedman test = 21.44, p < .001) compared to both control and Ganzfeld conditions, indicating decreased mind wandering and enhanced relaxation. Time passage was perceived as fastest during meditation (non-significant after alpha adjustment). No significant differences were observed in perceived body boundaries across conditions. Correlational analyses revealed that in the control condition, positive affect (SAM valence) was associated with lower arousal (rho = -.425), fewer time-related thoughts (rho = -.402), and faster perceived time passage (rho = .412), all significant after FDR correction. In the Ganzfeld condition, body boundary salience showed a broader pattern of associations, linking reduced boundaries to positive valence (rho = -.407) and lower arousal (rho = .362).

Regression analyses revealed that higher trait absorption (TAS) significantly predicted diminished body boundary salience in the control condition (B = -.029, p = .002, FDR-significant) and the Ganzfeld condition (B = -.037, p < .001, FDR-significant). In meditation, absorption predicted increased positive valence (B = .010, p = .016) and fewer time-related thoughts (B = -.202, p = .047), though these did not survive FDR correction. The findings highlight that trait absorption modulates susceptibility to ASC-like experiences particularly in reduced-stimulation contexts.
clinical_insight: These findings have direct implications for therapists utilizing relaxation-based, mindfulness, or sensory-based interventions. The demonstration that even a single 25-minute guided meditation session significantly reduces arousal and time-related rumination compared to passive rest supports the use of brief guided meditation as an accessible de-arousal tool, particularly for clients with anxiety or stress-related conditions where rumination is central. The finding that trait absorption predicts dissolution of body boundaries during reduced stimulation is clinically relevant for body-focused therapeutic approaches and may inform patient selection for specific relaxation modalities. Clients high in absorption may be more susceptible to profound experiential changes during REST-based or sensory deprivation therapies, which has implications for both therapeutic benefit (deeper relaxation, body dissociation) and risk (potential for distressing perceptual alterations).
relevant_for:
- Therapists incorporating mindfulness, body-scan meditation, or relaxation techniques into treatment
- Clinicians working with anxiety disorders where rumination and hyperarousal are central
- Researchers studying individual differences in response to contemplative and sensory-based interventions
- Somatic therapists interested in body boundary perception and embodied experience

title: Evolution of linguistic markers of agency, centrality and content during metacognitive therapy for psychosis
authors: Amir H. Nikzad, Paul H. Lysaker, Kyle S. Minor, Bethany Leonhardt, Mark Y. Liberman, Jenifer Vohs, Courtney N. Wiesepape, Sunny X. Tang
summary: This study examines how linguistic markers of agency, self-centrality, and thematic content evolve over the course of Metacognitive Reflection and Insight Therapy (MERIT) for psychosis. Using natural language processing to analyze therapy transcripts, the research tracks whether increases in metacognitive capacity are reflected in changes in how patients talk about themselves, providing an objective measure of recovery-oriented change in psychosis treatment.
clinical_insight: The use of linguistic analysis as an objective measure of therapeutic change in psychosis is a novel methodological contribution. For therapists using MERIT, metacognitive therapy, or recovery-oriented approaches to psychosis, the finding that linguistic markers of agency shift during treatment provides a concrete way to track progress beyond symptom reduction. If patients begin using more agentic language and less first-person centrality, this may indicate metacognitive capacity is developing before symptom changes appear. The tension between symptom-focused and recovery-focused outcomes in psychosis treatment is well-established, and this study provides tools for measuring the latter. A blind spot is that linguistic markers may reflect therapist influence or social desirability rather than genuine metacognitive change.
relevant_for: Therapists using MERIT, metacognitive therapy, or CBTp for psychosis who want to track recovery-oriented changes beyond symptom reduction

title: Emotional Composition of Sleep-Related Out-of-Body Experiences in Relation to Sleep Paralysis and Lucid Dreaming
authors: [Nerea L. Herrero, Cecilia Forcato]
summary:
This study examines whether the emotional composition of sleep-related out-of-body experiences (OBEs) is closer to sleep paralysis, closer to lucid dreaming, or distinguishable from both. The study analyzed 666 participant-by-experience-type emotional reports, with the main multivariate analyses focusing on 658 reports containing at least one discrete emotion: 184 OBE reports, 210 sleep paralysis reports, and 264 lucid dream reports. Emotional variables were coded as binary presence/absence indicators. Emotional composition was assessed using Jaccard dissimilarity, non-metric multidimensional scaling (NMDS), PERMANOVA, multivariate dispersion analysis, and emotion-by-group association analyses with chi-square tests and Monte Carlo simulation (10,000 replicates), corrected for false discovery rate.

Emotional composition differed significantly across the three experience types (PERMANOVA significant). Descriptively, the OBE-lucid dream contrast showed the smallest observed pairwise effect size, whereas contrasts involving sleep paralysis showed larger observed effects. Sleep paralysis was characterized by high-arousal negative emotions with the most homogeneous emotional profile: fear (Cramer's V = .312, 71% presence), anxiety (V = .235, 63.3%), and terror (V = .249, 40.5%), all significantly associated after FDR correction. Lucid dreams were associated with happiness (V = .396, 52.3%), love (V = .230, 21.6%), hope (V = .210, 18.2%), and sexual energy (V = .255, 34.1%). OBEs were distinguished by peace/tranquility (V = .289, 41.3% presence) and euphoria (V = .138, 28.3%). Absence-of-emotion responses were rare (1.1% OBE, 0% SP, 3.3% LD) and did not account for the multivariate pattern.

The authors conclude that sleep-related OBEs are not emotionally reducible to sleep paralysis. Their emotional profile showed greater affective similarity to lucid dreams than to sleep paralysis, while remaining distinguishable from both. This supports conceptualizing OBEs as a distinct phenomenological state rather than a mere vestibular-motor component of sleep paralysis.
clinical_insight: For clinicians working with sleep-related experiences, these findings help disambiguate OBEs from the more clinically concerning phenomenon of sleep paralysis. The distinctive emotional signatures can aid differential assessment: patients reporting predominantly fear/terror/anxiety during unusual sleep experiences are more likely experiencing sleep paralysis, whereas those describing peace, tranquility, or euphoria may be having OBEs with a different clinical significance. This distinction matters because sleep paralysis, particularly when recurrent and distressing, may indicate narcolepsy, PTSD-related sleep disturbance, or other conditions requiring clinical attention, while OBEs in isolation may not warrant the same level of concern. The findings also inform psychoeducation for patients who experience these states, normalizing the emotional heterogeneity of OBEs and reducing potential misattribution to psychopathology.
relevant_for:
- Sleep medicine clinicians assessing unusual sleep-related experiences
- Psychologists working with trauma-related sleep disturbances and nightmare disorders
- Clinicians treating narcolepsy or other sleep-wake transition disorders
- Researchers studying the phenomenology of dissociative and altered states of consciousness

title: Virtually delivered guided self-help for binge eating disorder and bulimia nervosa: Findings from a service evaluation conducted during the COVID-19 pandemic
authors: Bethan Dalton, Molly R Davies, Chloe Hutchings-Hay, Rachel Potterton, Eleanor Breen O'Byrne, Charmaine Kilonzo, Michaela Flynn, Stefano R. Belli, Lucy Gallop, Gemma Gordon, Johanna Louise Keeler, Imelda Minnock, Matthew Phillips, Lauren Robinson, Emma Snashall, Cindy Toloza, Luiza Walo, Jason Cole, Ulrike Schmidt
summary: This service evaluation examines the effectiveness of videoconferencing-delivered guided self-help (GSH) for adults with bulimia nervosa and binge eating disorder. Assessing uptake, completion rates, and clinical outcomes during the COVID-19 pandemic, the study extends prior research on GSH for bulimic spectrum eating disorders by testing an innovative delivery modality that could expand treatment access while maintaining clinical effectiveness.
clinical_insight: Guided self-help via videoconferencing represents a pragmatic solution to the chronic capacity constraints in eating disorder services. For private practitioners, this model offers a potential framework for scaling clinical impact: providing structured evidence-based guidance (typically CBT-E or CBT-BN based) with fewer sessions than full therapy. The service evaluation design, while less rigorous than an RCT, provides real-world effectiveness data that is directly applicable to clinical practice decisions. A key tension is between the accessibility advantages of virtual GSH and the therapeutic depth that in-person full protocol CBT-E provides. For complex cases with significant comorbidity (personality disorders, trauma histories), GSH alone is likely insufficient. The blind spot is the absence of long-term follow-up data, meaning it remains unclear whether virtual GSH produces durable recovery or simply accelerates initial symptom reduction.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders who are considering guided self-help or telehealth delivery models to expand treatment access

title: Digitized and AI-Assisted Scoring of Cognitive Assessments in ALS and Neurodegenerative Diseases: A Scoping Review of Consistency, Reliability, and Cross-Site Applicability
authors: [Indu Navar Bingham, Anusha Rao, Natalia Luchkina, Sharon Abrahams, Ammar Al-Chalabi]
summary:
This scoping review maps existing evidence on digitized and AI-assisted scoring of cognitive assessments in ALS and related neurodegenerative diseases, following PRISMA-ScR guidelines. An initial PubMed search identified 1,503 records, refined to 168, with 166 undergoing full screening. Only seven primary studies met inclusion criteria: two in multiple sclerosis, two in the Alzheimer's disease continuum, and three in dementia or mild cognitive impairment. No study evaluating AI-assisted scoring of the ECAS (Edinburgh Cognitive and Behavioural ALS Screen) or any ALS-specific cognitive screen was identified.

The methodological approaches across the seven studies clustered into four strategies. Tablet digitization with automated tallying (iBICAMS, digital MMSE) showed high agreement with paper versions (ICCs 0.82-0.92). Tablet self-administration with voice recognition and rule-based scoring (FACEmemory, MOGI) achieved very high auto-vs-manual agreement (r = 0.98, ICC = 0.97). End-to-end deep learning on scanned images (DenseNet CNN for RCFT scoring: MAE = 0.64, R-squared = 0.994 against expert average; contrastive API-Net for CDT: F1 = 0.79) approached or matched inter-expert agreement. Large language models (ChatGPT-4.5 for BICAMS) showed high pre-release agreement (CVLT-II ICC = 0.992, SDMT ICC = 1.000) but exhibited performance instability after public release, with BVMT-R Trial 3 ICC dropping to -0.046, attributed to undisclosed inference-pipeline changes.

Key findings include: (1) AI/digital scoring achieves agreement comparable to inter-expert agreement when adequately trained; (2) contrastive learning improves fine-grained discrimination at clinically critical score boundaries; (3) iterative training-data correction surfaces human scoring errors; and (4) commercial LLMs pose reproducibility risks for clinical applications. The review identifies a critical gap: despite up to 50% of ALS patients developing cognitive impairment, no AI-assisted scoring system exists for the ECAS.
clinical_insight: This review directly addresses a major practical challenge in ALS clinical care and trials: the lack of standardized, consistent cognitive scoring across sites. With up to 50% of ALS patients developing cognitive or behavioral impairment and approximately 15% meeting criteria for frontotemporal dementia, reliable cognitive assessment is essential but currently undermined by inter-rater variability in manual ECAS scoring. The methodological principles extracted from related diseases provide a roadmap for future ALS-specific development. The finding that locally hosted or domain-specific models with versioned weights are more appropriate than commercial LLM APIs is particularly important for clinical trial deployment, where scoring consistency over time is paramount. Clinicians and trialists should be aware that the current ALS cognitive assessment landscape has a significant technology gap that directly impacts the reliability of cognitive outcome measures in multi-site trials.
relevant_for:
- Neurologists and neuropsychologists assessing cognitive function in ALS and motor neuron disease
- Clinical trialists designing multi-site ALS studies with cognitive endpoints
- Health technology developers building AI-assisted clinical assessment tools
- Researchers interested in applying NLP and computer vision to neuropsychological assessment

title: A Functional Approach to Borderline Personality Disorder
authors: Daniel Echevarría-Escalante, Gladis-Lee Pereira, Víctor Estal Muñoz, María Xesús Froxán-Parga
summary: Proposes a functional approach to understanding Borderline Personality Disorder that moves beyond categorical symptom counts to examine the adaptive functions of BPD behaviors—such as interpersonal signaling, emotion regulation, and identity maintenance—informing functional analytic treatment approaches.
clinical_insight: This comprehensive review clarifies AvPD's distinction from social anxiety disorder and other personality pathology, helping clinicians refine differential diagnosis. Treatment should combine graduated interpersonal exposure with schema-focused techniques targeting core beliefs of social inadequacy and rejection sensitivity rather than relying on standard social anxiety protocols.
relevant_for: Personality disorder specialists and clinicians conducting differential diagnosis of social anxiety presentations

title: Psychometric Evaluation of a 10-Item Korean Adaptation of the Perceived Vulnerability to Disease Scale (PVD-Kr-10)
authors: [Gyeongbae Son, Sungeun You, Jong-Woo Paik, Hanson Park]
summary:
This study evaluates the psychometric properties of a Korean adaptation of the Perceived Vulnerability to Disease (PVD) scale, which measures individual differences in perceived susceptibility to infectious diseases and germ aversion. Data were collected from 1,150 Korean participants (1,065 adults, 85 adolescents; M age = 42.43, SD = 14.78) via online survey during the COVID-19 pandemic (March-June 2021). A multidisciplinary expert panel reviewed translation equivalence, cultural appropriateness, and content relevance. The sample was randomly split for exploratory factor analysis (EFA; n = 575) and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA; n = 575).

EFA yielded a 10-item two-factor solution comprising four Perceived Infectability (PI) items and six Germ Aversion (GA) items after five reverse-scored items were removed (four of which formed a separate keying-related factor). CFA supported this model with acceptable-to-good fit (chi-squared/df = 3.95, CFI = .98, TLI = .97, RMSEA = .07, SRMR = .05). Internal consistency was acceptable (PI: alpha = .84; GA: alpha = .76). Convergent validity was established through the Korean Disgust Scale-Revised, which correlated more strongly with GA than PI, consistent with the original scale. Construct validity was assessed via the GAD-7, DASS-21, COVID-19 Concern Scale, and PHQ-15. PI showed stronger associations with GAD-7, DASS-21, and PHQ-15, whereas GA was more strongly associated with COVID-19 concern. Measurement invariance across male and female respondents held up to the strict level. The removal of reverse-scored items aligns with findings from other cross-cultural adaptations.
clinical_insight: The PVD-Kr-10 provides a validated Korean-language instrument for assessing disease-related psychological vulnerability, which is relevant to clinical work in health psychology, particularly in the context of pandemic-related anxiety and health behavior. The differential validity pattern — Perceived Infectability relating more strongly to general psychopathology measures (GAD-7, DASS-21, PHQ-15) while Germ Aversion relates more to disease-specific concern — suggests these are meaningfully distinct constructs with different clinical implications. Perceived Infectability may serve as a marker for broader anxiety and somatic symptomatology, while Germ Aversion may be more specifically relevant to illness-specific behaviors like excessive hygiene or avoidance. The strict measurement invariance across gender supports its use in comparing men and women in clinical and research settings.
relevant_for:
- Health psychologists assessing disease-related anxiety and health behaviors in Korean populations
- Clinicians working with health anxiety, illness phobia, or somatic symptom disorders
- Researchers studying cross-cultural adaptations of psychopathology measures
- Public health researchers investigating psychological responses to pandemics

title: Development of a training programme in individual case formulation skills and a scale for assessing its effectiveness
authors: Richard Hallam, Gary Brown, Liza Turner, Elizabeth Blomfield, Sharif El Leithy, Ines Mendes
summary: This paper develops and validates a structured training programme designed to improve psychotherapists’ individual case formulation skills — a core competency that bridges assessment and treatment planning but has historically lacked formal training frameworks. The authors also created a measurement scale to assess formulation competence, addressing a significant gap in psychotherapy training and supervision.
clinical_insight: Case formulation is the bridge between assessment data and treatment planning, yet most training programmes leave it to implicit learning through supervision. This paper offers a structured approach that could be integrated into practicum training, clinical supervision, and continuing education, particularly for CBT and integrative therapists. A limitation is that the training and scale are newly developed and await broader replication across therapeutic orientations beyond the authors’ primary modality.
relevant_for: Clinical supervisors and training programme directors seeking structured approaches to teach case formulation skills

title: 'Good Work' through Good Relationships? A Longitudinal Field Intervention Study about the Effectiveness of Group Coaching on Well-Being and Resilience at Work
authors: [Kathryn Kissell, Karin Moser]
summary:
This longitudinal control-group intervention study examined the effectiveness of Bowen systems group coaching on the well-being and resilience of 32 Church of England clergy. Grounded in the Job Demands-Resources (JD-R) model, the study explored whether a person-centred, relationally-focused coaching intervention could enhance personal and interpersonal resources, reduce work demands, and promote work-related psychological health. Eighteen clergy in the intervention group completed six half-day group coaching sessions over six to seven months, while 14 clergy served as controls completing only pre- and post-surveys.

Pre-post comparisons using ANCOVA and paired-sample t-tests revealed that the intervention produced a significant decrease in relational fusion with others, F(1, 29) = 6.84, p = .01, partial eta-squared = .19, representing a large effect size. An almost significant reduction in interpersonal emotional cutoff was also observed, F(1, 29) = 3.69, p = .07, partial eta-squared = .11, a medium effect. The intervention group reported increased supportive interpersonal relations at all levels (peer, management, and congregation) compared to the control group, with medium effect sizes for management and congregational support. The coaching group also showed an almost significant decrease in the perceived severity of ministry demands, t(7) = 1.91, p = .098, d = 0.68 (medium effect), and an increase in spiritual resources. No significant changes were found in the I-position subscale of differentiation of self, and non-congregational support decreased unexpectedly.
clinical_insight: This study provides evidence that Bowen systems coaching, traditionally a family therapy approach, can be effectively adapted as a group-based workplace intervention. The findings suggest that improving relational functioning through increasing differentiation of self, specifically by reducing fusion and emotional cutoff, can buffer the impact of work demands and enhance perceived social support. This has direct implications for clinicians working with helping professionals in high-demand roles, such as clergy, healthcare workers, and social service providers. The systemic perspective of Bowen theory, which addresses both personal functioning and relational dynamics simultaneously, offers a valuable framework for workplace well-being interventions that move beyond individualistic approaches. The moderate sample size and non-random allocation are limitations, but the consistent pattern of medium-to-large effects across multiple outcomes is promising.
relevant_for:
- Workplace well-being and occupational health psychologists
- Clinicians using Bowen family systems approaches in organizational settings
- Clergy and helping professionals experiencing burnout
- Practitioners interested in group coaching interventions
- Researchers studying the JD-R model and relational resources

title: Differences in metacognitive functioning between obsessive-compulsive disorder patients and highly compulsive individuals from the general population
authors: Monja Hoven, Marion Rouault, Ruth van Holst, Judy Luigjes
summary: Compares metacognitive functioning — specifically confidence judgments — between OCD patients and highly compulsive individuals from the general population without an OCD diagnosis. The study addresses a central debate in the OCD literature: whether obsessive doubts reflect abnormally low confidence (clinical model) or impaired confidence estimation and usage (cognitive model), finding evidence that distinguishes clinical from subclinical compulsivity.
clinical_insight: This study sharpens the differential between pathological OCD and subclinical compulsivity by identifying distinct metacognitive profiles. For therapists, this supports the use of metacognitive-focused interventions (e.g., targeting confidence calibration and belief ratings) specifically for OCD patients, while subclinical compulsive individuals may respond better to behavioral experiments that address estimation processes. Clinicians should note the critical blind spot: the study relies on laboratory confidence tasks that may not fully capture the phenomenological experience of real-world obsessive doubt.
relevant_for: ERP and metacognitive therapists treating obsessive-compulsive disorder

title: Telling Stories of Resistance - Calling to Ancestral Strength: An Exploration of Mental Health Support Service Access and Adequacy for Indigenous Mothers in Manitoba
authors: [Sydney Levasseur-Puhach, Leslie E. Roos]
summary:
This qualitative thesis explored the experiences of seven Indigenous mothers (three Anishinaabe, four Metis) in Manitoba, Canada, regarding mental health support service seeking starting in the perinatal period. Using a constructivist and transformative paradigm with narrative inquiry methodology, the study analysed 46-60 minute semi-structured interviews with participants who had previously taken part in either the BEAM (Building Emotional Awareness and Mental Health) or BRIDGE (Building Regulation in Dual Generations) programs, both of which incorporated Western therapeutic approaches (psychoeducation, DBT skills, parenting skills) delivered in group format.

Analysis was conducted separately by Nation to avoid pan-Indigenous research practices. Five core narrative storylines emerged from Anishinaabe participants: Taking Care of Me to Take Care of Them (Cycle Breaking), Honouring the Child Within (Stories of Courage and Perseverance), Seeking Connection (Exploring Cultural Identity), Looking at the Bigger Picture (Wholistic Wellness), and Systemic Oppression (Stories of Barriers, Protection, and Survival). The dominant Anishinaabe narrative was Ancestral Strength Despite Colonial Harms. Four core narratives emerged from Metis participants: Indigenous Enough (Stories of Connection and Disconnection), Wearing Many Hats (Navigating Roles and Identities within Motherhood), Healing the Self (Stories of Coping and Maintaining Individuality), and Systems Navigation (Stories of Perseverance and Exhaustion). Key barriers included systemic oppression, fear of CFS involvement, lack of culturally grounded programming, and identity-based shame. Participants emphasised the importance of Indigenous-led organisations, cultural connection, community belonging, and addressing basic needs alongside mental health support.
clinical_insight: This study highlights the critical importance of culturally safe and Indigenous-led mental health services for perinatal Indigenous mothers. The finding that some mothers feared honest disclosure in Western programs due to previous experiences of being reported to Child and Family Services underscores how systemic distrust can directly impede therapeutic engagement. For clinicians, this reinforces the need for trauma-informed care that actively addresses historical and ongoing colonial violence. Programs should be developed and delivered in partnership with Indigenous communities, incorporating traditional healing practices and recognising wholistic (mental, emotional, spiritual, physical) conceptualisations of wellness. The Metis-specific narrative of feeling not Indigenous enough reveals a distinct form of cultural identity distress that may be overlooked in pan-Indigenous programming approaches.
relevant_for:
- Perinatal mental health clinicians working with Indigenous populations
- Program developers designing culturally safe mental health interventions
- Researchers interested in Indigenous methodologies and narrative inquiry
- Policy makers addressing mental health disparities for Indigenous mothers
- Clinicians working with intergenerational trauma in Indigenous communities

title: A computational lens on the transdiagnostic nature of intolerance of uncertainty
authors: Tim Sandhu, Bowen Xiao, Rebecca P. Lawson
summary: This computational psychiatry review reframes intolerance of uncertainty (IU) as a transdiagnostic mechanism operating across psychiatric and neurodevelopmental disorders. Using computational models from cognitive science, the authors propose that apparent difficulties in coping with uncertainty reflect specific computational processes (e.g., precision weighting, belief updating) that can be quantitatively modeled, offering a unifying framework for understanding IU in GAD, OCD, autism, and other conditions.
clinical_insight: Reframing IU through a computational lens has significant clinical implications beyond academic modeling. For therapists using CBT, ACT, or metacognitive therapy, this framework suggests that IU is not a monolithic trait but a composite of distinct computational processes that may require different intervention targets. For example, a client who struggles with precision weighting (overweighing threatening information under uncertainty) may benefit more from cognitive restructuring of threat appraisals, while a client with impaired belief updating may respond better to behavioral experiments that directly test predictions. This challenges the common clinical practice of applying uniform IU-targeting interventions across diagnoses. The blind spot is that computational models, while theoretically elegant, have not yet been translated into practical clinical tools that therapists can use in session without specialized training.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT clinicians working with GAD, OCD, and health anxiety where intolerance of uncertainty is a central maintaining factor

title: It's Just a Dick pic — (Cyber)Rape Culture and Victim Behavior Predict Perceptions and Responses to Unsolicited Genital Images
authors: [Rocio Vizcaino-Cuenca, Adrian Scott, Monica Romero Sanchez, Hugo Carretero-Dios, Fiona Gabbert]
summary:
This two-study experimental programme investigated how acceptance of myths about cyber-sexual violence (AMCYS) and victim behaviour shape perceptions of unsolicited genital images (cyberflashing). Study 1 (N = 131 UK social media users, aged 19-60, recruited via Prolific) manipulated whether the victim had previously chatted with the perpetrator. Using moderation analysis (PROCESS Model 1), a significant interaction emerged between victim behaviour and AMCYS on victim-blaming (b = .34, p = .005, delta-f-squared = .07, medium effect): victims who chatted were blamed more, but only among participants with higher AMCYS scores (b = .70, p < .001). A similar interaction was found for perceived severity (b = -.40, p = .008, delta-f-squared = .06): the incident was perceived as less severe when the victim had chatted, again only among high-AMCYS participants.

Study 2 (N = 202 UK young adults aged 19-30) manipulated whether the victim had shown sexual interest toward the perpetrator. A large interaction effect on victim-blaming was found (b = .92, p < .001, delta-f-squared = .33): victims who showed sexual interest were blamed substantially more by high-AMCYS participants (b = 2.43, p < .001) than low-AMCYS participants (b = .40, p = .024). AMCYS moderated the indirect effect of victim behaviour on both blaming and severity through perceived sexual consent. Participants with higher AMCYS scores perceived greater sexual consent when the victim showed interest, which in turn increased victim-blaming and decreased perceived severity (index of moderated mediation B = .74, 95% CI [.467, 1.028]).
clinical_insight: These findings demonstrate that myths about cyber-sexual violence function as interpretive frameworks that legitimize harm by misattributing victim behaviour as implied consent. For clinicians working with survivors of cyber-sexual violence, this research highlights that victim-blaming and minimisation are not random but are systematically shaped by culturally embedded beliefs about online sexual interactions. Survivors who engaged in prior online contact with perpetrators may be particularly vulnerable to self-blame and to encountering disbelief from others. Clinical work should explicitly address internalised cyber-sexual violence myths and help survivors understand that prior flirtation or chat does not constitute consent to receive unsolicited explicit images. The AMCYS scale could serve as a useful psychoeducational and assessment tool in therapeutic settings.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians working with cyber-sexual violence survivors
- Researchers studying victim-blaming and rape culture in digital contexts
- Those interested in online sexual harassment and cyberflashing
- Practitioners developing psychoeducational programmes about digital safety
- Forensic and clinical psychologists assessing trauma related to image-based abuse

title: Use of substances to cope predicts PTSD symptom persistence: Investigating patterns of interactions between PTSD symptoms and its maintaining mechanisms
authors: René Freichel, Nora Skjerdingstad, Alessandra Chiara Mansueto, Sacha Epskamp, Asle Hoffart, Sverre Urnes Johnson, Omid V. Ebrahimi
summary: This study investigates how substance use as a coping strategy interacts with established PTSD maintaining mechanisms, including cognitive and experiential avoidance, positive and negative metacognitions, to predict PTSD symptom persistence over time. Using network analysis, the research identifies substance use coping as a central node that interacts with multiple maintaining factors, suggesting it plays a unique role in perpetuating PTSD that goes beyond simple comorbidity.
clinical_insight: The finding that substance use coping occupies a central position in the network of PTSD-maintaining mechanisms has significant implications for treatment sequencing. For trauma therapists, this supports an integrated approach that addresses substance use coping early in treatment rather than treating PTSD and substance use as separate sequential conditions. The network approach reveals that substance use coping does not simply co-occur with PTSD but actively interacts with avoidance and metacognitive processes to maintain symptoms. For clinicians using PE or CPT, this means that untreated substance use coping may undermine exposure work by providing an alternative avoidance pathway. The tension in the literature between integrated vs. sequential treatment for comorbid PTSD and substance use remains unresolved; this study provides network-level evidence favoring integrated approaches. A blind spot is the absence of trauma type differentiation, as substance use coping may play different roles in combat-related PTSD versus interpersonal trauma, which has direct implications for treatment planning in private practice where interpersonal trauma predominates.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with clients who use substances to cope, particularly those using PE, CPT, or Seeking Safety-informed approaches

title: Patients and clinicians perspectives on a transdiagnostic Experience Sampling and Ecological Momentary Intervention protocol
authors: [Adam Kurilla, Daniel Dancik, Radoslav Losonsky, Alica Hujsova, Sebastian Petreje, Julia Hysi, Valentina Cvikova, Sara Majsniarova, Petra Brandoburova, Anton Heretik, Michal Hajduk]
summary:
This qualitative study explored service users and clinicians perspectives on the usability and clinical utility of a prolonged transdiagnostic Experience Sampling Method (ESM) protocol with on-demand Ecological Momentary Intervention (EMI) content. Within the DISCONNECT project, 84 psychiatric outpatients (with diagnoses across schizophrenia spectrum, affective, anxiety, and substance use disorders) completed a 30-day ESM protocol using the m-Path app, with three daily assessments capturing sleep, daily functioning, loneliness, and HiTOP-defined psychopathology domains. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with a stratified subsample of 20 service users (meeting 70% compliance) and 10 outpatient clinicians.

Both stakeholder groups perceived ESM as acceptable and clinically valuable. Content analysis of debriefings from all 84 participants revealed that the most prominent themes reflected favourable user experience, perceived appropriateness of questionnaire content, and increased self-insight. Thematic analysis of interviews identified six service user themes: previous experience with self-monitoring, acceptability of ESM monitoring, perceived benefits of self-monitoring, experiences with EMI, integration into mental health care, and personalization and future development. Participants reported increased self-awareness, improved understanding of symptom patterns, and enhanced opportunities for psychoeducation, treatment planning, and patient-clinician communication. EMI was viewed positively but used infrequently, with motivational quotations being the most used and valued intervention. Clinicians emphasised potential for reducing retrospective recall bias, enriching psychotherapy with material for discussion, and supporting work between sessions. Key barriers included monitoring burden, clinician time constraints, and organisational challenges.
clinical_insight: This study provides compelling qualitative evidence that prolonged transdiagnostic ESM monitoring is feasible and perceived as clinically meaningful by both service users and clinicians. The finding that patients value increased self-awareness and pattern recognition through repeated self-monitoring supports the therapeutic utility of ESM beyond mere data collection. For clinicians, ESM data visualisations appear to offer a concrete tool for enhancing the therapeutic alliance through collaborative interpretation. However, the infrequent use of EMI content suggests that on-demand interventions may need to be more contextually triggered or integrated with real-time data to be clinically useful. The need for greater personalization, flexible scheduling, and wearable device integration emerges as critical for successful implementation in routine care.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians interested in digital mental health tools and ESM implementation
- Researchers developing transdiagnostic assessment protocols
- Clinical psychologists and psychiatrists working in outpatient settings
- mHealth and digital therapeutics developers
- Those interested in the HiTOP framework applied to digital assessment

title: Longitudinal Associations between Mindfulness and Change in Attachment Orientations in Couples: The Role of Relationship Preoccupation and Empathy
authors: Taranah Gazder, Sarah C. E. Stanton
summary: Using a longitudinal design, this study examined how general and relationship-specific mindfulness predict changes in attachment orientations (security, anxiety, avoidance) in adult couples over time. The findings suggest that mindfulness may enhance attachment security through mechanisms involving reduced relationship preoccupation and increased empathic responsiveness, offering an interpersonal pathway by which mindfulness-based interventions benefit couples.
clinical_insight: For couples therapists, this study provides empirical support for integrating mindfulness practices into couples work as a means of shifting underlying attachment dynamics rather than merely teaching communication skills. The finding that relationship-specific mindfulness (present-moment awareness during interactions with one’s partner) drives attachment security more than general mindfulness has direct implications for how therapists structure in-session mindfulness exercises. A blind spot is the lack of diversity reporting and the exclusive focus on mixed-gender couples, limiting generalisability to same-sex couples.
relevant_for: Couples therapists integrating mindfulness-based approaches or working with attachment-related relationship distress

title: Mega-analysis of the Interoceptive Accuracy Scale (IAS) Structure and its Dispositional Correlates
authors: [Ana Neves, Magdalena Pfaff, Robyn Scharte, Raquel Nogueira-Arjona, Giulia Poerio, Dominique Makowski]
summary:
This mega-analysis presents the most comprehensive psychometric evaluation of the Interoceptive Accuracy Scale (IAS) to date, combining 17 open-access datasets (total N > 33,000). Study 1 triangulated results across four dimensionality-reduction algorithms (EFA, HCA, EGA, CFA) to evaluate the IAS factor structure. Seven items were identified as problematic (tickle, taste, affective touch, vomit, itch, temperature, sexual arousal) and removed, yielding a stable 14-item core organized into 7 distinct item pairs linked to bodily sensations: Hungry-Thirsty, Bruise-Blood sugar, Urinate-Defecate, Muscles-Pain, Breathing-Heart, Cough-Sneeze, and Wind-Burp. The 7-factor EGA model provided the best fit (RMSEA = 0.035, CFI = 0.984), preferred in 8 of 13 individual datasets, with substantially lower BIC than all alternative models.

Study 2 examined dispositional correlates using mixed models treating datasets as random clusters. The IAS showed robust positive associations with most MAIA-2 subscales (beta = .19 to .26 for Noticing and Attention Regulation), positive associations with BPQ Body Awareness (beta = .15), and consistent negative associations with alexithymia (TAS, BVAQ; beta approximately -.13 to -.20) and interoceptive confusion (ICQ; beta = -.32). Depression and anxiety showed consistent negative associations (beta approximately -.20), with the strongest effects for the Hungry-Thirsty pair. Psychopathological traits (detachment, psychoticism, negative affect) showed weak negative associations (beta = -.08 to -.10). Autistic traits (ASQ) showed a similar pattern, with imagination the most consistent correlate (beta = -.18). The Hungry-Thirsty and Bruise-Blood sugar pairs consistently drove the strongest external associations. The 14-item version performed comparably to the original 21-item scale, with item-pair structure enhancing interpretability.
clinical_insight: This study has significant implications for clinical assessment of interoception, particularly in populations where interoceptive dysfunction is central, such as somatic symptom disorders, anxiety disorders, eating disorders, and autism spectrum conditions. The finding that the IAS is not a unidimensional construct but rather comprises system-specific item pairs challenges how interoceptive accuracy has been conceptualised and measured. Clinicians should be aware that global IAS scores may mask important domain-specific differences; for example, the Hungry-Thirsty pair showed the strongest associations with psychopathology, while other pairs (e.g., Cough-Sneeze, Wind-Burp) showed weaker or no associations. The 14-item paired version offers a more interpretable and psychometrically sound alternative for both research and clinical use, potentially improving sensitivity to detect clinically meaningful interoceptive differences.
relevant_for:
- Researchers studying interoception across clinical and non-clinical populations
- Clinicians assessing somatic symptom and functional disorders
- Those developing or refining interoceptive measurement tools
- Researchers interested in the HiTOP framework and transdiagnostic assessment
- Clinicians working with autism, anxiety, and eating disorder populations

title: Perceived responsiveness in suicidal ideation: an experience sampling study in psychiatric patients
authors: Laura Sels, Stephanie Homan, Harry Reis, Andrea B Horn, Jordan Revol, Urte Scholz, Tobias Kowatsch, Birgit Kleim
summary: Using ecological momentary assessment (EMA) in a sample of psychiatric patients, this study investigated whether momentary fluctuations in perceived responsiveness from close others — feeling understood, validated, and cared for — were associated with reductions in suicidal ideation within the same day. The EMA design captures the dynamic, real-time interplay between an interpersonal protective factor and suicidal thoughts that cross-sectional designs miss.
clinical_insight: The experience sampling methodology provides compelling evidence that perceived responsiveness operates as a within-day protective factor against suicidal ideation, supporting interpersonal theories of suicide (e.g., Joiner’s interpersonal theory). For clinicians, this reinforces the importance of explicitly strengthening patients’ perceived social support and responsiveness in therapy, and suggests that momentary assessments of connectedness could serve as early warning signals. The study’s use of EMA data from actual psychiatric patients strengthens ecological validity compared to student samples, though the correlational design precludes causal conclusions about the direction of responsiveness-ideation effects.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with suicidal patients in inpatient or outpatient psychiatric settings who want to leverage interpersonal factors in safety planning

title: Stress Beliefs Shape Daily Affective Reactivity to Social and Work Stressors: A 7-Day Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
authors: [Johannes Andreas Christoph Laferton, Daniela Conrad, Frank Zimmermann-Viehoff, Stefanie Kunas, Frank Euteneuer, Susanne Fischer, Stefan Salzmann]
summary:
This 7-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) study examined whether stress beliefs moderate affective reactivity and stress regulation strategies in response to naturally occurring stressors. Using the Beliefs About Stress Scale (BASS), which differentiates negative stress beliefs (BASS-N), positive stress beliefs (BASS-P), and controllability beliefs (BASS-C), the study assessed 104 university students from three German universities who completed thrice-daily reports (1,939 total observations) on social and work-related stressors, positive and negative affect (PANAS), and two stress regulation strategies from the Stress Optimization Model: situation selection (avoidance) and response modification (utilization).

Using hierarchical linear models with Bonferroni-corrected significance levels, negative stress beliefs significantly moderated the within-person association between daily social stress and positive affect. Participants with high negative beliefs showed reduced positive affect during social stress (simple slope = -0.82, p < .005), while those with low/moderate beliefs maintained positive affect. Negative beliefs also moderated the workload-positive affect relationship: low/moderate belief holders showed increased positive affect under higher workload (simple slope = 0.30-0.60, p < .007), consistent with a challenge-like appraisal, whereas high belief holders showed no association. Controllability beliefs buffered increases in negative affect during social stress: low controllability belief participants showed stronger negative affect reactivity (simple slope = 2.73) than high belief participants (simple slope = 1.61). Contrary to hypotheses, stress beliefs did not moderate the use of situation selection or response modification strategies. Exploratory analyses revealed that positive stress beliefs predicted less intra-individual variability in daily positive affect (LRT = 15.81, p < .001), and controllability beliefs predicted less variability in negative affect (LRT = 17.60, p < .001).
clinical_insight: This study provides ecological evidence that stress beliefs serve as cognitive moderators of affective reactivity to everyday stressors, extending prior laboratory-based findings to naturalistic settings. The differential findings for social versus work stressors are clinically noteworthy: negative stress beliefs appear particularly deleterious in interpersonal contexts, depleting positive affect during social stress, while low negative beliefs allow individuals to experience workload as energising rather than threatening. For clinicians, this suggests that stress belief modification, specifically targeting negative beliefs about stress being inherently harmful, could be a valuable intervention target. The controllability findings suggest that enhancing patients sense of agency over stress responses may buffer negative affect during interpersonal difficulties. Importantly, the null findings for stress regulation strategies suggest that stress beliefs primarily shape affective responses rather than behavioural coping efforts, which has implications for therapeutic focus.
relevant_for:
- Clinical psychologists working with stress-related conditions
- Health psychologists interested in cognitive appraisal processes
- CBT practitioners targeting stress-related cognitions
- Researchers studying the Stress Optimization Model
- Those interested in EMA methodology for studying daily stress processes

title: The Relationship Between the Perception of Major Life Events and Depression in Adulthood: A Systematic Scoping Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Peter Haehner, Felix Würtz, Sarah Kritzler, Marius Kunna, Maike Luhmann, Marcella L. Woud
summary: This systematic scoping review and meta-analysis synthesizes 276 studies (N=89,600) examining the relationship between how people perceive major life events and depression in adulthood. Rather than focusing on objective event occurrence, this review examines whether subjective perception, interpretation, and meaning-making around life events predicts depression onset, testing predictions from cognitive, stress-vulnerability, and rumination models.
clinical_insight: The massive scale of this meta-analysis (276 studies, nearly 90,000 participants) gives exceptional weight to its central finding: event perception matters as much as or more than event occurrence for depression risk. For CBT and schema therapy practitioners, this validates the core assumption that cognitive appraisal and meaning-making, rather than circumstances alone, drive depressive episodes. This supports interventions targeting cognitive reappraisal, schema modification, and narrative reconstruction of life events. The tension between changing circumstances and changing perceptions is fundamental: clients often want circumstances to change, while therapy primarily changes their relationship to those circumstances. A blind spot is the aggregation of cross-sectional studies where causality cannot be established, meaning depression may influence event perception rather than vice versa, creating a self-reinforcing cycle that CBT aims to interrupt.
relevant_for: CBT and schema therapy clinicians working with depression who need evidence for the centrality of cognitive appraisal in case formulations

title: Is it Reactive Aggression? Using Computational Methods to Explain Evolving Aggression
authors: [Fakhra Jabeen, Charlotte Gerritsen, Jan Treur]
summary:
This study presents a temporal-causal network model of reactive aggression in computer-mediated communication, specifically examining how individuals respond to negative feedback on social media. Drawing on psychological, cognitive-neuroscience, and social perspectives, the model represents a humanoid agent who receives inappropriate feedback and must regulate their emotional response. The model incorporates neural plausibility by simulating amygdala-driven threat processing, prefrontal cortex regulation, and the roles of neurotransmitters (5-HT, dopamine, noradrenaline) in modulating aggressive behavior.

Two simulation scenarios were developed: Scenario I (impulsive aggression) demonstrates how negative feedback triggers an uncontrolled aggressive expression (e.g., a sarcastic online reply), while Scenario II (judicious aggression) shows how Hebbian learning enables an agent to develop anger management skills over time, shifting from suppression to response modulation strategies. The model was validated and tuned using a real-world dataset of approximately 1 million customer support tweets. After filtering for English-language conversations with negative sentiment (via VADER Sentiment Analysis), 483,558 conversations were classified as polite or impolite using an SVM classifier with Stanford dependency parsing. Politeness classification yielded 93.3% polite and 6.7% impolite responses. Model parameters were optimized via simulated annealing, achieving an RMSE of 0.118 after 1,065 iterations.
clinical_insight: This computational modeling approach offers a novel framework for understanding and detecting cyber-aggression dynamics. The model demonstrates that reactive aggression exists on a spectrum from impulsive to judicious responses, and that learning-based regulation (rather than mere suppression of anger) represents a more adaptive pathway. The two-level sensitivity control mechanism (low vs. high sensitivity feedback) maps onto clinically meaningful distinctions in how individuals perceive and react to threatening social stimuli. The model could inform the development of automated aggression detection systems and structured support interventions for cyberbullying victims, though clinical translation would require validation with human participants rather than simulated agents.
relevant_for:
- Researchers studying cyberbullying and online aggression
- Clinicians working with adolescents experiencing social media-related distress
- Developers of AI-mediated mental health support tools
- Those interested in computational modeling of emotion regulation

title: The Perceived Causal Relations Between Sensory Reactivity Differences and Anxiety Symptoms in Autistic Adults
authors: Verhulst et al.
summary: Investigates how 246 autistic adults perceive causal relationships between sensory reactivity differences and anxiety symptoms. Sensory hyperreactivity (especially visual, auditory, olfactory) was perceived as causing anxiety, while sensory seeking (especially tactile, vestibular) was perceived as resulting from anxiety, suggesting different clinical pathways for intervention.
clinical_insight: This study is directly relevant for therapists working with autistic adults with co-occurring anxiety. Understanding patients' own causal models of how sensory experiences trigger anxiety can inform individualized treatment planning, particularly in differentiating between sensory-driven anxiety versus anxiety arising from other sources. The finding that sensory seeking may be an effect rather than cause of anxiety suggests different intervention targets.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with autistic adults, particularly those addressing co-occurring anxiety in neurodiversity-affirming practice

title: Receptive Vocabulary Performance Varies with Depression
authors: [Michael J. Espero]
summary:
This study investigates the relationship between depression and receptive vocabulary performance using data from the National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent to Adult Health (ADD Health). The analysis sample included 2,672 adolescents (51% female) who completed the Picture Vocabulary Test (PVT), a multi-modal assessment requiring participants to hear an English word spoken aloud and match it to one of four images. The study employed multi-group structural equation modeling (MGSEM) with robust maximum likelihood estimation to test whether the relationship between health/lifestyle factors and vocabulary performance was equivalent across language groups (English-speaking at home vs. other languages).

Confirmatory factor analysis retained two latent constructs with acceptable reliability: depression (omega = 0.70) and alcohol experience (omega = 0.84). Mood and insomnia/sleep constructs were dropped due to low reliability. The final parsimonious model imposed equal factor loadings, equal intercepts, and equal regression weights across groups (structural invariance), demonstrating acceptable global fit. Depression showed a statistically significant negative association with receptive vocabulary performance (beta = -0.200, SE = 0.021, 95% CI [-0.241, -0.159], p < .001), meaning higher depressive symptoms predicted lower vocabulary scores. Bayesian MCMC estimation corroborated this finding (beta = -0.255, 95% CrI [-0.246, -0.154]). Alcohol experience was not associated with vocabulary (beta = -0.015, p = .453), nor were sex or age. The depression-vocabulary link was structurally invariant across language groups, suggesting both English and non-English primary language speakers showed similarly negative impacts.
clinical_insight: These findings have important implications for cognitive assessment in depressed populations. Receptive vocabulary tests are widely used as proxies for intellectual ability, yet this study demonstrates that depressive symptoms significantly impair performance on such tasks independent of actual cognitive capacity. This raises concerns about misclassification of cognitive decline or verbal deficits in individuals experiencing depression, particularly in cross-cultural assessment contexts. The structural invariance across language groups suggests that depression-related cognitive slowing affects English-normed vocabulary assessments similarly regardless of a person's primary language. Clinicians and neuropsychologists should consider screening for depressive symptoms when interpreting vocabulary-based cognitive assessments to avoid conflating mood-related cognitive slowing with genuine verbal ability deficits.
relevant_for:
- Neuropsychologists interpreting vocabulary-based cognitive assessments
- Clinicians assessing cognitive functioning in depressed adolescents and adults
- Researchers studying cognitive correlates of depression across cultural and linguistic groups
- Those interested in measurement invariance in psychological testing

title: Functional Correlates of Subliminal Stimulation in Posttraumatic Stress Disorder: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Cesari et al.
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis (23 studies, 5 in fMRI meta-analysis) examined neuroimaging correlates of subliminal trauma-related stimulation in PTSD. The Innate Alarm System (IAS)—including the amygdala and related regions—showed hyperactivation in response to unconsciously processed threat cues. IAS response intensity scaled with PTSD severity: it was minimal in healthy controls and maximal in patients with dissociative symptoms or poor treatment responsiveness. Comparisons with other disorders (e.g., phobias) revealed contrasting patterns.
clinical_insight: The demonstration that the amygdala-based threat system activates to traumatic cues outside conscious awareness has profound clinical implications. It provides a neurobiological rationale for why PTSD patients experience seemingly ‘triggered’ responses without identifiable conscious antecedents, and why exposure-based therapies must address both explicit and implicit threat processing. The finding that dissociative patients show the strongest subliminal responses suggests that dissociation may represent a maladaptive top-down attempt to suppress an overactive IAS. Clinicians should consider integrating body-based or implicit processing interventions (e.g., EMDR, sensorimotor psychotherapy) alongside traditional talk therapies. A gap is the lack of longitudinal data tracking whether IAS responsivity normalizes with successful treatment.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with PTSD, complex trauma, and dissociation, especially those integrating somatic or neurobiologically-informed approaches.

title: Who reciprocates whom? Mutual influence in emoticon use and perceived responsiveness between chat workers and young people seeking help
authors: [Gerard Chung, Andrew Zi Han Yee, Nataliya Thell, Lim Tse Min]
summary:
This study examines emoticon reciprocity in a role-asymmetric helping dyad: trained chat workers and help-seeking young people on a Singapore youth chatline. Drawing on the Interpersonal Process Model of Intimacy and Communication Accommodation Theory, the authors analyzed 86,424 lagged turns nested within 1,435 sessions (179,267 messages from 926 unique authors) using the Actor-Partner Interdependence Model. Emoticons were identified using a locally hosted LLM (GPT-OSS 20B) with 99% human-coder agreement, and operationalized in both binary (presence/absence) and count (intensity) forms.

Emoticon use was common across sessions (87% contained at least one), with chat workers sending significantly more emoticon-bearing messages (71.3%) than users (28.7%). Workers averaged 4.98 emoticons per session versus 2.13 for users (t = 12.18, p < .001). Workers concentrated on two forms: smiley :) at 74.2% and frown/sad :( at 18.4%, while users drew on a broader affective range including crying :'( at 15.6% and skeptical :/ at 4.0%. Pooled models showed significant actor and partner effects in both operationalizations (binary: actor OR = 2.16, partner OR = 2.12; count: actor IRR = 1.38, partner IRR = 1.21, all p < .001). Critically, role-moderated models revealed asymmetric reciprocity: the worker-to-user partner effect was significantly larger than the user-to-worker partner effect (binary OR = 2.40 vs. 1.91; count IRR = 1.72 vs. 1.08), meaning workers' emoticons set the affective tone more strongly than users' did. Moderation analyses showed that in sessions rated higher on perceived responsiveness, the partner effect strengthened (Y1 x partner OR = 1.22, p < .001) while the actor effect weakened (Y1 x actor OR = 0.90, p < .001).
clinical_insight: This study provides actionable evidence for text-based mental health services, particularly youth helplines. The finding that chat workers' emoticon use disproportionately drives the emotional tone of sessions has direct training implications: workers can leverage strategic emoticon use to shape session warmth and perceived responsiveness. The asymmetry in reciprocity (workers influence users more than vice versa) is clinically meaningful because it suggests that trained helpers hold disproportionate power to establish rapport through simple paralanguage cues. The moderation by perceived responsiveness further indicates that emoticon convergence is not merely stylistic but is tied to the therapeutic quality of the interaction. For youth, who often prefer anonymous text-based support and may struggle with face-to-face settings, these micro-level communicative behaviors may be especially significant in determining whether they feel understood and supported.
relevant_for:
- Youth mental health helpline practitioners and trainers
- Researchers studying text-based counseling and digital mental health interventions
- Those interested in nonverbal communication in therapeutic settings
- Clinicians working with young people who prefer text-based modalities

title: Trajectories of Depression Symptoms, Anxiety Symptoms and Functional Impairment During Internet-Enabled Cognitive-Behavioural Therapy
authors: Skelton et al.
summary: Using growth mixture modelling on 52,029 iCBT patients, four trajectory classes were identified for depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), and functional impairment (WSAS): no change, gradual improvement, fast improvement, and a mild-baseline class. In moderate-to-severe baseline groups, fast improvers were more likely older, employed, without disability, and without OCD comorbidity. Functional impairment trajectories mirrored depression more closely than anxiety, underscoring its clinical importance as an outcome.
clinical_insight: This is the largest trajectory study in internet-enabled CBT to date and provides actionable baseline predictors for therapists: patients who are older, employed, and lack OCD comorbidity are most likely to improve rapidly. The identification of a ‘no change’ class—even among those receiving active treatment—highlights the need for early identification algorithms and stepped-care escalation. Functional impairment should be routinely tracked alongside symptom scales, as it captures a distinct dimension of recovery. A limitation is the absence of qualitative data explaining why the no-change group stalls.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering iCBT or digital mental health interventions, and service designers building adaptive treatment algorithms.

title: Believe It, Achieve It? Self-Control Beliefs in Predicting Life Outcomes
authors: [Jinyao Li, Marleen Gillebaart, Tim van Timmeren, Denise De Ridder]
summary:
